NOTICE
SECTION I: USE OF THE CLINICAL PRACTICE GUIDELINE
This Clinical Practice Guideline document is based upon literature searches last conducted in
June 2025. It is designed to assist decision-making. It is not intended to define a standard of
care and should not be interpreted as prescribing an exclusive course of management.
Variations in practice will inevitably and appropriately occur when clinicians consider the
needs of individual patients, available resources, and limitations unique to an institution or type
of practice. Healthcare professionals using these recommendations should decide how to apply
them to their own clinical practice.

AF
T

SECTION II: DISCLOSURE

D

R

Kidney Disease: Improving Global Outcomes (KDIGO) makes every effort to avoid any actual
or reasonably perceived conflicts of interest that may arise from an outside relationship or a
personal, professional, or business interest of a member of the Work Group. All members of
the Work Group are required to complete, sign, and submit a disclosure and attestation form
showing all such relationships that might be perceived as or are actual conflicts of interest.
This document is updated annually, and information is adjusted accordingly. All reported
information is published in its entirety at the end of this document in the Work Group
members’ Disclosure section and is kept on file at KDIGO.

xii

Calgary, Alberta, Canada

The Work Group consisted of a geographically diverse group of experts including the following specialties:
adult and pediatric nephrology, critical care medicine, emergency medicine, general medicine, pharmacy and
pharmacology, as well as people with lived experience of AKI.

xiii

ABSTRACT

AF
T

The Kidney Disease: Improving Global Outcomes (KDIGO) 2026 Clinical Practice
Guideline for Acute Kidney Injury (AKI) and Acute Kidney Disease (AKD) is an update of the
KDIGO 2012 guideline on the topic. The guideline informs the care of adults and children at
risk for or with AKI and AKD. The guideline includes chapters dedicated to AKI and AKD
definition, identification, and classification; AKI prediction, risk stratification, and diagnostic
evaluation; prevention and treatment of AKI, drug- and nephrotoxin-associated AKI; renal
replacement therapy; and follow-up care after AKI or AKD. The update considers evidence
from studies published through June 2025. The guideline provides actionable recommendations
based on a rigorous formal evidence review, practice points that were not based on a
systematic review and supporting infographics. The target audiences for the guideline are
healthcare professionals involved in the care of people with AKI or AKD as well as people
with or after resolution of AKI themselves. Development of the guideline followed an explicit
process of evidence review and appraisal. The guideline recommendations are based on
systematic reviews of relevant studies, and appraisal of the certainty of evidence and of the
strength of recommendations following the “Grading of Recommendations Assessment,
Development and Evaluation” (GRADE) approach. Limitations of the evidence are discussed
and suggested areas for future research are also presented.

D

R

Keywords: acute kidney disease, acute kidney injury, evidence-based; guideline; KDIGO;
systematic review; GRADE

xiv

SUMMARY OF RECOMMENDATION STATEMENTS AND
PRACTICE POINTS
CHAPTER 1. AKI AND AKD DEFINITION, IDENTIFICATION,
AND CLASSIFICATION
1.1. Definition of AKI
Practice Point 1.1.1: Acute kidney injury (AKI) in children and adults is defined by the
presence of one or more abnormalities of kidney function or structure known or
presumed to have occurred within 7 days, in the appropriate clinical context (Table 1).

AF
T

Table 1 | Criteria for acute kidney injury for children and adults (any one or more of the
following within the corresponding time period)
Functional criteria
Structural criteria
• Increase in SCr by ≥0.3 mg/dl
• Elevation of a biomarker of kidney
(≥26.5 µmol/l) within 48 hours
damage when used for its clinically
validated and regulatory qualified
• Increase in SCr by ≥1.5 times
indication, which is known or
baseline that is known or presumed
presumed to have occurred within
to have occurred within the prior 7
the prior 7 days
days

R

• Increase in serum cystatin C by
≥1.5 times baseline that is known or
presumed to have occurred within
the prior 7 days

D

• Mean urine volume of less than 0.5
ml/kg/h (based on ideal body
weight) for ≥6 hours
SCr, serum creatinine

Recommendation 1.1.1: We suggest using serum cystatin C to identify AKI in children
and adults in clinical situations when serum creatinine (SCr) is less accurate (2B).
Practice Point 1.1.2: A baseline SCr value should be ascertained for all patients with
suspected or confirmed AKI.
Practice Point 1.1.3: In adults, select the baseline SCr or cystatin C for an AKI episode
using a hierarchical approach (Figure 1).

Practice Point 1.2.1: The severity of AKI in children and adults is staged according to
individual components or combinations of the following SCr and urine output criteria
and presence or absence of an elevated biomarker of kidney stress or damage (Table 6).

Practice Point 1.2.2: Transient AKI is differentiated from persistent AKI based on an
increase in SCr or cystatin C or decrease in urine output lasting ≤48 versus >48 hours
(Table 7).

Duration of reduction in kidney function
Transient AKI

Persistent AKI

reduced urine output
AKI, acute kidney injury; SCr, serum creatinine

Practice Point 1.2.3: The underlying cause of AKI should be identified based on
susceptibilities and exposures, clinical context, history and physical examination, and
appropriate diagnostic laboratory and imaging testing to inform specific management.
1.3. Definition of AKD
Practice Point 1.3.1: Acute kidney disease (AKD) in children and adults is defined as
the presence of any of the following functional or structural criteria in the appropriate
clinical context with a duration ≤3 months (Table 9).
3

Practice Point 1.3.2: AKD without AKI should be differentiated from AKD following
AKI because the underlying causes and clinical management can be different.
1.4. AKI and AKD resolution
Practice Point 1.4.1: Complete and partial resolution of AKI and AKD are defined by
changes in SCr, serum cystatin C, or estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR) as
shown in Table 10.

D

1.5. Recurrent AKI
Practice Point 1.5.1: Recurrent AKI is defined as a new episode of AKI occurring after
partial or complete resolution from a previous episode of AKI.
1.6. Considerations specific to children
1.6.1. Definition of AKI
Practice Point 1.6.1.1: Age specific references ranges should be used when serum
cystatin C is used to detect AKI in children
1.6.2. Determination of baseline kidney function
Practice Point 1.6.2.1: In children (except neonates), select the baseline SCr or serum
cystatin C for an AKI episode using a hierarchical approach (Figure 3).

4

AF
T

Figure 3 | Hierarchical approach to the selection of the baseline SCr value used to
characterize an AKI event in children*. *A representative outpatient SCr should ideally
reflect the mean, median, or most recent value within 3 months of admission or acute event.
AKI, acute kidney injury; Cys C, cystatin C; eGFR, estimated glomerular filtration rate;
RRT, renal replacement therapy; SCr, serum creatinine
Practice Point 1.6.2.2: In children, specific considerations related to age are needed
when determining a baseline SCr.

R

1.6.3. Criteria for neonatal AKI
Practice Point 1.6.3.1: The criteria for neonatal AKI include a lack of decline in SCr
within the first 7 days of birth, in addition to neonatal specific increases in SCr or
cystatin C levels, or reduced urine output. (Table 12).

D

Table 12 | Criteria for AKI in neonates (any one or more of the following within the
corresponding time period)
Neonatal AKI criteria
• No decline in SCr from birth to 7 days of life
• Increase in SCr by ≥0.3 mg/dl (≥26.5 µmol/l) within 48 hours
• Increase in SCr by ≥1.5 times baseline that is known or presumed to have
occurred within the prior 7 days
• Increase in serum cystatin C by 1.25 times baseline that is known or
presumed to have occurred within the prior 7 days
• Serum cystatin C >2.2 mg/dl
• Urine output of less than 1.0 ml/kg/h for ≥24 hours
AKI, acute kidney injury; SCr, serum creatinine

Practice Point 1.6.4.1: In neonates, the severity of AKI is staged according to individual
components or combinations of the following SCr and urine output criteria (Table 13).

CHAPTER 2. AKI PREDICTION, RISK STRATIFICATION, AND
DIAGNOSTIC EVALUATION
2.1. Risk prediction models
Recommendation 2.1.1: We suggest using externally validated clinical risk models to
identify children and adults at increased risk of AKI and guide the use of kidneyprotective strategies (2C).
Recommendation 2.1.2: We recommend using externally validated clinical risk models
to identify adults at risk of receiving renal replacement therapy (RRT) for AKI to
inform patient choices and shared decision making about their care (1C).

AF
T

Practice Point 2.1.1: In community and low-resource settings, simplified AKI risk
scores can support early identification and management of adults at risk for
community-acquired AKI.

Practice Point 2.1.2: The performance of electronic health record (EHR)-based AKI
risk models should be tested in the settings they will be deployed. Harmonizing
terminology and data structure within EHR systems and reassessment of performance
over time is needed to ensure predictive accuracy in a local setting and over time.

R

2.2. Electronic AKI alerts
Recommendation 2.2.1: We recommend against delivering interruptive, creatininebased electronic alerts for AKI in isolation without systematic strategies for response in
routine clinical care of adults (1B).

D

2.3. Kidney functional testing
Practice Point 2.3.1: Assessment of kidney functional reserve before a potential
exposure may help to identify children and adults with an increased likelihood of
developing AKI.
Recommendation 2.3.1: We suggest using the furosemide stress test (FST) to assess the
risk of progression to Stage 3 AKI, including RRT in adults (2C) and children (2B) with
Stage 1 or 2 AKI who are euvolemic or hypervolemic.
Recommendation 2.3.2: We suggest calculating kinetic estimated glomerular filtration
rate (KeGFR) in hospitalized adults with or at risk of AKI when an estimate of GFR
under non-steady-state conditions will affect clinical decision-making (2B).

7

2.4. Biomarkers for AKI risk stratification
Practice Point 2.4.1: Baseline albuminuria (measured by urine albumin-to-creatinine
ratio [UACR or dipstick protein testing]) offers additional prognostic value to eGFR for
evaluating AKI risk in adults across various settings.
Recommendation 2.4.1: In acute care settings where children and adults are susceptible
to AKI, we suggest using biomarkers of kidney tubular stress or damage according to
their clinically validated and regulatory approved indication for the early identification
of people at risk of moderate or severe AKI, to inform targeted kidney-protective
strategies (adults: 2B; children: 2C).

AF
T

Practice Point 2.4.2: Biomarkers of kidney stress (e.g., urine [TIMP-2]*[IGFBP7]) can
be used to assess the risk of moderate-to-severe AKI in critically ill adults, especially
within the first 12 hours of ICU admission or after high-risk events including major
surgery or sepsis.
Practice Point 2.4.3: Biomarkers of kidney damage (e.g., urine NGAL) can be used to
identify children and adults at high risk of severe AKI in hospital settings.

R

2.5. Tests for AKI etiology and diagnostic evaluation
Recommendation 2.5.1. In adults with AKI, we suggest urine microscopy as part of the
diagnostic evaluation, particularly when the etiology is unclear or intrinsic kidney
disease is suspected, as urine sediment examination can provide diagnostic and
prognostic information that complements clinical assessment and other laboratory
studies (2D).

D

Practice Point 2.5.1: Muddy brown granular casts and renal tubular epithelial cells can
assist in distinguishing acute tubular injury (ATI) from hemodynamic-mediated AKI
and predict progression to severe AKI.
Practice Point 2.5.2: Urine eosinophil testing has low sensitivity and moderate
specificity, limiting its diagnostic value particularly for excluding a diagnosis of acute
interstitial nephritis (AIN).
Practice Point 2.5.3: Urinary inflammatory cytokines may be useful in distinguishing
AIN from other causes of AKI or AKD in adults.
Practice Point 2.5.4: Urine chemistries, including fractional excretion of sodium (FeNa)
and fractional excretion of urea (FeUrea) should be reserved for use in differentiating
hemodynamic-mediated (prerenal) AKI from ATI in selected clinical scenarios and
groups of children and adults with AKI, due to their limited overall clinical utility.

8

Practice Point 2.5.5: In adults with cirrhosis-associated AKI, FeNa can be an adjunctive
tool to distinguish hepatorenal syndrome from ATI when interpreted in the appropriate
clinical context.
Practice Point 2.5.6: In adults with cirrhosis-associated AKI, urinary or plasma NGAL
may be an adjunct to help differentiate between ATI and hepatorenal syndrome,
particularly when clinical features or response to volume expansion or vasoconstrictor
therapy are equivocal.
Practice Point 2.5.7: Kidney biopsy should be considered for children and adults with
unexplained AKI when clinical evaluation does not clearly differentiate among potential
causes or the findings are expected to influence management decisions.

AF
T

Practice Point 2.5.8: Kidney Doppler ultrasound, including measurement of renal
resistive index (RRI), may aid in risk stratification for AKI progression and severity,
particularly in critically ill populations.
Practice Point 2.5.9: In hospitalized or critically ill patients with AKI, point-of-care
ultrasound (POCUS) may assist in assessing volume status and distinguishing volume
responsive AKI from intrinsic AKI.
2.6. Considerations specific to children
Practice Point 2.6.1: Children at increased risk for community-acquired AKI should
undergo a kidney health assessment during episodes of acute illness.

D

R

Recommendation 2.6.1: For critically ill children at risk for AKI, we suggest using the
renal angina index (RAI) to identify children at high risk of severe AKI and RRT (2B).

9

CHAPTER 3. PREVENTION AND TREATMENT OF AKI
3.1 Fluid management
Recommendation 3.1.1: We recommend using crystalloids rather than colloids
(albumin, gelatin, or starches) as initial management for intravascular volume
expansion in children and adults at risk of or with AKI or AKD (1B).
Recommendation 3.1.2: We recommend using buffered crystalloids instead of 0.9%
saline for volume expansion in the absence of traumatic brain injury in children and
adults at risk of or with AKI or AKD (1B).

AF
T

Recommendation 3.1.3: For children and adults with AKI or AKD who have severe
metabolic acidosis with acidemia (pH <7.20), we suggest the use of intravenous
bicarbonate over RRT unless there is another urgent indication for RRT (2C).
Practice Point 3.1.1: Offer oral rehydration to children and adults at risk of or with
AKI or AKD being managed in settings where volume expansion with intravenous fluid
administration is not possible or practical.
Recommendation 3.1.4: For adults undergoing elective major abdominal surgery, we
recommend a liberal intravenous fluid management strategy, aiming for a positive fluid
balance gain of 1–2 kg at 24 hours after surgery, over a restrictive strategy (net zero
fluid balance) (1B).

R

3.2 Hemodynamic management
Practice Point 3.2.1: Use hemodynamic monitoring strategies for adults at risk of and
with AKI to assess adequate organ perfusion and oxygen delivery, which will be
context-sensitive and resource availability dependent.

D

Practice Point 3.2.2: Use vasoactive medications in conjunction with fluids in children
and adults with hypotension not responsive to initial fluid administration and who are
at risk of or have AKI.
Recommendation 3.2.1: We suggest a mean arterial pressure (MAP) of >65 mm Hg as a
target to guide fluid and vasopressor administration in critically ill adults at high risk
of or with AKI (2C).
Practice Point 3.2.3: Higher or lower MAP targets may be appropriate for individual
critically ill adults, for instance to maintain renal perfusion in patients with chronic
hypertension and to avoid adverse effects of excessive vasopressor therapy in patients
with chronic hypotension.

10

Recommendation 3.2.2: We recommend against using low-dose dopamine for kidney
protection in children or adults at risk of or with AKI or AKD (1A).
Recommendation 3.2.3: We recommend the use of a multicomponent kidney-protection
strategy in adult surgical patients at high risk for AKI identified by clinical factors and
biomarkers (Figure 9; 1B).

AF
T

Practice Point 3.2.4: In children and adults with established AKI or AKD, kidneyprotection strategies should be considered.

Figure 9 | Multicomponent kidney protection strategy for prevention and management
of AKI. AKI, acute kidney injury

R

3.3 Use of diuretics
Recommendation 3.3.1: We suggest use of diuretics in the management of clinically
significant volume overload of children and adults with or at risk of AKI or AKD (2B).

D

Practice Point 3.3.1: Intermittent boluses, rather than continuous infusions of loop
diuretics, are appropriate as the initial diuretic management strategy in adults with or
at risk of AKI or AKD.
Recommendation 3.3.2: We recommend initial use of diuretics in preference to
extracorporeal fluid removal for adults at risk of or with AKI or AKD who have
clinically significant volume overload in the absence of another indication for RRT
(1C).
3.4 Glucose control
Practice Point 3.4.1: Insulin therapy targeting a plasma glucose level of 140–180 mg/dl
(7.8–10 mmol/) is acceptable over more aggressive targets in critically ill children and
adults with or at risk of AKI or AKD.

11

3.5 Prevention of cardiac surgery-associated AKI
Recommendation 3.5.1: In adults without advanced CKD having cardiac surgery, we
suggest that prophylactic intravenous amino acid administration can be used to prevent
AKI (2A).
Recommendation 3.5.2: We suggest remote ischemic preconditioning for patients at
high risk for AKI undergoing cardiac surgery without propofol anesthesia to prevent
AKI in children (2D) and adults (2B).
3.6 Hepatorenal syndrome-associated AKI
Recommendation 3.6.1: We recommend the use of vasoconstrictor therapy for adults
with hepatorenal syndrome-associated AKI (1C).

AF
T

Practice Point 3.6.1: The choice of vasoconstrictor should be based on the clinical care
context and patient condition. Terlipressin or norepinephrine are preferrable therapies;
midodrine with octreotide can be used as an alternative for adults if terlipressin and
norepinephrine are unavailable.
3.7 Tumor lysis syndrome-associated AKI
Practice Point 3.7.1: Volume expansion without urinary alkalinization using
intravenous crystalloids should be done in children and adults with or at risk of tumor
lysis syndrome.

R

Recommendation 3.7.1: In children and adults with or at high risk of tumor lysis
syndrome, we recommend rasburicase over allopurinol or febuxostat, if not
contraindicated due to glucose 6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) deficiency (1B).

D

Practice Point 3.7.2: In children and adults at lower risk of tumor lysis syndrome, or
when rasburicase is contraindicated or inaccessible, xanthine oxidase inhibitors may be
used.
3.8 Rhabdomyolysis- and hemolysis-associated AKI
Practice Point 3.8.1: Use intravenous crystalloids for volume expansion in children and
adults at risk of or with heme pigment nephropathy due to rhabdomyolysis or
hemolysis.
3.9 Nutritional intervention in AKI
Practice Point 3.9.1: Nutrition should be provided preferentially via the enteral route in
children and adults at risk of or with AKI or AKD.
Practice Point 3.9.2: Avoid restriction of protein intake with the aim of preventing or
delaying initiation of RRT in children and adults with AKI or AKD.

12

Practice Point 3.9.3: Excessive protein intake should be avoided in adults with AKI or
AKD.
Practice Point 3.9.4: Caloric targets do not need to be changed due to AKI or AKD in
children or adults, unless they are receiving RRT.
Practice Point 3.9.5: Regular review and supplementation of water-soluble vitamins and
micronutrients should be considered in children and adults receiving continuous RRT
(CRRT).

AF
T

3.10 AKI in pregnancy
Practice Point 3.10.1: Pregnant women with AKI or AKD require specific treatments,
including consideration of early delivery.
3.11 Environmental and tropical AKI
Practice Point 3.11.1: For children and adults presenting with AKD or AKI in tropical
and subtropical regions, evaluate for region-specific infectious and environmental
causes and initiate early disease-specific therapy in addition to standard supportive
management of AKI.
Practice Point 3.11.2: For children and adults presenting with AKD or AKI in regions
where traditional medicines and toxic chemicals are used, include AKI due to toxins in
the differential diagnosis.

R

Practice Point 3.11.3: For children and adults presenting with AKD or AKI following
prolonged exposure to high temperatures, consider heat injury as a cause of AKI.

D

3.12 Considerations specific in children
3.12.1 Hemodynamic management
Practice Point 3.12.1.1: Use age-appropriate blood pressure targets to guide
hemodynamic management in children at risk or with AKI.
3.12.2 Nutritional intervention in AKI
Practice Point 3.12.2.1.: In children with AKI or AKD, age-recommended protein
targets can be used without protein restriction, and a minimum daily protein intake of
2.5 grams/kg should be maintained for children receiving CRRT.
Practice Point 3.12.2.2: Supplement water-soluble vitamins routinely in children
receiving CRRT.
3.12.3 Hepatorenal syndrome-associated AKI
Practice Point 3.12.3.1: It is reasonable to use terlipressin or norepinephrine for
children with hepatorenal syndrome-associated AKI depending on the clinical care
context and patient condition.
13

3.12.4 Theophylline use in neonates
Recommendation 3.12.4.1: We recommend a single dose of theophylline be given to
neonates with severe perinatal asphyxia for the prevention of AKI (1A).

D

R

AF
T

3.12.5 Use of nitric oxide in children
Recommendation 3.12.5.1: We recommend against routine use of inhaled nitric oxide to
prevent cardiac surgery-associated AKI in children with low-complexity cardiac lesions
and no signs of pulmonary hypertension or right ventricular dysfunction undergoing
cardiopulmonary bypass (1C).

14

CHAPTER 4. DRUG- AND NEPHROTOXIN-ASSOCIATED
AKI

R

AF
T

4.1. Categorization and classification of medications associated with AKI
Practice Point 4.1.1: Drugs that may contribute to AKI can be classified into one of 4
categories based on kidney function and damage biomarkers assessed at the time of
exposure (Figure 11).

D

Figure 11 | Classifying drugs that potentially cause AKI.530 Iterative classification of
agents that have potential to cause kidney dysfunction/abnormal kidney function or kidney
injury or both. Functional and injury biomarkers have a role in distinguishing among the
different pathophysiological processes. Examples of deleterious risk modifiers are duration
of therapy, drug burden, hypotension, and pharmaco/pharmacodynamic interactions.
Examples of interventions to mitigate risk are daily dynamic prescribing, kidney monitoring,
and patient and provider education. Susceptibility factors include those listed in Table 14 in
Chapter 2. Any final impact depends on underlying susceptibility, associated risk factors,
clinical context, drug management, and modifying factors. Examples of drugs that
correspond to the relevant categories above include trimethoprim, cimetidine (neither
dysfunction nor damage); angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors, angiotensin-receptor
blockers (dysfunction without damage); aminoglycoside, acyclovir, vascular endothelial
growth factor antagonists (damage without dysfunction); and nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory
drugs (dysfunction and damage). AKI, acute kidney injury. Adapted from Ostermann et al.
2020.531
4.2. Kidney protective strategies
Recommendation 4.2.1: For children or adults with or at risk of AKI, we recommend
drug and nephrotoxin stewardship using a multimodal approach (1C).
15

Practice Point 4.2.1: Strategies to manage drug-related acute kidney disease include:
a) discontinuation of offending nephrotoxic agents known to cause kidney damage, if
possible;
b) provision of a therapeutic equivalent alternative that is less nephrotoxic, if
available and necessary;
c) reduction in dose if drug continuation is necessary for those drugs with a dose
dependent direct nephrotoxic effect.

AF
T

Practice Point 4.2.2: Therapeutic drug monitoring is a component of multimodal
stewardship that involves serial assessments of drug concentrations in children and
adults receiving potentially nephrotoxic drugs, drugs excreted by the kidneys, and
drugs with narrow therapeutic margins or low therapeutic indices.
Practice Point 4.2.3: Surveillance for pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic drugdrug interactions that increase the risk of AKI is a component of multimodal
stewardship.
Practice Point 4.2.4: Intravenous hydration strategies may be used for the prevention of
drug-induced crystalline nephropathies as part of multimodal stewardship.

R

4.3. Implementation of kidney protective strategies
Practice Point 4.3.1: Pharmacists should perform medication reconciliation and
medication regimen reviews and perform drug and nephrotoxin stewardship for
hospitalized children and adults with or at risk of AKI.

D

Practice Point 4.3.2: Clinical decision support systems (CDSS) can be an effective tool
to facilitate drug and nephrotoxin stewardship strategies in children and adults to
prevent drug-associated AKI or the development of adverse reactions from drugs
excreted by the kidney.
4.4 Biomarker characterization of drug-related AKI
Practice Point 4.4.1: Biomarkers of tubular damage are approved for monitoring of
drug-induced AKI.
Practice Point 4.4.2: Biomarkers may be useful for differentiating mechanisms and
etiologies of drug-related kidney diseases in children and adults.
4.5. Management of drugs eliminated by the kidneys
Practice Point 4.5.1: Dosing of drugs excreted by the kidneys should not be based on
creatinine clearance or glomerular filtration rate (GFR) estimated using equations that
assume a steady state of creatinine production and clearance in children and adults
children with developing or resolving AKI or AKD.
16

Practice Point 4.5.2: Trends in SCr and urine output should be used to guide dosing of
drugs excreted by the kidney. In adults with AKI, calculation of a KeGFR may be
useful when sufficient SCr measurements are available.
The Work Group agrees with the following practice point related to drug dosing from
the KDIGO 2024 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Evaluation and Management of CKD
and believes it also applies to people with AKI and AKD.
Practice Point 4.5.3: Consider and adapt drug dosing in people where GFR, non-GFR
determinants of the filtration markers, or volume of distribution are not in a steady
state.

D

R

AF
T

Practice Point 4.5.4: Assess and adjust drug dosing in children and adults receiving
CRRT based on drug-, patient-, and RRT-related factors (Table 34).

17

Table 34 | Drug-, patient-, and RRT-related factors to consider when determining drug doses for people receiving CRRT
Drug-related
• Medications eliminated by the kidney with volume of distribution <0.7 l/kg and/or protein-binding <80% are likely
factors
to be removed with CRRT and may require supplemental dosing with CRRT use.

•
•
•
•
•
•

D
R

Patientrelated
factors

Molecular weight of a drug is an important consideration when using CRRT diffusive techniques compared to
convective techniques because the diffusion coefficient, or saturation coefficient (Sd or SA) is inversely
proportional to the molecular weight such that, as the SA decreases, the molecular weight increases. Conversely,
convective transport across modern membranes is independent of molecular weight in convective hemofiltration
techniques.594, 595
Most drugs have a molecular weight <2000 Daltons and are removed to some extent with newer high-flux and super
high-flux dialyzers that have a pore size between 10,000–30,000 (likely diffusion technique, as well)
Administering supplement doses for drugs with >30%–40% post-hemodialysis removal is suggested.
Optimal dosage regimens for medications eliminated by the kidney and nephrotoxic medications (e.g., digoxin,
vancomycin, aminoglycosides, and calcineurin inhibitors) can be achieved by performing therapeutic drug
monitoring in people who are critically ill receiving RRT.
For antibiotic dose adjustments during AKI and RRT, be mindful of pharmacokinetic target concentration
attainment and optimizing pharmacodynamic effects with consideration to the antibiotic concentration and time
dependent bactericidal effects.
Hypoalbuminemia affects drugs that are highly bound to albumin, by increasing free fraction of the drug leading to
increased clearance and volume of distribution.
Fluid resuscitation, and capillary leak increase the volume of distribution of hydrophilic drugs, for example in
people with sepsis. This may require a loading dose in the range of 25% more than usual.
People with significant urine output (e.g., >20 ml/hr or 500 ml/day), should have clearance from the residual urine
volume added to RRT clearance calculations to determine total drug clearance for the person.
Non-kidney drug clearance can be affected by RRT for AKI. Drugs eliminated by the liver and formed metabolites
may accumulate during AKI, while RRT can possibly increase CYP3A4 activity. Estimating non-kidney clearance
in people who are critically ill is challenging without assessing serum drug levels due to the various pathways
involved in drug metabolism and clearance.

AF
T

•

•
•

18

•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•

Effluent flow rate determines drug clearance in CRRT given it is slower than the blood flow rate and more drug
removal occurs with faster effluent flow rates.596
If RRT is <25% of the patient’s clearance (sum of residual kidney and non-kidney), then RRT appears not to add
significantly to person’s drug elimination process.595
RRT modality affects drug clearance such that when a similar blood flow rate is used, drug elimination by RRT can
be ordered from the highest to lowest as follows: CVVHDF > CVVH > IHD. 594
For CRRT, clearance can be estimated by checking the run time and subtracting the down time.
Replacement fluid is commonly provided in convective techniques of RRT, either pre- or post-dilution modes. Predilution mode reduces drug clearance more than post-dilution by approximately 15%.594
Hemofilter lifespan can affect solute clearance (i.e., older filters provide less clearance).
Membrane types affect adsorption and adsorption is inversely correlated to effluent flow rate. 594, 597, 598
Drug removal estimates for IHD are not equivalent to CRRT. When suing drug removal estimates from primary or
secondary sources, it is important to confirm that the RRT modality, technique, and settings are the same as the
patient scenario being evaluated.

AF
T

RRT-related
factors*

D
R

*RRT related factors do not apply to children due to the size of the extracorporeal volume. AKI, acute kidney injury; CRRT, continuous renal replacement
therapy; CVVH, continuous venovenous hemofiltration; CVVHD, continuous venovenous hemodialysis; CVVHDF, continuous venovenous
hemodiafiltration, IHD, intermittent hemodialysis, PIRRT, prolonged intermittent renal replacement therapy; RRT, renal replacement therapy

19

4.6. AKI following other nephrotoxic exposures
Practice Point 4.6.1: In children and adults presenting with community-acquired AKI,
perform a thorough social, occupational, and demographic history to understand
potential exposures to pesticides and environmental exposures as a possible cause of
AKI.
Practice Point 4.6.2: Determine and limit the use of natural products that have the
potential to contribute to AKI and AKD through patient-centered discussions.
4.7. Contrast-associated AKI (CA-AKI): Recognition
Practice Point 4.7.1: Consider the differential diagnosis of post-contrast AKI, which
may have causes other than contrast exposure.

AF
T

4.8. Contrast-associated AKI: Risk assessment
Practice Point 4.8.1: Use validated tools to assess the risk of AKI in adults receiving
intra-arterial radiocontrast for cardiac procedures.
Practice Point 4.8.2: Assess children and adults being considered for intravenous
radiocontrast administration for risk factors for contrast-associated AKI, and in
particular screen for preexisting impairment of kidney function.

D

R

4.9. Contrast-associated AKI: Prevention
4.9.1. Choice of imaging technique
Practice Point 4.9.1.1: Following a risk assessment, the decision to administer
intravascular contrast for a diagnostic or interventional procedure should be made
weighing the risks versus benefits of the procedure. Where the perceived benefit exceeds
the risk, contrast procedures should not be deferred or delayed. However, alternative
imaging methods should be used for people with or at high risk of contrast-associated
AKI (CA-AKI) (Figure 12).

20

AF
T

Figure 12 | Approach to risk assessment for CA-AKI with diagnostic or interventional
contrast imaging procedures. CA-AKI, contrast-associated acute kidney injury

R

Practice Point 4.9.1.2: Iso-osmolar or low-osmolar iodinated contrast media may be
used in children and adults at risk of CA-AKI (1B).

D

Practice Point 4.9.1.3: For children and adults at high risk of CA-AKI, use the lowest
possible dose of intravascular radiocontrast to achieve adequate imaging.
4.9.2. Contrast-associated AKI Prevention: Fluid administration
Practice Point 4.9.2.1: For children and adults at high risk of CA-AKI, incorporate
volume status assessment into intravenous hydration management strategies to reduce
the risk of AKI.
Recommendation 4.9.2.1: For adults at high risk of AKI without fluid overload
undergoing intravascular iodinated contrast-based procedures, we recommend
prophylactic periprocedural volume expansion with 0.9% saline over hypotonic
solutions (e.g., 0.45% saline), sodium bicarbonate, or no intravenous hydration (1B).

21

Practice Point 4.9.2.2: In the absence of volume overload, it is appropriate to hold
diuretics when administering isotonic crystalloid to prevent CA-AKI in adults at high
risk.
4.9.3 Prophylactic therapies: Medication management
The Work Group agrees with the following practice point and radiology guideline
statements related to the intravenous administration of radiocontrast from the KDIGO 2024
Clinical Practice Guideline for the Evaluation and Management of CKD.
Practice Point 4.9.3.1: The intravenous administration of radiocontrast media can be
managed in accordance with consensus statements from the radiology societies in
people with AKI or GFR <60 ml/min per 1.73 m2 (CKD G3a–G5) undergoing elective
investigation.

D

R

AF
T

The Work Group refers the reader to the most recent radiology guidelines specifically
noting the difference between intravenous and intra-arterial radiocontrast:
• Use of low-osmolality contrast media and iso-osmolarity contrast media
• Use of minimum radiocontrast dose to achieve a diagnostic study
• Withdrawal of nonessential potentially nephrotoxic medications (e.g., NSAIDs,
diuretics, aminoglycosides, amphotericin, platins, zoledronate, and methotrexate) in
people with AKI or eGFR <30 ml/min per 1.73 m2 for 24–48 hours before and 48
hours after radiocontrast exposure
• In people with eGFR >30 ml/min per 1.73 m2 and without evidence of AKI,
metformin need not be stopped before iodinated contrast media (ICM)
administration, and there is no need for testing to evaluate GFR afterward. For
people with AKI or an eGFR ≤30 ml/min per 1.73 m2, it remains appropriate to stop
metformin at the time of or before ICM injection and should not be restarted for at
least 48 hours and only then if GFR remains stable and the ongoing use of
metformin has been reassessed by the clinical team.673
• Consideration of avoiding dehydration for people not undergoing dialysis and who
have eGFR <30 ml/min per 1.73 m2 or AKI and receiving intravenous contrast.674,
675

•

•

Use of N-acetylcysteine, ascorbic acid, furosemide, dopamine, fenoldopam, or
calcium channel blockers as preventative measures of CA-AKI has not been shown
to be a consistent benefit.613
Prophylactic pericontrast hemodialysis has been shown to be potentially harmful
and is not recommended.613

Recommendation 4.9.3.1: We suggest not routinely withholding renin-angiotensin
system inhibitors (RASi) before procedures with iodinated radiocontrast administration
in adults (2C).

22

4.10 Temporary withdrawal of medications to prevent AKI during major surgery
Recommendation 4.10.1: We suggest not routinely withholding renin-angiotensin
system inhibitors (RASi) to prevent AKI in adults having elective major cardiac (2D) or
non-cardiac surgery (2B).
4.11 Medication management during intercurrent illness or “sick day protocols”
Recommendation 4.11.1: We suggest temporarily stopping specific classes of drugs in
the context of acute illnesses that predispose to volume depletion, to prevent AKI caused
by hypovolemia (2D).

AF
T

The Work Group also agrees with the following practice point from Section 4.3 of the
KDIGO 2024 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Evaluation and Management of CKD and
believe it also applies to people with AKI or AKD:16
Practice Point 4.11.1: If medications are discontinued during an acute illness,
communicate a clear plan of when to restart the discontinued medications to
the affected person and healthcare professionals, and ensure documentation
in the medical record.

4.12. Considerations specific to children
4.12.1 Biomarker characterization of drug-related AKI
Practice Point 4.12.1.1: Urine biomarkers of kidney damage can be helpful in detecting
nephrotoxin-associated AKI in newborns..

D

R

4.12.2. Management of drugs eliminated by the kidney
Practice Point 4.12.2.1: Trends in SCr and urine output can be used to guide dosing of
drugs excreted by the kidney. The validity of KeGFR equations to inform drug dosing
in children is uncertain.

23

CHAPTER 5. RENAL REPLACEMENT THERAPY
5.1. Starting renal replacement therapy (RRT)
Practice Point 5.1.1: For children and adults with AKI, a process of informed shared
decision-making should be used with patients, family, caregivers, and healthcare
professionals on the relative merit and risk of treatment with RRT.
Recommendation 5.1.1: For adults with AKI, we recommend a strategy of deferred
timing of RRT initiation as preferred over a strategy of early (pre-emptive) RRT
initiation (1C).

AF
T

Practice Point 5.1.2: For children and adults with AKI and high probability of
requiring RRT, planning for RRT should be considered before indications are met,
including, where applicable and available, transfer to centers with nephrology and RRT
services.
Practice Point 5.1.3: For children and adults with AKI and medically refractory fluid,
electrolyte, acid-base, and/or metabolic complications (e.g., uremia), initiate RRT if
consistent with patient preferences and goals of care.

R

Practice Point 5.1.4: Among patients with hepatic encephalopathy (grade ≥2) due to
acute or acute-on-chronic liver failure, initiation of continuous RRT (CRRT) to manage
hyperammonemia may be considered even when conventional RRT indications are
absent.
Practice Point 5.1.5: For critically ill children and adults with AKI, a time-limited trial
of RRT can be started if uremic and/or metabolic complications are plausibly
confounding neurological evaluation or chances of awakening from coma.

D

Practice Point 5.1.6: For children and adults with AKI, particularly those receiving
vasoactive support, identify and treat modifiable factors and mitigators of
hemodynamic instability at the commencement of RRT.
5.2. RRT modality
Practice Point 5.2.1: The initial selection of RRT modality in children and adults with
AKI, including CRRT, intermittent RRT (IRRT), or acute peritoneal dialysis (PD),
should integrate patient characteristics, clinical context, local expertise, and resource
availability.
Recommendation 5.2.1: For critically ill adults with AKI and hemodynamic instability,
we suggest using CRRT or acute PD over conventional IRRT (2C).
24

Practice Point: 5.2.2: CRRT is preferable to conventional IRRT for critically ill children
and adults with AKI and acute brain injury or other causes or risks of increased
intracranial pressure (ICP) or cerebral edema.
Practice Point 5.2.3: For children and adults with AKI, particularly those requiring
vasoactive support, identify and treat modifiable factors and mitigators of
hemodynamic instability during RRT.
Practice Point 5.2.4: Prolonged IRRT (PIRRT) is an acceptable alternative to CRRT as
the initial RRT modality for hemodynamically unstable, critically ill adults with AKI.

AF
T

Practice Point 5.2.5: Transition from CRRT to IRRT can be considered when children
and adults who have not recovered from AKI are physiologically and metabolically able
to tolerate IRRT and the risks of intracranial hypertension or cerebral edema, if
applicable, are resolved.
5.3. RRT dose-intensity
Recommendation 5.3.1: We recommend delivering an effluent volume of 20-25 ml/kg/h
over higher volumes for solute clearance in adults receiving CRRT, or a Kt/V of 3.9 per
week over higher urea clearance in adults receiving IRRT or PIRRT for AKI (1B).

R

Practice Point 5.3.1: An initial CRRT dose-intensity for adults with high body mass
index (BMI; e.g., ≥30 kg/m2) based on ideal or adjusted body weight, rather than actual
body weight, can be used to avoid delivery of excess CRRT dose-intensity.

D

Recommendation 5.3.2: We recommend not prescribing high-volume hemofiltration
(HVHF) compared with standard CRRT dose-intensity for critically ill adults with AKI
in the setting of sepsis or septic shock (1B).
Practice Point 5.3.2: The adequacy of the RRT dose-intensity to correct for electrolyte,
acid-base, and metabolic (e.g., nitrogenous waste) derangements should be reviewed
daily for CRRT and prior to starting each RRT session for IRRT and the prescription
adjusted when needed.
Practice Point 5.3.3: When using acute PD for adults with AKI, deliver a weekly Kt/V
of 2.2 (18-24 l PD fluid/day) over higher doses.
Practice Point 5.3.4: In adults with AKI, the initial PD prescription, including duration
of cycle time and glucose concentration of PD fluid, may need to be modified to rapidly
correct acid-base, electrolyte, metabolic, or fluid complications in adults with AKI.

25

5.4 Catheters for RRT
Practice Point 5.4.1: Vascular access for RRT, including type, size, length, and site of
catheter placement, should consider feasibility and weigh the risks of catheter
colonization/infection or bleeding with the desire to maintain catheter/circuit patency in
children and adults.
Practice Point 5.4.2: Adoption of standardized care bundles for dialysis catheter
insertion and maintenance can reduce the risk of complications.
Practice Point 5.4.3: Use of existing arteriovenous fistulas/grafts may be suitable for
IRRT in children and adults to avoid new dialysis catheter placement.

AF
T

Practice Point 5.4.4: It is preferable to avoid using existing arteriovenous (AV)
fistulas/grafts for CRRT in children or adults, due to concern for needle dislodgement
or arteriovenous fistulae/graft damage with prolonged cannulation.
Practice Point 5.4.5: The decision on how to replace poorly performing uncuffed
nontunneled dialysis catheters should be individualized. The options include a new
venipuncture or, in select cases, a guidewire exchange of the existing dialysis catheter.
Practice Point 5.4.6: Do not perform routine or scheduled change of dialysis catheters
to reduce the risk of catheter-related infection.

R

Practice Point 5.4.7: In circumstances where prolonged indications for RRT are
anticipated, a cuffed tunneled catheter can be considered when initiating RRT for AKI,
particularly for children.

D

Practice Point 5.4.8: For patients who remain dialysis dependent with low probability of
resolution of AKI or patients being prepared for hospital discharge, transition to a
cuffed tunneled catheter should be undertaken.
5.5. Anticoagulation for RRT
Recommendation 5.5.1: Where available, we recommend using regional citrate
anticoagulation rather than heparin in children and adults receiving CRRT who do not
have contraindications to citrate (1B).
Practice Point 5.5.1: Unfractionated heparin, epoprostenol, or nafamostat mesylate are
acceptable alternatives to regional citrate anticoagulation for children and adults
receiving CRRT if citrate is contraindicated or not available.

26

Practice Point 5.5.2: The addition of regional citrate anticoagulation to systematic
anticoagulation can be appropriate for children and adults with hypercoagulable states
receiving CRRT.
Practice Point 5.5.3: Unfractionated or low-molecular-weight heparin should be
considered for children and adults receiving intermittent hemodialysis (IHD) or PIRRT
without increased bleeding when not already receiving systematic anticoagulation.
Practice Point 5.5.4: Anticoagulation with argatroban, danaparoid, or bivalirudin,
rather than other non-heparin anticoagulants, should be used in adults receiving RRT
with acute heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) who require systemic
anticoagulation.

AF
T

Practice Point 5.5.5 Regional citrate anticoagulation rather than heparin or nonheparin anticoagulants is preferable in adults receiving RRT with subacute (HIT-A,
HIT-B) or remote HIT, who are not receiving systemic anticoagulation and require
anticoagulation to prevent clotting of the dialysis circuit.
5.6 Hemofilters for RRT
Practice Point 5.6.1: Biocompatible membrane hemofilters and dialyzers should be used
in children and adults with AKI undergoing CRRT or IHD.

R

Practice Point 5.6.2: Non-pharmacologic strategies that may be used to maintain and
maximize circuit/hemofilter lifespan include optimizing catheter position in the right
atrium, use of predilution hemofiltration, and continuous venovenous hemodialysis
(CVVHD) or hemodiafiltration (CVVHDF) rather than continuous venovenous
hemofiltration (CVVH).

D

Practice Point 5.6.3: Where regional citrate or systemic heparin anticoagulation are
contraindicated or not available, priming of the CRRT circuit/filter with a solution
containing unfractionated heparin (UFH) may be used to increase circuit/hemofilter
lifespan.
5.7. Replacement and dialysate solutions for RRT
Practice Point 5.7.1: Use bicarbonate, rather than lactate, as a buffer in dialysate and
replacement fluid for CRRT in adults with AKI in the following scenarios: circulatory
shock, liver failure or lactic acidemia.
Practice Point 5.7.2: CRRT dialysate and replacement solutions should be sterile.
Dialysate and replacement solutions for IRRT should comply with American
Association of Medical Instrumentation (AAMI) standards regarding contamination
with bacteria and endotoxins.
27

Practice Point 5.7.3: Locally compounded dialysate and replacement CRRT solutions
should be prepared using standard quality control processes or commercially available
dialysate and replacement CRRT solutions should be used to allow flexibility in
achieving various goals of plasma electrolyte compositions to address most clinical
circumstances.
Practice Point 5.7.4: Base the choice of dialysate and replacement CRRT solutions with
specific compositions upon the goals for serum electrolyte and acid–base parameters.
The use of dialysate and replacement solutions should be individualized and reassessed
at frequent intervals depending upon the clinical condition of the patient to ensure that
they are appropriate.

AF
T

5.8. Ultrafiltration in RRT
Practice Point 5.8.1: The target ultrafiltration should be prescribed daily for CRRT and
prior to each IRRT session and be frequently monitored.
Practice Point 5.8.2: The prescribed intensity of ultrafiltration (e.g., timing and rate of
removal) should be individualized and consider patient characteristics, clinical context,
and phase of illness.

R

5.9 Discontinuation of RRT
Practice Point 5.9.1: Discontinue RRT when it is no longer needed either because of
evidence of resolution of AKI to the point that kidney function is adequate to meet the
patient’s metabolic and fluid balance demands or because RRT is no longer consistent
with the patient’s preferences for care.

D

Practice Point 5.9.2: Clinical and metabolic parameters, interdialytic SCr changes,
urine output, fluid balance, other parameters of resolution of AKI, and residual kidney
function can be used to guide decision-making for RRT discontinuation.
Practice Point 5.9.3: Urine output >450 ml/24 hours without exposure to diuretics or
>2300 ml/24 hours with exposure to diuretics and 2-hour timed creatinine clearance
≥23 ml/min can be used to predict successful RRT discontinuation in adults.
Practice Point 5.9.4: Use of diuretic therapy prior to and following RRT discontinuation
can be used to augment urine output and manage fluid balance but has not been proven
to facilitate resolution of AKI.

28

AF
T

5.10 Quality assurance for RRT
Practice Point 5.10.1: RRT programs should include the following quality standards to
reduce unnecessary variation, facilitate adoption of evidence-based practices, and
support opportunities for continuous quality improvement:
• Dedicated leadership, ideally with a medical (physician) and nursing dyad
partnership.
• Certified training and continuous education of all healthcare professionals
prescribing and delivering acute RRT.
• Standardized order sets for the acute prescription of RRT, including decisionsupport for dose-intensity of solute clearance, ultrafiltration for fluid removal, and
anticoagulation strategy.
• Standardized charting for RRT for both conventional and electronic health
records, inclusive of parameters of patient and machine solute clearance and fluid
balance.
• Monitoring and reporting of quality and key performance measures of RRT
Practice Point 5.10.2: Key quality performance measures of RRT include measures of
effective treatment time (i.e., downtime), filter lifespan, prescribed-to-delivered doseintensity, and ultrafiltration.
5.11 RRT with extracorporeal life support (ECLS)
Practice Point 5.11.1: Strategies for integration of RRT into existing circuits in patients
receiving extracorporeal life support (ECLS) depend on clinical context, local expertise,
and available technology and resources.

R

Practice Point 5.11.2: Most patients receiving ECLS have concomitant hemodynamic
instability. For these patients, initial support with CRRT rather than IRRT may be
preferential.

D

Practice Point 5.11.3: Fluid accumulation that is refractory to medical therapy
independent of kidney function may be a reasonable indication for RRT in patients
receiving ECLS.
5.12. Considerations specific for children
5.12.1 Starting renal replacement therapy (RRT)
Practice Point 5.12.1.1: In critically ill children with severe AKI, RRT initiation within
2 days of intensive care unit (ICU) admission or AKI onset should be considered as it
may be associated with improved outcome.
Practice Point 5.12.1.2: In critically ill children, isolated fluid overload can be an
indication for RRT initiation.
29

5.12.2. RRT modality
Practice Point 5.12.2.1: Acute PD is an acceptable RRT modality and may be preferable
to CRRT for children in select circumstances.
5.12.3. RRT dose-intensity
Practice Point 5.12.3.1: An initial CRRT effluent volume for children can be 25–30
ml/kg/h. Thereafter, dose-intensity can be adjusted based on the adequacy of correction
of electrolyte, acid-base, and metabolic derangements.
Practice Point 5.12.3.2: The dose-intensity prescription in children receiving CRRT
should be weight-based (ml/kg/h), according to either body weight at hospital or ICU
admission or ideal body weight and not prescribed based on body surface area.

AF
T

Practice Point 5.12.3.3: In children with AKI, PD can be initiated with a fill volume of
10–20 ml/kg and short cycle times (60–90 minutes), with gradual increases in volume
and dwell time as tolerated, with continuous therapy over 24 hours for the first 1–3
days to achieve solute clearance and fluid balance goals.
Practice Point 5.12.3.4: Augmentation of initial RRT dose-intensity for CRRT is
warranted when treating children with hyperammonemia. CRRT dose-intensity up to
200 ml/kg/h may be acceptable to achieve adequate clearance of ammonia. Children
should be appropriately monitored for unintended solute losses during periods of
augmented CRRT dose-intensity.

D

R

5.12.4. Catheters for RRT
Practice Point 5.12.4.1: In small children, including neonates with small vessel size, use
of a pliable cuffed catheter, as compared to a rigid temporary catheter, may be less
thrombogenic and preferred, even when the expected dialysis duration is uncertain.
The cuff can be left outside the body when the projected duration of use is short.
5.12.5. Anticoagulation for RRT
Practice Point 5.12.5.1: In children requiring small catheters and circuit volume and
lower blood flow rates, anticoagulation is often necessary and preferred over no
anticoagulation during CRRT.
5.12.6. Hemofilters for RRT
Practice Point 5.12.6.1: Appropriately sized membranes proportional to body surface
area are preferred for children with AKI receiving CRRT or IRRT.

30

5.12.7. Ultrafiltration in RRT
Practice Point 5.12.7.1: In children, net ultrafiltration (UFNET) should not exceed 2.5
ml/kg/h. Frequent assessment of fluid balance (i.e., every 4-6 hours) should be
undertaken to evaluate tolerance for fluid removal and adjust fluid balance goals, as
necessary.

D

R

AF
T

5.12.8. Discontinuation of RRT
Practice Point 5.12.8.1: Urine output exceeding >0.5 ml/kg/h over 6 hours can indicate
resolution of AKI and be used to predict successful RRT discontinuation in children.

31

CHAPTER 6. FOLLOW-UP CARE AFTER AKI OR AKD
6.1 Planning follow-up care with people after AKI and/or AKD
Recommendation 6.1.1: We recommend coordinated follow-up for children and adults
after AKI and/or AKD, with personalized care according to individual clinical context
and vulnerability risks (1B).

AF
T

Practice Point 6.1.1: Transition of care from hospital to home requires clear
communication between inpatient and outpatient care teams, an identified healthcare
professional for AKI follow-up, and resources for patient engagement and education
about their kidney health and kidney health protection.
Practice Point 6.1.2: Provide discharge summaries for children and adults with AKI
that include details of the AKI episode, status of the resolution of AKI, medication
management, and follow-up plans with recommendations.
Practice Point 6.1.3: A comprehensive clinical assessment of children and adults
following an episode of illness affected by AKI/AKD includes assessment of blood
pressure (BP) and volume status, medication reconciliation/review, and kidney function
and albuminuria after hospital discharge.

R

Practice Point 6.1.4: Provide children and adults (and their caregivers) with a history of
AKI and/or AKD access to education about kidney health, sick day guidance and
kidney care, and psychosocial support to enhance functional status and quality of life.

D

Recommendation 6.1.2: We suggest risk stratification of AKI survivors at hospital
discharge using a validated tool to identify children and adults at high risk of CKD who
may benefit from nephrology care after AKI (2D).
6.2. Medication management following AKI and/or AKD
Recommendation 6.2.1: We recommend starting or resuming RASi in adults, unless
there is a contraindication, in accordance with evidence-based guideline indications
(adults: 1B; children: 1D).
Practice Point 6.2.1: Evaluate the clinical indication for starting or resuming sodiumglucose cotransporter-2 inhibitors (SGLT2i), glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists
(GLP-1 RA) and mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists (MRA) in children and adults.
Practice Point 6.2.2: Assess the appropriateness of starting or resuming guidelineindicated medications at hospital discharge and by 3 months after AKI/AKD.
xxxii

Practice Point 6.2.3: Monitor RASi, MRA, or diuretic prescription and management
following AKI/AKD in accordance with the KDIGO 2024 Clinical Practice Guideline
for the Evaluation and Management of Chronic Kidney Disease.
6.3 Evaluation for CKD
Practice Point 6.3.1: Assess kidney function and markers of kidney damage 3 months
after AKI or AKD to evaluate for resolution, and new onset or worsening of preexisting
CKD.

AF
T

Practice Point 6.3.2: Serum cystatin C-based GFR estimation can be used to assess for
CKD in children and adults after AKI in clinical situations where SCr is less accurate.
6.4 Post-discharge care for children and adults receiving RRT following AKI in the
outpatient setting
Practice Point 6.4.1: RRT programs should incorporate comprehensive dialysis care
and communication plans for children and adults receiving RRT following AKI tailored
to their likelihood of dialysis liberation.
Practice Point 6.4.2: Engage patients, caregivers, and outpatient dialysis care teams in
the development of a post-discharge care plan prior to transition from the hospital to
community settings.

D

R

Practice Point 6.4.3: Dialysis care for children and adults receiving RRT following AKI
requires clinical assessment of volume status, laboratory evaluation for evidence of
kidney recovery, medication review and reconciliation, and patient education about
identification of return of kidney function and minimizing kidney injury during the
resolution phase.
Practice Point 6.4.4: Provide education and engage adults, children, and caregivers in
shared decision-making focused around self-care, short- and long-term risks of
permanent RRT and mortality, quality of life, and goals of care to inform decisions
around dialysis care.
Practice Point 6.4.5: Upon discontinuation of outpatient dialysis, a nephrologist should
conduct a comprehensive assessment within 7 days, followed by an additional
evaluation of kidney function at 90 days.

xxxiii

6.5. Considerations specific to children
6.5.1 Planning follow-up care for children after AKI and/or AKD
Practice Point 6.5.1.1: Assess children following AKI for global functional status at
hospital discharge and for growth parameters and functional status at outpatient
follow-up.
Practice Point 6.5.1.2: In children previously taking RASi, these medications should be
resumed with resolution of AKI and/or AKD, unless there is a contraindication.

AF
T

6.5.2. Post-discharge care for children receiving RRT following AKI in the outpatient
setting
Practice Point 6.5.2.1: A risk-based approach guided by cause and severity of AKI and
kidney health assessments at hospital discharge and one year following an AKI episode
can be used to determine need for future nephrology follow-up in children.

D

R

Practice Point 6.5.2.2: Children recovering from AKI treated with RRT require
individualized follow-up that includes age-appropriate functional and developmental
assessments, growth and neurodevelopmental surveillance, and integrated care across
nephrology, primary care, and relevant subspecialties.

xxxiv

CHAPTER 1. AKI AND AKD DEFINITION, IDENTIFICATION,
AND CLASSIFICATION

AF
T

The definition of acute kidney impairment has evolved from a fragmented landscape
of disparate terms into a unified framework of Acute Kidney Injury (AKI) and Acute Kidney
Disease (AKD). While the field has relied on serum creatinine (SCr) and urine output since
the standardization efforts of 2009, the shift toward consistent Kidney Disease: Improving
Global Outcomes (KDIGO)-based staging has been instrumental in improving global
epidemiological tracking and clinical risk stratification. However, as SCr often serves as a
trailing marker of kinetic change, the current frontier of nephrology emphasizes integrating
novel biomarkers and updated evidence to identify kidney stress earlier. This chapter
explores the transition from these traditional functional markers to a modern, precision-based
approach to diagnosis and classification.
1.1. Definition of AKI
Practice Point 1.1.1: Acute kidney injury (AKI) in children and adults is defined by the
presence of one or more abnormalities of kidney function or structure known or
presumed to have occurred within 7 days, in the appropriate clinical context (Table 1).

R

Table 1 | Criteria for acute kidney injury for children and adults (any one or more of the
following within the corresponding time period)
Functional criteria
Structural criteria
• Increase in SCr by ≥0.3 mg/dl (≥26.5 µmol/l) • Elevation of a biomarker of kidney damage
within 48 hours
when used for its clinically validated and
regulatory qualified indication, which is
• Increase in SCr by ≥1.5 times baseline that is known or presumed to have occurred within
known or presumed to have occurred within
the prior 7 days
the prior 7 days

D

• Increase in serum cystatin C by ≥1.5 times
baseline that is known or presumed to have
occurred within the prior 7 days

• Mean urine volume of less than 0.5 ml/kg/h
(based on ideal body weight) for ≥6 hours
SCr, serum creatinine

The KDIGO 2012 Clinical Practice Guideline for Acute Kidney Injury provided a
definition for AKI based on changes in SCr and urine output that are associated with the risks
of death and receipt of renal replacement therapy (RRT) in hospitalized patients.1 SCr and
urine output are markers of kidney function, specifically glomerular filtration rate (GFR).
Both measures are widely used in routine clinical practice and can be obtained at relatively
low cost. However, neither measure is entirely sensitive or specific to GFR and can lead to
misclassification of AKI (Table 2).

35

R

D

The development and validation of several novel biomarkers over the last decade has
enabled earlier detection of kidney cellular stress, damage, or inflammation compared with
the use of functional markers alone, thus enabling earlier intervention and potentially
improving outcomes.2 Further, alternative functional biomarkers (i.e., serum cystatin C) are
now available that overcome some of the limitations of SCr. For instance, cystatin C is not
confounded by muscle mass. However, these markers have their own limitations, as well
(Recommendation 1.1.1).
The updated definition adds new criteria for the purpose of AKI detection based on
abnormalities of kidney structure, which reflect cellular stress, damage, or inflammation that
may occur in the presence or absence of reduced kidney function. These new structural
criteria for AKI are intended to encompass a number of urine or serum biomarkers that have
received regulatory qualification for use in specific clinical settings based in part on their
ability to predict the development of AKI before functional criteria are met (Table 3 and 4).

36

These biomarkers may become elevated before an increase in SCr or reduction in
urine output occurs; a state that has previously been termed “subclinical AKI”. Examples of
these biomarkers include urinary tissue inhibitor of metalloproteinases-2 (TIMP-2), insulinlike growth factor binding protein 7 (IGFBP7), neutrophil gelatinase-associated lipoprotein
(NGAL), kidney injury molecule‑1 (KIM-1), and liver-type fatty acid-binding protein (LFABP).

AF
T

A common method of categorizing kidney biomarkers is to stratify them based on
their characteristics. Thus, some biomarkers primarily reflect glomerular filtration (i.e. serum
cystatin C) whereas others represent tubular cell stress (i.e., IGFBP7 and TIMP-2) or tubular
cell damage [i.e. NGAL, KIM-1, NAG, L-FABP]. Prolonged and increased expression of
TIMP-2 and IGFBP7 may be synonymous with cell damage. Other kidney biomarkers
indicate intrarenal inflammation (i.e. IL-18, IL-9, CCL14).
All biomarkers have specific characteristics, are released in response to particular
triggers and have unique kinetic profiles, including half-life and time to peak value (Table 4).
Many are elevated in AKI but also confounded by different medical conditions or
interventions. Importantly, several studies in the literature used different cut-offs to
distinguish between “biomarker-positive” versus “biomarker-negative” conditions, which
explains some of the differences in performance but also makes direct comparisons between
studies challenging.

R

It is important to keep these aspects in mind when measuring and interpreting
biomarker values in specific patient cohorts. When deciding whether to integrate a biomarker
into routine monitoring or to include a biomarker in the diagnostic work-up of a specific
clinical setting, it is essential to select one that is physiologically and biologically relevant to
the question of interest. Further, timing of measurement needs to be guided by the kinetic
profile of the biomarker.

D

The availability and higher costs of new biomarker tests limit their use in many
settings but it is possible that earlier detection of AKI may ultimately offset these costs due to
lower resource utilization and healthcare spending.

37

AF

AIN, acute interstitial nephritis; AKI, acute kidney injury; CE, European Conformity; CKD, chronic kidney disease; FDA, Food and Drug Administration; ICU,
intensive care unit; RRT, renal replacement therapy; SCr, serum creatinine; U.S., United States; UTI, urinary tract infection

44

Recommendation 1.1.1: We suggest using serum cystatin C to identify AKI in children
and adults in clinical situations when serum creatinine (SCr) is less accurate (2B).
This recommendation puts a high value on the greater sensitivity of cystatin C for detecting
AKI in specific patient populations where changes in SCr can be insensitive. The
recommendation places a lower value on resource use and additional costs. The feasibility of
implementing this recommendation is highly dependent on the availability and turnaround
time for cystatin C measurement.

AF
T

Key information
Balance of benefits and harms
Previous AKI criteria using SCr and urine output measurements were established
based on risks of kidney failure and mortality.1, 12 Cystatin C is an alternative endogenous
filtration marker that provides a more accurate GFR estimate than SCr in some specific
populations. In a study of 38 mechanically ventilated adults in the intensive care unit (ICU),
longer ICU stay was associated with greater differences between GFR estimated by serum
cystatin C and SCr, suggesting that SCr can systematically overestimate GFR after prolonged
critical illness.13 A recent modeling study found that serum cystatin C can achieve steadystate concentration and meet AKI diagnostic thresholds significantly faster than SCr, with an
advantage of 6–48 hours, depending on chronic kidney disease (CKD) severity. Serum
cystatin C had greater sensitivity in detecting GFR reductions, with the ability to detect AKI
within 12–24 hours after an insult, compared with 12–72 hours for SCr.14

D

R

Several meta-analyses have evaluated the diagnostic performance of cystatin C for
early AKI identification. A meta-analysis by Lan et al. included 4 diagnostic accuracy
studies that evaluated the performance of serum cystatin C, most often measured within 6–12
hours of major surgery in adults, using a reference standard of later development of AKI
defined by Risk, Injury, Failure, Loss of kidney function, and End-stage kidney disease
(RIFLE), Acute Kidney Injury Network (AKIN), or the KDIGO 2012 AKI criteria
(Supplementary Tables S5).15 Serum cystatin C exhibited relatively high discrimination with
an area under the receiver-operating characteristics curve (AUROC) of 0.82 (95% confidence
interval [CI]: 0.78–0.85). In an accompanying network meta-analysis, serum cystatin C
exhibited the best discrimination and had the greatest specificity for early AKI detection
among the 7 biomarkers included in the analysis.15 Evidence review of 16 pediatric studies
found that serum cystatin C had moderate accuracy to predict incident AKI (AUC 0.80) and
moderate or severe AKI (AUC 0.83) in children (Supplementary Table S6).16
Certainty of evidence
In adults, there is moderate certainty of evidence that serum cystatin C has
“moderate” accuracy as an early predictor of post-major surgery AKI, defined as an AUROC
curve of between 0.75 and 0.85. The certainty of evidence was downgraded for some
methodological limitations of the studies.
45

In children, there is moderate certainty of evidence that serum cystatin C has
“moderate” accuracy as an early predictor of incident AKI and Stage 2–3 AKI, defined as an
AUROC of between 0.75 and 0.85. The certainty of evidence was downgraded for some
methodological limitations of the studies, in addition to imprecision of the accuracy of Stage
2–3 AKI.

AF
T

Values and preferences
This recommendation places a high value on the need for accurate and timely
detection of a decline in GFR. The Work Group judged that healthcare professionals and
most people at risk for AKI would prefer to use a more accurate test enabling earlier
identification of AKI in situations where changes in SCr are insensitive or nonspecific.
However, these benefits need to be balanced against the additional consequences and costs
associated with cystatin C testing. The Work Group therefore prioritized reserving use of
cystatin C for AKI detection to the clinical situations provided in Table 5 where more
accurate measurement may alter clinical decisions.

R

Resource use and costs
Costs incurred for the higher frequency of cystatin C testing include one-time costs
associated with initiation of the assay within a laboratory including building the information
technology infrastructure and method verification studies; and continuous costs associated
with consumables and maintaining the assay. If cystatin C is performed in an outside
laboratory, other costs may ensue as with any laboratory test. Additional costs may also
result from increased referrals to nephrologists to assist in interpretation of potentially
discordant results between SCr and cystatin C results. Ideally, these costs would decrease
over time with increased utilization and familiarity with the properties of the test.

D

Considerations for implementation
Implementation of this recommendation requires cystatin C testing be available in
acute care centers with results available in a timely manner. Guidance to clinical laboratories
on implementation and laboratory standards for cystatin C testing is provided in the KDIGO
2024 Clinical Practice Guideline for CKD (Practice Points 1.2.3.1 and 1.2.3.2).17 Due to the
increased costs of cystatin C testing, clear indications for testing with cystatin C in addition
to SCr should be developed within institutions and SCr should remain the primary laboratory
tests for AKI identification in the majority of patients. Education for healthcare professionals,
caregivers, and people with AKI for optimal use and interpretation of cystatin C results is
required.
Rationale
Changes in SCr may be insensitive for detection of AKI in acute and chronic illnesses
that affect liver function and muscle mass (Table 2). In these scenarios, serum cystatin C may
offer greater accuracy than SCr for early detection of AKI and opportunities for intervention.
46

Potential harms include false positive test results, overdiagnosis, and the increased health
care costs associated with the test itself as well as follow-up testing. In addition, discordant
results between SCr and cystatin C may lead to increased nephrology consultations.
Obtaining cystatin C results may take longer than SCr result depending on the local
laboratory infrastructure and processes, which may lead to delays in recognition and offset
the benefits of its use if results are not reported in a timely manner. Table 5 outlines specific
situations where cystatin C measurement may be preferable to SCr to detect reduced GFR.

AF
T

Table 5. Specific situations where serum cystatin C measurement may be preferable to SCr to
detect reduced GFR
Domain
Specific clinical condition
Changes in muscle mass Muscle wasting after prolonged illness
Neurological diseases (e.g., muscle dystrophy)
Spinal cord injury, paraplegia/quadriplegia
Above knee amputation
Extreme sport/exercise
Eating disorders
Diet
Low-protein diet
Vegetarian
High protein diets and creatine supplementation
Medications
Drugs that interfere with tubular secretion of creatinine (e.g.,
cimetidine, trimethoprim)
GFR, glomerular filtration rate; SCr, serum creatinine

Practice Point 1.1.2: A baseline SCr value should be ascertained for all patients with
suspected or confirmed AKI.

D

R

The KDIGO SCr criteria for AKI are based on an increase from a baseline value
known or presumed to have occurred over a short time frame. Identification of an accurate
baseline value is therefore required to establish whether a specific SCr value represents a
change and whether the change is sufficient to meet AKI criteria. This is also needed for
staging the severity of AKI based on the magnitude of SCr increase and for ascertaining the
extent of recovery of GFR following AKI. A baseline SCr should be representative of a
patient’s GFR before the onset of AKI. In clinical practice, variation in the extent of SCr
testing for a patient will influence the selection of a representative SCr value as the baseline
value. Access to previous laboratory results obtained prior to an acute illness is helpful in
establishing an appropriate baseline. In their absence, several approaches have been
recommended in the literature including use of a SCr representative of normal GFR for a
patient’s age and sex, or the use of a subsequent lower SCr value when SCr values are
declining in the absence of RRT.

47

AF
T

Practice Point 1.1.3: In adults, select the baseline SCr or cystatin C for an AKI episode
using a hierarchical approach (Figure 1).

Figure 1 | Hierarchical approach to the selection of the baseline SCr value used to
characterize an AKI event in adults*. *A representative primary care/outpatient serum
creatinine (SCr) should ideally reflect the stable clinical state, and may be a mean, median, or
most recent value between 8–365 days before a hospital admission (or acute event). AKI,
acute kidney injury; Cys C, cystatin C; eGFR, estimated glomerular filtration rate; RRT,
renal replacement therapy

D

R

There are several different approaches described for ascertaining baseline SCr values
(Supplementary Tables S7 and S8). Therefore, we advise using a mean, median, or most
recent SCr measurement between 8–365 days prior to admission or acute event as the
preferred method to establish baseline kidney function representing a stable clinical state.
Differences in methods for establishing baseline SCr can affect whether patients meet AKI
criteria leading to both over- and underdiagnosis. Underdiagnosis of AKI can lead to delayed
recognition thereby delaying investigations and treatment. Overdiagnosis of AKI in people
with CKD may expose patients to unwarranted tests and interventions. In addition, use of
varying methods in epidemiological studies impacts the comparability of findings, limiting
interpretation of consistency of findings across different geographic regions and clinical
settings.
Access to outpatient SCr values may not be available in some settings. Wherever
possible, systems should ideally be integrated or if this is not possible, primary care and
outpatient values should be accessible to clinicians in secondary care settings, and vice versa.
Education of healthcare professionals is required to ensure optimum implementation of this
recommendation.

48

In the absence of an available outpatient SCr within the last year, we advise using SCr
on admission as the next option unless AKI is suspected on admission (e.g., communityacquired AKI). This provides a readily available value that is more appropriate for patients
admitted for elective procedures (e.g., surgery, imaging procedures) but may be affected by
conditions which have precipitated nonelective admission for conditions such as volume
depletion or sepsis. An alternative to this approach is the use of the minimum value during
admission including post-AKI values, allowing for retrospective AKI diagnosis. This
approach is less desirable due to the influence of fluctuations or interventions that might
lower SCr, such as fluid administration or withdrawal of medications that lower GFR and
would not be representative of the true baseline in patients who have not fully recovered
kidney function.

AF
T

In the absence of any available measurements, we advise back calculating, assuming
an estimated GFR (eGFR) of 75 ml/min per 1.73 m2 for adults, based on a validated eGFR
estimating equation is acceptable.18 Use of an equation with greater accuracy at higher eGFR,
such as the Chronic Kidney Disease Epidemiology Collaboration Equation (CKD EPI)
equation, is preferable to equations that have been previously recommended such as the
Modification of Diet in Renal Disease (MDRD) equation. However, assuming a baseline
GFR of 75 ml/min per 1.73 m2 for adults does not account for individual patient
characteristics and the presence of underlying CKD. Studies have shown that this method
leads to both over- and underestimation of AKI.19-24 Approaches specific to calculating a
baseline SCr in children are discussed in Practice Point 1.6.2.

R

For the purpose of epidemiological research, use the median of outpatient values 7–
365 days prior to admission or an acute event is advised. It is important to ensure that there is
a uniform approach to defining AKI for epidemiological studies, allowing for valid
comparisons between studies and for analyses of trends over time.

D

1.2. Staging of AKI
Practice Point 1.2.1: The severity of AKI in children and adults is staged according to
individual components or combinations of the following SCr and urine output criteria
and presence or absence of an elevated biomarker of kidney stress or damage (Table 6).

49

Table 6 | Severity staging system for AKI
Functional criteria
Serum creatinine
Stage

Criteria

C0

C1

C2
