*C1-C3 indicates acute kidney injury (AKI) severity stage according to serum creatinine (SCr)
criteria, U1-U3 indicates AKI severity stage according to urine output criteria, B0/B1 indicates
staging by absence or presence of an elevated damage biomarker. Relevant components of the staging
system can be employed to classify the severity of AKI according to the measurements collected for
an individual. RRT, renal replacement therapy

D

R

The KDIGO 2012 AKI staging system was based on SCr and urine output criteria
with the patient assigned a severity Stage (1–3) according to the highest (worst) SCr or urine
output stage. Subsequently, numerous large observational studies from intensive care settings
have shown poor agreement and varying prognosis between SCr and urine output criteria for
identifying and staging AKI (Supplementary Tables S9 and S10). Kellum et al. examined
outcomes of 32,045 patients from 8 adult ICUs in the U.S. with electronic records of SCr and
urine output measures and showed that, across the KDIGO 2012 AKI stages based on SCr,
higher stages of urine output criteria were generally associated with higher risks of both
hospital mortality and RRT.25 A subsequent study restricted to adults who received cardiac
surgery found that major adverse kidney events (MAKE) at 180 days after surgery (defined
by death, RRT, or persistent abnormal kidney function) also increased with both higher SCr
and urine output stages.26 In a study by Bianchi et al., examining the KDIGO 2012 criteria in
an adult ICU in Switzerland, urine output identified AKI in patients who did not fulfill SCr
criteria or increased their AKI stage in 8073 (51.7%) patients with AKI.27, 28 This included
2443 (30.2%) patients whose AKI severity stage was increased by adding urine output
criteria. This reclassification of the AKI stage with the addition of urine output to SCr criteria
had important prognostic implications, as 90-day mortality varied by urine output category
within each SCr category. In multivariable adjusted models, greater urine output stages were
50

associated with a significantly higher 90-day mortality within each SCr stage; for example,
among patients with Stage 1 AKI based on SCr, those with Stage 3, 2, 1 and no AKI based
on urine output criteria had 90-day mortality risks of 37%, 17%, 16%, and 11%, respectively.
Additional studies have demonstrated that the addition of the urine output criteria to the SCr
criteria further stratifies patients for risk of adverse patient outcomes 25, 26, 28-30

AF
T

Recently, alternative urine output criteria based on modified thresholds for urine
output staging have been proposed (Supplementary Tables S11 and S12). Machado et al.
compared the sensitivity and specificity of different urine thresholds and time frames for
mortality prediction in critically ill adults.31 They selected 3 cut-off points for alternative
urine output staging: Stage 1 (0.2–0.3 ml/kg/h), Stage 2 (0.1–0.2 ml/kg/h), and Stage 3 (<0.1
ml/kg/h), all over 6 hours. When combined with KDIGO SCr criteria, this alternative staging
system exhibited better discrimination, with net improvement in risk classification compared
with the KDIGO 2012 urine output and SCr criteria for predicting mortality. This suggests
that lower urine thresholds within 6 hours are prognostically important in critically ill adults
but further research is needed.

R

The inclusion of structural criteria for AKI based on elevation of damage biomarkers
has been shown to further improve the classification and prognostic risk stratification of AKI
beyond that provided by SCr alone. A pooled analysis of critically ill patients from 10
observational studies reported that, among patients meeting SCr criteria for AKI, the
subsequent risk of RRT initiation, length of ICU and hospital stay, and in-hospital mortality
were greater in NGAL-positive compared with NGAL-negative patients.32 In a study of adult
patients receiving mechanical ventilation, SCr combined with urinary NGAL provided a
more accurate prediction of RRT requirement than SCr or NGAL alone.33 Another structural
biomarker, CCL14, has been shown to identify patients at higher risk of persistent (≥72
hours) Stage 3 AKI in critically ill patients meeting KDIGO 2012 Stage 2 or 3 AKI criteria. 34

D

The updated staging system for AKI presented here incorporates both functional
(SCr, and urine output) in addition to structural criteria (damage biomarkers) to stage the
severity of AKI and to improve risk stratification for important clinical outcomes of AKI.
Although measurement of SCr is routinely performed in many acute care settings, accurate
measurement of urine output is not feasible in all clinical settings which limits accurate
staging of patients outside intensive care units and for patients without urinary catheters.
This revised staging system places a high value on more granular evaluation of AKI
severity based on prognosis. However, new damage biomarker tests are more expensive and
availability is limited in both high and low resource settings. Nonetheless, relevant
components of the staging system can be employed to classify the severity of AKI according
to the measurements available in a given institution.

51

Practice Point 1.2.2: Transient AKI is differentiated from persistent AKI based on an
increase in SCr or cystatin C or decrease in urine output lasting ≤48 versus >48 hours
(Table 7).
Table 7 | Definitions of transient and persistent AKI
Duration of reduction in kidney function
Transient AKI
≤48 hour increase in SCr or cystatin C or reduced urine output
Persistent AKI
>48 hour and up to 7 days increase in SCr or cystatin C or
reduced urine output
AKI, acute kidney injury; SCr, serum creatinine

D

R

AF
T

Since publication of the KDIGO 2012 AKI guideline, there have been variations in
how the trajectory of kidney function, resolution, and transient versus persistent AKI have
been defined (Supplementary Tables S13 and S14). Transient AKI has typically been defined
by a return of SCr to baseline levels within 48–72 hours. Studies of AKI resolution patterns
have consistently demonstrated that complete and sustained reversal of AKI within this
timeframe is associated with better long-term outcomes.35-57 This may reflect the fact that
transient AKI is often caused by reduced kidney perfusion, and that correction of kidney
perfusion, such as by correcting volume status or removing medications that lower
glomerular pressure, can rapidly restore kidney function to baseline levels. In 2017, the
Acute Disease Quality Initiative (ADQI) group recommended defining transient or rapid
reversal of AKI as within 48 hours of onset and persistent AKI as beyond 48 hours. 58 Jensen
et al. showed in a large population-based cohort that, compared to transient AKI (≤48 hours),
persistent AKI (2–7 days) was associated with a higher incidence of CKD and kidney
failure.44 Analysis of a large cohort of hospitalized patients showed that mortality was higher
in those with persistent AKI compared with transient AKI defined as reversal within 48
hours.40 A cohort study from the Veterans Health Administration in the U.S. demonstrated
a >40% increase in risk of CKD G3 or higher following transient AKI lasting less than 2
days, compared to a 2-fold increase in risk among those with AKI whose kidney function
recovered to within 0.3 mg/dl of baseline SCr within 3–10 days.57 Distinguishing persistent
versus transient AKI is thus relevant to assessing the long-term risk of CKD, kidney failure,
and survival after AKI, with implications for subsequent lab testing and follow-up of people
with AKI. Standardizing the definition for transient and persistent AKI is important to enable
consistent communication about the trajectory of AKI and for comparison of both
epidemiological and clinical studies.
The Work Group favored the adoption of a 48-hour time frame as opposed to the 72hour time frame because i) an earlier assessment of kidney function after onset of AKI was
felt to be most clinically appropriate, and ii) it reflects the definitions used in the majority of
the available evidence and the consistency of relationships with CKD and mortality across
studies. Although most studies have defined transient versus persistent AKI on the basis of
SCr alone, the Work Group felt it was appropriate to also apply this timeframe to other

52

functional criteria for AKI, including serum cystatin C and urine output. This definition
applies to both children and adults.
Practice Point 1.2.3: The underlying cause of AKI should be identified based on
susceptibilities and exposures, clinical context, history and physical examination, and
appropriate diagnostic laboratory and imaging testing to inform specific management.

AF
T

AKI is a heterogeneous clinical syndrome with many etiologies, which vary in
frequency by clinical setting and their responsiveness to general supportive care of the
acutely ill patient. Some causes of AKI require specific treatments, including acute
glomerulonephritis, vasculitis, interstitial nephritis, thrombotic microangiopathy, and urinary
tract obstruction (Table 8). In some patients, AKI is part of a systemic illness, such as
myeloma or connective tissue disease where AKI may improve with treatment of the
underlying condition. The clinical evaluation of AKI includes a careful history and physical
examination to identify exposures and susceptibilities and guide appropriate laboratory and
imaging tests.

D

R

Important exposures include dehydration, infections, trauma, burns, surgery, shock,
drugs, poisons, and toxins. Susceptibilities to AKI following these exposures can vary
between individuals on the basis of age, sex, race and comorbidities such as CKD, diabetes
mellitus, cancer, heart, lung, and liver disease.

53

54

1.3. Definition of AKD
Practice Point 1.3.1: Acute kidney disease (AKD) in children and adults is defined as
the presence of any of the following functional or structural criteria in the appropriate
clinical context with a duration ≤3 months (Table 9).
Table 9 | Criteria for AKD (any one or more of the following with a duration ≤3 months)
Functional criteria
Structural criteria
AKI (by functional criteria),
AKI (by structural criteria)
Or
Or
GFR <60 ml/min per 1.73 m2*
Marker of kidney damage (albuminuria,
Or
hematuria, or leukocyturia are most common)
2
Decrease in GFR by ≥35 ml/min per 1.73 m
from baseline
Or
Increase in SCr by >50%

AF
T

*GFR <60 ml/min per 1.73 m2 criterion does not apply to children younger than 2 years old. AKI,
acute kidney disease; AKI, acute kidney injury; GFR, glomerular filtration rate; SCr, serum creatinine

D

R

AKD includes AKI as well as alterations of kidney function and structure with a
duration ≤3 months. Patients with AKD may not meet the criteria for AKI because the GFR
decline is not sufficient to fulfill criteria for AKI (i.e., GFR decline is too small or too slow).
Recognizing AKD enables an integrated clinical approach to people with abnormalities of
kidney function and structure, including alterations of kidney function and structure that do
not meet the criteria for the definition of either AKI or CKD, yet may need medical attention
to restore kidney function and reverse damage to kidney structure to avoid adverse outcomes.
AKD is nearly 3 times as prevalent as AKI (Figure 2).59 The association between AKD and
adverse patient outcomes has been well-established with many observational studies showing
that AKD without AKI is associated with CKD, requirement for RRT, MAKE, length of
hospital stay, and mortality (Supplementary Table S15). This definition applies to both
children and adults.

Figure 2 | AKD, AKI, and kidney disease outcomes. AKD, acute kidney disease; AKI,
acute kidney injury; CKD, chronic kidney disease; GFR, glomerular filtration rate; NKD, no
kidney disease
A recent systematic review on the incidence of AKD and its associated adverse
outcomes included 59 studies involving almost 6 million participants.60 The authors included
studies defining AKD by the KDIGO definition. The pooled incidence of AKD defined by
55

the KDIGO criteria was 11.1% (95% CI: 7.6%–16.3%). Among studies that were not
restricted only to patients with AKI at study entry, AKD incidence was similar at 11.1% and
the pooled odds ratio (OR) of all-cause mortality associated with AKD was 3.8 (95% CI:
2.2–6.7).

AF
T

Recognition of AKD across healthcare settings identifies patients with kidney disease
at risk of adverse long-term kidney and related outcomes, such as cardiovascular events and
mortality. There may be a risk of misdiagnosing CKD as AKD when prior testing has not
been performed, leading to unnecessary interventions and anxiety. Increased testing may
place an additional burden on healthcare systems and there also may be variability in
interpretation and management. However, earlier identification and management of AKD
may reduce longer term costs associated with CKD such as the requirement for RRT and
cardiovascular complications. Education and training of healthcare professionals on
interpretation of this definition is required, particularly in primary care as it is more
commonly encountered in the outpatient setting, and healthcare professionals may be less
familiar with management approaches for AKD than CKD. Where possible, integration into
electronic health care record systems and decision support tools may assist the
implementation.
Practice Point 1.3.2: AKD without AKI should be differentiated from AKD following
AKI because the underlying causes and clinical management can be different.

R

The diseases and disorders that cause AKI can impact long-term kidney function and
so AKD may be present following AKI, with the risk varying according to AKI severity and
preexisting CKD. Patients can also be identified with AKD without meeting criteria for AKI.
In these patients, changes in kidney function and structure are not of sufficient magnitude or
identified rapidly enough to meet AKI criteria yet have been shown to be prognostically
important.

D

A cohort study of 1.1 million adult Canadian residents concluded that AKD without
AKI was 3 times more common than AKI, and was more often identified on outpatient rather
than inpatient SCr and albuminuria testing.61 AKD without AKI was significantly associated
with an increased risk for mortality and RRT requirement in comparison with no kidney
disease. However, the multivariable-adjusted hazard ratio (HR) of mortality for AKD without
AKI was lower than for AKI. AKD without AKI was also associated with a higher risk of
new onset CKD than AKI among patients without preexisting CKD. In a cohort of over 5900
critically ill pediatric patients with preserved baseline kidney function, AKD without AKI
was independently associated with a >7-fold increased odds of MAKE at 90 days (MAKE90),
including persistent abnormal kidney function, dialysis, or death, compared to those with no
AKD (adjusted OR: 7.83; 95% CI: 4.54–13.51).62

56

AKD without AKI is often seen in community or primary care settings and has been
described in populations with liver cirrhosis,63, 64 acute decompensated heart failure,65 and
among patients who received a kidney biopsy, including those with biopsy-proven crescentic
glomerulonephritis.66 AKD without AKI likely identifies patients with a distinct distribution
of etiologies of kidney disease and concomitant risk of adverse outcomes, which may warrant
different clinical management strategies to those with AKD following AKI.
1.4. AKI and AKD resolution
Practice Point 1.4.1: Complete and partial resolution of AKI and AKD are defined by
changes in SCr, serum cystatin C, or estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR) as
shown in Table 10.
Table 10 | Criteria for resolution of AKI and AKD
Complete resolution
AKI
SCr or serum cystatin C <1.2 times higher
than baseline within 7 days
AKD SCr or serum cystatin C <1.2 times higher
than baseline,
Or
eGFR >80% of baseline within 3 months

AF
T

Partial resolution
SCr or serum cystatin C ≥1.2 to <1.5 times
higher than baseline within 7 days
SCr or serum cystatin C ≥1.2 to <1.5 times
higher than baseline,
Or
eGFR <80% to >66% of baseline within 3
months
AKD, acute kidney disease; AKI, acute kidney injury; eGFR, estimated glomerular filtration rate;
SCr, serum creatinine

D

R

Although many studies have evaluated the relationships between recovery of kidney
function based on SCr values and patient outcomes, inconsistent definitions have been used
for resolution of AKI and AKD67 and considerable variation of the criteria is applied in the
published literature.38, 51, 52, 54, 56, 57, 68, 69 A standardized definition in children and adults is
important for both clinical practice and research. Firstly, a lack of a standard definition
results in variations of interpretation by healthcare professionals which leads to inconsistent
decision-making around different aspects of clinical care such as follow-up planning and
resource allocation. Secondly, identifying partial resolution can allow for earlier
interventions with the potential to mitigate the AKI to CKD transition and thus improve
kidney outcomes. Thirdly, a standardized definition would allow for comparison across
studies and is vital for clinical trial design as well as for validation of biomarkers that predict
resolution versus nonresolution.
The Work Group judged that a return of SCr to <1.2 times higher than baseline
indicates return of GFR to baseline for most patients, allowing for non-clinically significant
fluctuations in SCr. The prognostic relevance of this definition is supported by several
studies. Pannu et al. examined different percentage changes in SCr from baseline to define
recovery of kidney function in a population-based cohort in Alberta, Canada.54 There was a
significantly higher risk for mortality and adverse kidney outcomes among patients with AKI
and a SCr that remained 25% above baseline at 90 days after AKI onset. In a population57

based cohort from Scotland, Sawhney et al. defined complete resolution of AKI by the last
SCr <1.2 times higher than baseline SCr and partial resolution of AKI as the last SCr ≥1.2 to
<1.5 times higher than baseline within 7 of AKI onset. The percentage of patients without
subsequent kidney recovery by 90 days was 3.5% among those with complete resolution,
10.4% among those with partial resolution, and 30.1% without partial or complete
resolution.69

AF
T

By definition, patients with AKI who do not recover kidney function by 7 days are
deemed to have AKD which can last for up to 3 months. Resolution of AKD should be
determined before 3 months since closer clinical assessment to identify and address factors
that may cause ongoing kidney injury and mediate AKD to CKD transition may be
warranted. Beyond 3 months, patients without resolution of AKD meet the criteria for CKD
and should be described as having CKD and a history of AKD (Section 6.3). Large
population-based studies have examined the prognosis of AKD according to SCr recovery
status within 3 months of AKI onset.38, 68 These studies have demonstrated that people with
AKD are at increased risk for development of new onset CKD as well as progression of
preexisting CKD.38, 61 People not meeting criteria for CKD after the period of AKD should
be described as having a history of AKD and can be further distinguished as having partial or
complete resolution of AKD, to further identify their increased risk for new onset or
progression of CKD.
1.5. Recurrent AKI
Practice Point 1.5.1: Recurrent AKI is defined as a new episode of AKI occurring after
partial or complete resolution from a previous episode of AKI.

D

R

Recurrent AKI is associated with adverse outcomes.46, 50, 55 However, there is
considerable variation in how recurrent AKI has been defined previously. Liu et al. defined
recurrent AKI in adults as being rehospitalized with a SCr level increase by ≥0.3 mg/dl
within 48 hours of readmission or ≥50% above a “baseline” SCr level.55 Walker et al.
defined recurrent AKI as meeting the biochemical definition of AKI within the observation
period of 6 years50 and Altawalbeh et al., within 5 years.46
In the Assessment of Worldwide Acute Kidney injury Epidemiology in Neonates
(AWAKEN) cohort study of 2162 critically ill neonates, recurrent AKI was defined as a new
AKI episode occurring only after SCr had returned to the prior baseline.36 Among neonates
with AKI, 22% experienced recurrent AKI. These infants had significantly longer hospital
stays than those with a single episode of AKI (median 60 vs. 18 days). Recurrent AKI was
also independently associated with reduced likelihood of discharge (adjusted HR: 0.7; 95%
CI: 0.6–0.9). Similarly, in a retrospective cohort of preterm neonates ≤28 weeks gestation,
Adegboyega et al. found that 24% developed recurrent AKI.47 Early-onset and severe initial
AKI were associated with increased risk for recurrent AKI, which in turn was linked with a

58

significantly longer hospital stay (+36 days) and increased mortality (adjusted OR: 4.55; 95%
CI: 1.12–18.51) compared to a single episode of AKI.
The Work Group deemed that the definition of recurrent AKI be consistent with the
proposed resolution definition (Table 10), so that each episode of AKI is identified as a
separate event, based on a new baseline SCr or cystatin C where warranted, without imposing
a timeframe between a first and subsequent AKI episode. A standardized definition will
allow for comparison of predictors, incidence, and prognosis across studies, and could aid in
distinguishing patients with new episodes warranting assessment from those with sequelae of
AKD or CKD.

AF
T

1.6. Considerations specific to children
1.6.1. Definition of AKI
Practice Point 1.6.1.1: Age specific references ranges should be used when serum
cystatin C is used to detect AKI in children.

R

In children, like adults, AKI is defined by functional and structural criteria (Table 1).
Serum cystatin C as an alternative marker of kidney function that has robust evidence of
performance in children across diverse clinical settings, including critical illness, septic
shock, cardiac surgery, and liver disease with strong diagnostic performance for early AKI
detection. However, cystatin C levels are influenced by age, with known elevations during
infancy and changes in adolescence due to developmental changes. Therefore, age-specific
reference ranges are essential for accurate interpretation, particularly when using cystatin C
to detect AKI in neonates. Serum cystatin C levels vary significantly during puberty: median
concentrations increase in boys (to approximately 0.98 mg/l) and decrease in girls (to around
0.86 mg/l) between ages 11 and 14. These changes strongly correlate with Tanner stage
(β = +0.105 mg/l per stage in males and β = –0.093 mg/l in females, both p < 0.01).70

D

1.6.2. Determination of baseline kidney function
Practice Point 1.6.2.1: In children (except neonates), select the baseline SCr or serum
cystatin C for an AKI episode using a hierarchical approach (Figure 3).

59

AF
T

Figure 3 | Hierarchical approach to the selection of the baseline SCr value used to
characterize an AKI event in children*. *A representative outpatient SCr should ideally
reflect the mean, median, or most recent value within 3 months of admission or acute event.
AKI, acute kidney injury; Cys C, cystatin C; eGFR, estimated glomerular filtration rate;
RRT, renal replacement therapy; SCr, serum creatinine

R

In children, defining baseline SCr for AKI diagnosis requires an approach that
prioritizes outpatient values obtained within the past 3 months, recognizing that kidney
function changes more dynamically in children than in adults.71 However, access to
outpatient SCr values may not be available in some settings. Wherever possible, systems
should ideally be integrated or if this is not possible, outpatient values should be accessible to
clinicians in secondary care settings. Education of healthcare professionals is required to
ensure optimum implementation of this recommendation.

D

When such values are unavailable or not appropriate, it is reasonable to use equations
such as the age-based creatinine and age-based creatinine with recovery equations, which
were derived from a large cohort of hospitalized children without known CKD (Table 11).72,
73 However, they have important limitations.
When no prior values are available, using the admission SCr may be acceptable with
caution, although this approach risks underrecognizing AKI in children who present with
early kidney injury.

AKI, acute kidney injury; CKD, chronic kidney disease; eGFR, estimated glomerular filtration rate; GFR, glomerular filtration rate; SCr, serum

61

Practice Point 1.6.2.2: In children, specific considerations related to age are needed
when determining a baseline SCr.
In pediatric studies, eGFR values are typically reported within the range of 100–120
ml/min per 1.73 m² and not all equations to determine baseline SCr consider patient age
(Table 10). For instance, back-calculating baseline SCr using a fixed eGFR of 100–120
ml/min per 1.73 m2 with the Schwartz equation has been shown to be particularly inaccurate
in children under 2 years of age, where kidney function is physiologically lower and more
variable. 74 Although using an assumed eGFR of 120 ml/min per 1.73 m² tends to produce
lower bias with about 25% absolute error, opting for 100 ml/min per 1.73 m² may provide a
more conservative estimate to minimize the risk of overestimating AKI.75

AF
T

In younger children, the use of age-based normative GFR values substantially
improves the accuracy and agreement of the estimated baseline SCr. Height-dependent
equations may not be feasible in hospitalized children because height is often missing or
unreliable in health records during acute illnesses.
Differences in methods for establishing baseline kidney function can affect whether
patients meet AKI criteria leading to both over- and underdiagnosis. Underdiagnosis of AKI
can lead to delayed recognition thereby delaying investigations and treatment. Overdiagnosis
of AKI in people with CKD may expose patients to unwarranted tests and interventions. In
addition, use of varying methods in epidemiological studies impacts the comparability of
findings, limiting interpretation of consistency of findings across different geographic
regions and clinical settings.

D

R

1.6.3. Criteria for neonatal AKI
Practice Point 1.6.3.1: The criteria for neonatal AKI include a lack of decline in SCr
within the first 7 days of birth, in addition to neonatal specific increases in SCr or
cystatin C levels, or reduced urine output. (Table 12).
Table 12 | Criteria for AKI in neonates (any one or more of the following within the
corresponding time period)
Neonatal AKI criteria
• No decline in SCr from birth to 7 days of life
• Increase in SCr by ≥0.3 mg/dl (≥26.5 µmol/l) within 48 hours
• Increase in SCr by ≥1.5 times baseline that is known or presumed to have
occurred within the prior 7 days
• Increase in serum cystatin C by 1.25 times baseline that is known or
presumed to have occurred within the prior 7 days
• Serum cystatin C >2.2 mg/dl
• Urine output of less than 1.0 ml/kg/h for ≥24 hours
AKI, acute kidney injury; SCr, serum creatinine

62

The definition of AKI in neonates requires modifications to the criteria for children
and adults in order to ensure accurate identification. Neonatal GFR is low at birth due to
immature kidney function, low kidney blood flow, and incomplete nephrogenesis,
particularly in preterm neonates.76-78 At birth, neonatal SCr primarily reflects maternal
creatinine levels. Over the first approximately 7 postnatal days, neonates typically experience
a physiologic decline in SCr, reflecting the transition from placental clearance to endogenous
kidney function. A failure to decrease SCr during this time suggests the presence of AKI. 79, 80
The rate of this decline is gestational age-dependent, with more immature neonates
demonstrating a slower decrease in SCr levels.81, 82

AF
T

The 0.3 mg/dl increase within 48 hours and 1.5 times increase of SCr from baseline
within 7 days criteria for neonatal AKI originate from the KDIGO 2012 AKI guideline. 12
Since then, numerous studies have demonstrated that these thresholds correlate with
increased mortality and morbidity in neonates.83-93 Notably, the neonatal KDIGO AKI
definition uses the lowest prior SCr value as the baseline for comparison with subsequent
SCr measurements.

R

Urine output plays a critical role in neonatal AKI identification and should be adapted
to neonatal physiology. Technical challenges in neonatal urine output monitoring include the
difficulty in obtaining accurate measurements due to the reliance on diaper weights, which
can be imprecise due to mixing with stool and unmeasured voids. Indwelling catheters pose
risks of infection and may not be feasible due to their small size. Neonates can experience
transient oliguria followed by diuresis in the first few days of life before urine output
stabilizes, making strict urine output thresholds difficult to apply. Given the dynamic nature
of neonatal urine output, AKI definitions should incorporate adjusted urine output thresholds
and require continuous 24-hour monitoring to account for fluctuations beyond the first 24
hours of life.

D

Investigators have explored refining AKI definitions based on chronological and
gestational age, as AKI risk differs between extremely preterm neonates (<29 weeks
gestational age) and more mature neonates.94, 95 However, there is currently insufficient data
to formally recommend gestational age-specific AKI definitions. Additionally, biomarkers
such as urine NGAL and serum cystatin C offer potential improvements in early AKI
detection. Urinary NGAL can serve as a noninvasive biomarker of tubular injury, though
gestational age-specific reference values are still needed.96, 97 Serum cystatin C has emerged
as a promising biomarker for neonatal AKI, offering potential advantages over creatininebased definitions. A large cohort study including over 50,000 neonates found that defining
neonatal AKI as cystatin C ≥2.2 mg/l or an increase of ≥25% was 6.5 times more sensitive in
predicting in-hospital mortality than SCr-based definitions.98
Training neonatal teams on AKI recognition can enhance early detection and
intervention. While access to advanced biomarkers may be constrained in resource-limited
63

settings, innovative and low-cost strategies, such as saliva-based blood urea nitrogen (BUN)
testing, are being explored as noninvasive alternatives for estimating kidney function. 99
These approaches may provide pragmatic solutions for monitoring high-risk neonates when
traditional serum testing is unavailable or impractical.
1.6.4. AKI staging in neonates
Practice Point 1.6.4.1: In neonates, the severity of AKI is staged according to individual
components or combinations of the following SCr and urine output criteria (Table 13).

C1-C3 indicates AKI severity stage according to serum creatinine (SCr) criteria, U1-U3 indicates
AKI severity stage according to urine output criteria, B-/B+ indicates staging by absence or presence
of an elevated damage biomarker. AKI, acute kidney injury; RRT, renal replacement therapy

Use of neonatal-specific thresholds for AKI staging enables more appropriate risk
stratification for morbidity and mortality that can inform clinical management and follow-up.
Large absolute changes in SCr are uncommon in neonates due to low muscle mass and
physiologically low baseline GFR. The SCr criteria for Stage 3 AKI are reduced to 2.5 mg/dl
in neonates, which represents a GFR of <10 ml/min per 1.73 m2. However, it is important to
recognize that even this reduced threshold may still be too high, particularly in preterm
neonates with inherently lower baseline SCr values due to smaller muscle mass. In these
populations, a SCr level of 2.5 mg/dl may reflect profound abnormal kidney function. Further
study is warranted to refine this cutoff in preterm infants and better align AKI staging with
meaningful clinical outcomes in this vulnerable group. In resource-rich settings, the inclusion
of additional damage biomarkers such as NGAL and filtration markers such as serum
cystatin C may enhance AKI severity staging.
64

D

R

AF
T

The use of neonatal-specific criteria introduces additional complexity and may be
influenced by delayed creatinine kinetics or transient physiologic oliguria in neonates. There
is a small risk of overestimating the severity of AKI using these criteria, particularly in
preterm infants or those with non-kidney comorbidities that influence fluid status and
creatinine handling. This may lead to a greater utilization of healthcare resources, including
biomarker testing and nephrology consultations. These extra demands on resources are
expected to be balanced by earlier recognition and timely treatment due to the high risk of
morbidity and mortality associated with AKI severity in neonates. The potential for timely
intervention and prevention of progression to kidney failure or death outweighs the risks of
overdiagnosis and resource use in most clinical contexts.

65

CHAPTER 2. AKI PREDICTION, RISK STRATIFICATION,
AND DIAGNOSTIC EVALUATION

D

R

AF
T

The risk of AKI is altered by the presence of factors that increase susceptibility to
AKI in combination with exposures that cause AKI. There are several features that determine
a person’s susceptibility to AKI including demographic characteristics, genetics, social and
environmental determinants, as well as comorbidities (acute or chronic conditions).
Preexisting CKD and previous episodes of AKI represent major susceptibilities. Common
exposures include conditions that alter fluid balance or hemodynamic status, infections, and
nephrotoxic medications, which are more likely to lead to AKI in susceptible individuals.
The interplay between susceptibilities and exposures is incompletely understood but it is
generally recognized that a person’s degree of susceptibility and intensity of exposures
together increase their risk of developing AKI (Figure 4).

Figure 4 | AKI exposures and susceptibilities. AKI, acute kidney injury; CKD, chronic
kidney disease; ECMO, extracorporeal membrane oxygenation
Recognition of a person’s risk factors for developing AKI and associated adverse
outcomes can be useful to target prevention and management strategies to people at risk.
However, the risk of AKI changes in an individual person over time. Although it can be
estimated at a given moment in time (e.g., at a preoperative clinic assessment or upon ICU
admission), it is important to recognize that a person’s risk is dynamic over the course of
illness. Further, some exposures may be modifiable, and addressing these can reduce the risk
of AKI. Susceptibilities and exposures for AKI can differ between community and hospital
settings (Table 14). While there has been increasing knowledge of the epidemiology and
outcomes of community-acquired AKI detected in hospital as well as community-acquired
66

AKI detected in primary care, most current knowledge has been obtained from research on
people in hospitals. Importantly, susceptibilities and exposures may interact in non-linear
ways, such that the same exposure can confer markedly different AKI risk depending on
baseline kidney function, frailty, comorbidity burden, and acute illness severity.

R

D

Figure 5 illustrates a risk stratification framework that integrates patient
susceptibilities and clinical exposures, which can be achieved by integrating a variety of
clinical tools to estimate a person’s likelihood of developing AKI (or related outcome). This
framework uses a tiered approach, beginning with identification of low-, moderate-, and
high-risk people using tools such as clinical scoring systems, biomarkers, or predictive
models that have been developed and validated in the specific clinical populations and
settings where they are being applied. For people classified as low risk, regular standards of
care may be appropriate whereas people at moderate or high risk may benefit from enhanced
monitoring, additional risk assessment, or implementation of prevention or management
strategies for AKI. The availability and feasibility of existing risk stratification tools varies
across settings, and the choice of a particular tool will depend on local availability, cost, and
expertise. Further, selection of an appropriate tool is context-specific and will vary by
population and healthcare setting. A personalized approach that applies the right tool for the
67

AF
T

right patient, at the right time in their care is necessary to optimize early recognition of risk,
appropriate interventions, and plan subsequent management, with the objective of reducing
the incidence and/or severity of AKI and its associated adverse outcomes.

R

Figure 5 | Conceptual framework for AKI risk stratification. This figure illustrates a
framework for classifying people by AKI risk across a continuum of clinical severity,
integrating susceptibilities (e.g., age, comorbidities) and exposures (e.g., nephrotoxins,
surgery, sepsis) (Figure 4) with clinical scoring systems, risk models, or biomarker data.
Using these tools, people can be stratified into low, moderate, or high risk of AKI or related
outcome to inform management. This approach supports proactive personalized AKI
prevention, early recognition, and individualized management. AI, artificial intelligence

D

2.1. Risk prediction models
Recommendation 2.1.1: We suggest using externally validated clinical risk models to
identify children and adults at increased risk of AKI and guide the use of kidneyprotective strategies (2C).
This recommendation emphasizes the use of externally validated clinical risk models for
early identification of children and adults at increased risk of AKI. Such models integrate
multiple patient-level and contextual risk factors and have demonstrated significant
discrimination and calibration for predicting AKI, supporting more consistent and objective
risk stratification. Applying these tools in susceptible populations may enable more
appropriate and efficient care through timely implementation of kidney-protective strategies
to people at higher risk. This approach can also avoid unnecessary testing and intervention
in people who are unlikely to benefit. Overall, this approach supports proactive, evidenceinformed personalized care aimed at reducing AKI incidence and associated morbidity.
68

AF
T

Key information
Balance of benefits and harms
The primary benefit of using validated risk models is improved identification of
children and adults at increased risk for AKI, enabling earlier and more targeted
implementation of kidney-protective strategies in people at high risk, which may reduce AKI
incidence, severity, and downstream complications. These models can standardize risk
assessment, reduce reliance on subjective clinical judgment, and support more efficient use
of preventive interventions in susceptible populations. Potential harms include
overestimation of risk in some individuals, which could lead to unnecessary monitoring,
interventions, or avoidance of beneficial therapies for underlying conditions. There is also a
risk that poor integration of models into clinical systems can contribute to workflow burden.
Embedding prediction models as electronic health record (EHR) alerts without linked action
pathways may increase alert fatigue and reduce the effectiveness of decision support
interventions. Overall, the anticipated benefits of more accurate risk stratification and
targeted prevention are judged to outweigh the generally low and manageable potential
harms when validated models are applied thoughtfully.

D

R

A systematic review and meta-analysis of 95 studies evaluating 302 externally
validated AKI prediction models used in various populations at risk for AKI found
substantial heterogeneity in model performance, though models generally demonstrated good
discrimination upon external validation for prediction of AKI (overall pooled area under
curve [AUC] 0.78, range 0.75–0.84 across hospitalization, ICU, post-contrast, and
postoperative settings (Supplementary Table S16).100 The cumulative evidence suggests most
externally validated models perform well for predicting AKI onset, AKI severity, and postAKI complications in hospitalized adults, supporting their use in care pathways to identify
high-risk surgical and critically ill populations. These models enable early identification of
people at elevated AKI risk but may also facilitate the targeted application of kidneyprotective strategies. However, there is a high degree of inconsistency across studies
regarding clinical settings, patient populations and predictive performance. Furthermore,
models used different criteria for AKI. The predictive performance of models for the revised
KDIGO AKI criteria is unknown.
Certainty of evidence
There is moderate certainty of evidence that the Mehta score, when used in adults
undergoing cardiac surgery (the population of most studies), has low-to-moderate accuracy to
predict AKI outcomes (specifically, low accuracy to predict incident AKI and moderate
accuracy to predict Stage 3 AKI and RRT).101-106 The level of accuracy was a priori defined
as low for area under the curve (AUC) of 0.51–0.75 and moderate for AUC of 0.76–0.85.
The certainty of evidence for predicting AKI was low, downgraded for methodological issues
69

with the studies and inconsistency across the studies, but moderate for predicting stage 3 AKI
and RRT, which were downgraded for methodological issues but had consistent findings
across studies. Without evidence from trials analyzing clinical impacts, the overall certainty
of evidence for implementing the Mehta score was downgraded, for indirectness, to low.
There is high certainty of evidence that the Simple Postoperative AKI risk (SPARK)
score, when used in adults undergoing noncardiac surgery, has low accuracy (as defined in
the prior paragraph) to predict AKI outcomes.107-109 The certainty of evidence was high for
prediction of incident AKI but moderate for Stage 3 AKI, downgraded for imprecision of the
summary estimate. Without trial evidence, the overall certainty of evidence for implementing
the SPARK score was downgraded, for indirectness, to moderate.

R

AF
T

There is low certainty of evidence that prediction tools, in general, have (overall)
moderate accuracy (as defined above) to predict AKI outcomes in both children and adults.
This conclusion applies to prediction of incident AKI in various settings in children and
adults and also other AKI-related outcomes and mortality in adults. The certainty of evidence
was downgraded for methodological concerns of the studies, a high degree of inconsistency
across studies, related to both statistical and clinical heterogeneity of studies conducted in
different populations, settings, and with very different prediction models,100 in addition to
indirectness since the analyses do not provide evidence for any specific score. Since this
analysis pertains to diagnostic accuracy and not the effect on clinical outcomes of
implementing prediction tools, the overall certainty of evidence was downgraded, for
indirectness, to very low for both children and adults.

D

Values and preferences
Identification of people at high risk of developing any form of AKI or progression to
severe AKI or needing RRT is viewed positively by well-informed patients, in particular if
the predictive models are based on routinely available data, do not require any additional
investigations, are sufficiently accurate, and offer the prospect of potential preventive
interventions to avoid AKI and subsequent adverse outcomes in vulnerable people.
Resource use and costs
Utilizing and applying most validated AKI risk models generally does not require
significant additional direct costs for the patient or institution, as they primarily rely on
healthcare professionals executing models using existing clinical data entered into an integer
scoring system or computer-based calculator. The primary expense with systematic
implementation of predictive models is the upfront and ongoing cost of integrating and
updating (when necessary) the risk calculation within the existing information technology
infrastructure of a hospital, such as an EHR. Further costs should be incurred to ensure the
model continues to accurately calculate risk while other costs may arise from additional
70

testing or treatments being applied to high-risk people. These potential harms may also be
increased by the degree a model falsely classifies people as being at high risk for AKI, which
could result in more unnecessary interventions and increased costs. However, overall, the
primary economic argument for integrating AKI risk models into care is that they may lead
to more targeted prevention efforts for AKI and related complications in people who are
more likely to benefit, reducing the need for scarce or expensive downstream resources for
care (e.g., ICU admission, RRT).110

AF
T

Considerations for implementation
Successful implementation of AKI risk models requires initial investment and
attention to specific clinical, technical, and institutional factors that impact model
performance, uptake, and outcomes. Predictive risk models are only valuable if they are
based on accurate and reliably collected predictor variables and delivered in a manner that
supports clinical judgement. Implementation will achieve greater value when risk
stratification is linked to specific, evidence-based interventions that can improve patient care
and outcomes.

D

R

Rationale
Risk prediction models combine multiple patient-, procedure-, and/or care-related
factors into a structured output on the probability of a future event for an individual person,
which facilitates risk stratification and can guide the timely deployment of kidney-protective
strategies in susceptible populations. Different risk models have been developed to predict
AKI within specified time horizons and using variables ascertained as specific time points;
for instance; for adults undergoing cardiac surgery (e.g., Mehta score; Supplementary Table
S17) or major non-cardiac surgery (e.g., SPARK score; Supplementary Table S18), for
people in the hospital, for people in the ICU,100, 111, 112 and people with sepsis.113, 114 In
addition, risk prediction models have been developed and validated specifically for pediatric
populations across different clinical settings, including hospitalized children,115, 116 children
in the pediatric intensive care unit (PICU),117, 118 pediatric cardiac surgery patients,119 and
children with septic shock120 (Recommendation 2.6.1; Supplementary Table S19). Model
performance can vary depending on the specific patient population and features of the model.
Significant heterogeneity in discrimination has been identified between externally validated
models for AKI,100 necessitating careful selection of models shown to have adequate
performance and clinical utility in the population and clinical setting they will be used.
Most AKI risk models assess risk at a single time point, such as pre-surgery or upon
ICU admission. Some newer models are dynamic, updating scores with data collected
throughout the hospital stay. Examples include models adding intra- and postoperative data
to improve the ability to predict postoperative AKI.121, 122 Similarly, other studies have
demonstrated that risk assessment improves with adding variables from day 1 or day 2 of a
71

non-postoperative ICU stay.111, 123 Thus, dynamic prediction of risk of AKI over time may
provide insights into which patients warrant greater focus on preventive interventions over
the course of an illness trajectory.
Recommendation 2.1.2: We recommend using externally validated clinical risk models
to identify adults at risk of receiving renal replacement therapy (RRT) for AKI to
inform patient choices and shared decision making about their care (1C).

AF
T

This recommendation places a high value on ensuring patients are provided with sufficient
and accurate information on risks to inform their treatment decisions and that their values,
preferences, and care choices are respected. The recommendations places a lower value on
the harm, potential use of excess resources, and inconvenience that could result from
clinically important overestimates of risk in a person who ultimately does not require support
with RRT.

D

R

Key information
Balance of benefits and harms
Valid risk prediction models can help identify adults at higher risk of requiring RRT
for AKI which can support timely, informed discussions about prognosis, treatment options,
and care planning. These models can enhance shared decision-making by providing
individualized risk estimates that help align clinical decisions with patient values and
preferences. However, all existing prediction models were developed prior to landmark RRT
timing trials.124-128 A potential harm exists if the data supporting the model or data on the
initiation of RRT changes and it increases the potential harm of incorrectly starting (or not
starting) RRT (Chapter 5). Potential harms include misclassification of risk which may cause
unnecessary anxiety, premature decision-making, or inappropriate preparation for RRT in
some patients. Overreliance on model outputs could also diminish clinical judgment or lead
to inequities if models are applied outside the populations in which they were validated.
Overall, when used as adjuncts to clinical assessment, the benefits of improved prognostic
clarity and patient-centered decision-making outweigh the relatively low risk of harm.
The Mehta score has been shown to predict need for RRT in adults undergoing
cardiac surgery. Evidence review of data from 3 trials concluded that the predictive accuracy
of the Mehta score was moderate with an AUC of 0.78 (0.69–0.87; Supplementary Table
S17). There was no data on the accuracy of the SPARK score in adults undergoing major
non-cardiac surgery.
Thakar et al. derived a clinical risk score in 33,217 participants undergoing cardiac
surgery, reporting C-statistics of approximately 0.80 for postoperative AKI and 0.95 for
dialysis-requiring AKI.129 Similarly, Wijeysundera et al. developed and externally validated
a simplified predictive index for RRT in a cohort of 928 participants, achieving AUCs of
72

0.93–0.95.130 Together, these large, diverse. inpatient cohorts, leveraging detailed
perioperative and clinical variables, demonstrate that models built on real-world data can
provide reasonably accurate estimates for predicting the need for RRT in AKI.
Zuo et al. validated a model for RRT requirement specifically for people with acute
pancreatitis with excellent discrimination, calibration, and clinical utility, emphasizing that
additional risk stratification for RRT for AKI can be achieved for disease-specific
situations.131 Models such as AKIpredictor111 and those by Malhotra et al.112 provide broader
ICU applicability and can be incorporated into electronic systems to aid identification of
people at high risk.

AF
T

Certainty of evidence
There is moderate certainty of evidence that the Mehta score has moderate accuracy
(defined as AUC between 0.76 and 0.85) to predict RRT in adults undergoing cardiac surgery
(the population of most studies).102-104 The certainty of evidence was downgraded for some
methodological limitations of the studies. Without trial evidence, the overall certainty of
evidence for implementing the Mehta score was downgraded, for indirectness, to low.
Eligible studies conducted in children did not report RRT, nor did they evaluate the SPARK
score to predict RRT specifically.

D

R

There is low certainty of evidence that prediction tools, in general, have (overall)
moderate accuracy to predict RRT in adults. The certainty of evidence was downgraded for
methodological concerns of the studies and a high degree of inconsistency across studies,
related to both statistical and clinical heterogeneity of studies conducted in different
populations, settings, and with very different prediction models,100 in addition to indirectness
since the analyses do not provide evidence for any specific score. Without trial evidence, the
overall certainty of evidence for implementing the Mehta score was downgraded, for
indirectness, to low. Eligible studies conducted in children did not report RRT.
Values and preferences
The initiation of RRT represents an escalation in medical care requirements,
accompanied by increased utilization of healthcare resources, increased workload for
healthcare professionals, prolonged hospitalization, and elevated healthcare expenditures.
Receipt of RRT significantly impacts patient experience and burden of care, and most people
want to avoid RRT, and if required, may benefit from being made aware and prepared to
receive it. Identification of people at high risk of needing RRT is likely to be viewed
positively by well-informed patients, in particular since it offers the prospect of potential
preventive interventions, patient-centered counselling, earlier nephrology consultation, and
timely preparation of RRT, including potential transfer to appropriate clinical settings such as
ICU or a site with dialysis services.
73

AF
T

Resource use and costs
Use of validated risk models generally requires modest resources, as most models
rely on routinely collected clinical and laboratory data already available in electronic health
records. Implementation costs may include integration into clinical workflows or electronic
systems, healthcare professional training, and periodic updates. These upfront investments
are likely to be offset by more efficient use of resources through improved targeting of
education, planning, and preparatory interventions for people at higher risk of RRT. As
noted, a potential harm exists if the data supporting the model or evidence on the timing of
RRT changes and the potential harm of incorrectly starting (or not starting) RRT increases
(Chapter 5). Further, falsely classifying people as high risk for RRT can result from
additional interventions and unnecessary transfer to an ICU or dialysis center. Earlier and
better-informed shared decision-making may also reduce potentially avoidable or unwanted
interventions and improve alignment of care with patient preferences. Overall, the resource
requirements are considered reasonable relative to the potential benefits in care planning
efficiency and patient-centered outcomes.

D

R

Considerations for implementation
Implementation of this recommendation requires selection of validated risk models
for RRT that are appropriate for the local patient population and clinical setting. This is
particularly important in rural and low-resource settings, as clinical practice patterns, access,
and resources for RRT differ across jurisdictions necessitating steps to determine whether a
model developed to predict RRT elsewhere (often a high-resource setting) provides accurate
information in another setting.129, 132-138 Integration of models into existing clinical
workflows and EHRs is important to ensure timely access to risk estimates without adding
undue burden to healthcare professionals. Healthcare professionals should receive training on
model interpretation, limitations, and how to communicate risk clearly and sensitively to
support shared decision-making. Risk models should be used as adjuncts to, rather than
replacements for, clinical judgment and individualized patient assessment. Ongoing
evaluation of model performance, equity, and patient understanding is important to ensure
appropriate and effective use in practice.
Rationale
This recommendation is based on evidence that several externally validated risk
models for AKI requiring RRT have shown very good calibration and discrimination,129, 130,
138, 139 with overall pooled AUROC of 0.82 (95% CI: 0.79–0.85; Supplementary Tables S16),
and with some individual external validation studies exhibiting AUROCs as high as 0.95.
This has most consistently been demonstrated for models predicting postoperative RRT for
AKI. Large inpatient cohorts have demonstrated that models based on routinely collected
clinical and laboratory variables can perform well for preoperative prediction of the need for
74

RRT for AKI following cardiac and major non-cardiac surgery.122, 129, 140 Further, some
research suggests these models can more accurately and consistently estimate a person’s risk
of progression to RRT than clinical judgment alone.141 Reliable risk stratification supports
timely, transparent discussions about prognosis, treatment options, and future care planning,
which are central to shared decision-making. Providing patients with individualized,
evidence-based risk information helps them to better understand potential trajectories and
align decisions with their values and preferences. External validation increases confidence
that these models can maintain performance in other populations and settings, although this
should be tested in a new (external) population where a model for RRT that was derived
elsewhere will be implemented. Overall, use of validated risk models promotes patientcentered, informed, and equitable decision-making around RRT for AKI.

AF
T

Practice Point 2.1.1: In community and low-resource settings, simplified AKI risk
scores can support early identification and management of adults at risk for
community-acquired AKI.

R

In low resource, community-based settings, the ISN 0by25 multicenter feasibility
study demonstrated that a simple symptom‐based risk score (including vomiting, low oral
intake, weakness, oliguria, hypotension, loss of appetite and swelling; Table 15) could be
used to guide point‐of‐care (POC) SCr and urine dipstick testing, resulting in improved early
AKI recognition and management. Across Bolivia, Nepal, and Malawi, 2101 people at
moderate-to-high risk were identified using this score. After implementation of the POC
driven protocol, more people with AKI were recognized (34.6% vs. 24.5%, p<0.0001).142
This was associated with a reduction in hospital admissions from 59% to 49% (p <0.001).

D

a
In the 0by25 feasibility study, among enrolled participants screened as moderate-to-high risk (score
≥3), the observed 7-day AKI risk was ~30% (severe AKI ~18%). Risk varied by kidney disease status
at enrollment: No kidney disease 13.9% (98/705); acute kidney disease 36.6% (439/1199); and
chronic kidney disease 46.2% (91/197). HIV, human immunodeficiency virus; HAART, highly active
antiretroviral therapy
75

These results support the practical application of simple risk tools in settings where
conventional laboratory infrastructure is limited. By enabling frontline healthcare
professionals to determine when to obtain SCr measurements, AKI was diagnosed and could
be managed earlier.142 Building on this model, the International Society of Nephrology (ISN)
Kidney Care Network (KCN) applied this protocol across 4311 participants in Bolivia,
Brazil, South Africa, and Nepal. AKI was substantially more common in those identified via
the modified score (80.2%) than by clinical judgment alone (32.5%).135, 143

AF
T

Together, these studies support clinical utility and scalability of simplified risk-based
approaches for AKI screening in the community in low-resource settings. Incorporating such
tools into primary healthcare workflows could enhance the timeliness and equity of AKI
diagnosis, especially in under-served and high-risk communities.
Practice Point 2.1.2: The performance of electronic health record (EHR)-based AKI
risk models should be tested in the settings they will be deployed. Harmonizing
terminology and data structure within EHR systems and reassessment of performance
over time is needed to ensure predictive accuracy in a local setting and over time.

D

R

Recent advances in machine learning and augmented intelligence (AI) using EHR
data have expanded the development and testing of predictive models for AKI in acute care
settings.123, 132, 144-149 These techniques can identify key trends in variables collected from
EHRs, such as routinely collected vital signs, lab results, nursing assessments, clinical
conditions, and patient exposures, to predict the development of AKI. A systematic review
and meta-analysis by Cama-Olivares et al. identified 95 machine learning models for AKI
risk assessment, including models based on logistic regression, neural networks, random
forests, and gradient boosting.100 The pooled AUCs of these models for AKI prediction were
0.82 (95% CI: 0.80–0.84) upon internal validation and 0.78 (95% CI: 0.76–0.80) with
external validation, highlighting the deterioration in model performance that can occur when
models are applied in geographically and temporally distinct settings from their development.
Many machine learning and AI risk models display excellent specificity and negative
predictive value. However, these scores have often suffered from low positive predictive
values (<50%) which has, in part, limited wide-scale implementation.123, 132, 148 It is
anticipated that more predictive models for AKI will arise in the future.
The performance of EHR-based AKI risk models depends on the quality, structure,
and context of the data from which they are derived, making rigorous validation essential
prior to clinical use. Local calibration is a critical component of implementation because data
definitions, practice patterns, patient demographics, baseline AKI incidence, laboratory
measurement practices, and thresholds for interventions can vary substantially across
76

institutions, which may affect performance. Even previously externally validated models may
systematically over- or under-estimate AKI risk if these setting-specific factors are not
accounted for. Routine assessment of calibration-in-the-large and across risk strata enables
institutions to update model outputs to ensure predictions remain trustworthy.
Effective implementation also requires harmonization of terminology, definitions, and
data structures within EHR systems. Variability in coding practices, laboratory units,
timestamps, and clinical concepts (e.g., AKI definitions or medication exposure) can impair
model performance and limit their portability to new settings. Standardization using common
data models and interoperable vocabularies facilitates consistent model execution and
interpretation across sites.

D

R

AF
T

Ongoing monitoring and periodic reassessment of model performance are necessary
to maintain predictive accuracy over time. Changes in patient populations, clinical practices,
data capture, and treatment strategies can lead to model drift, reducing reliability if left
unaddressed. Continuous evaluation of discrimination (i.e., updating rankings of those with
and without AKI) and calibration (i.e., aligning the predictions to the observed incidence and
improving this over time), with timely updates when necessary are needed to ensure EHRbased AKI risk models retain accuracy in real-world clinical settings. Figure 6 illustrates a
series of steps before and after implementation of an AKI risk model that can help ensure it
operates more effectively.

77

AF
T
R

D

Figure 6 | Pre- and post-implementation pathways for the development, validation,
implementation, maintenance, and on-going evaluation of a clinical risk model (adapted
from Quickfall et al 2024.150 AKI, acute kidney injury; ICU, intensive care unit; IT,
information technology
2.2. Electronic AKI alerts
Recommendation 2.2.1: We recommend against delivering interruptive, creatininebased electronic alerts for AKI in isolation without systematic strategies for response in
routine clinical care of adults (1B).
This recommendation applies to interruptive electronic alerts triggered by changes in SCr
and implemented without an accompanying, structured response pathway. Electronic alert
systems require significant effort and information technology infrastructure to develop and
maintain. Evidence indicates that alerts may increase AKI recognition and have modest
effects on clinical processes of care but alone do not consistently improve patient outcomes
and may contribute to alert fatigue. If electronic AKI alerts are used, they should be
78

embedded within a comprehensive care strategy that includes clear clinical guidance,
accountability for response, and integration with multidisciplinary workflows aimed at timely
evaluation and management of AKI.
Key information
Balance of benefits and harms
Electronic alerts to identify people with AKI can increase awareness of changes in
kidney function and prompt earlier recognition of AKI. However, in clinical trials where
SCr-based AKI alerts have been evaluated without standardized response protocols, clinical
decision support, or accountability structures, they have not been shown to improve patientcentered outcomes such as mortality, need for RRT, or recovery of kidney function.

AF
T

A meta-analysis of 13 randomized controlled trials (RCTs) comparing AKI alerts
versus no alerts in a total of 29,979 adults at risk of AKI showed no significant differences in
mortality (RR: 0.96; 95% CI: 0.91–1.02), progression to Stage 3 AKI (RR: 1.04; 95% CI:
0.79–1.36), need for RRT (RR: 1.02; 95% CI: 0.92–1.13), or development of MAKE
(adjusted OR: 0.83; 95% CI: 0.67–1.03; Supplementary Table S20). There were also no
differences in duration of AKI or chances of recovery of kidney function.

D

R

Potential harms of AKI include alert fatigue, workflow disruption, inappropriate or
delayed clinical decision-making, overtesting, and therapeutic inertia due to overreliance on
nonspecific SCr changes. Alerts based solely on SCr may also misclassify AKI, particularly
in people with CKD, fluctuating volume status, or following introduction of medications that
reduce GFR, leading to unnecessary interventions or consultations. Interruptive alerts can
impose cognitive burden on healthcare professionals and may divert attention from higherpriority clinical tasks, especially in resource-constrained environments. At a system level,
widespread implementation of isolated alerts consumes informatics and clinical resources
without clear evidence of benefit and may erode healthcare professionals trust in electronic
decision support tools.
Certainty of evidence
There is moderate certainty of evidence that, in adults, delivery of SCr-based
electronic alerts for AKI identification alone, without systematic care strategies, do not
improve AKI-related or other critical patient outcomes.151-156 The certainty of evidence was
downgraded for some methodological limitations.
Values and preferences
This recommendation reflects the value placed on patient-centered care and safety.
Patients are likely to prioritize care models that lead to timely, coordinated, and effective
responses to AKI risk over those that generate alerts without clear benefit, particularly when
79

alerts may contribute to unnecessary testing, treatment, or delays in other aspects of care.
Equity considerations were also important, as poorly designed alert systems may
disproportionately burden settings with limited staffing or informatics support, potentially
widening variation in care quality.

AF
T

Healthcare professionals and healthcare systems generally place greater value on
decision support tools that provide actionable information linked to recommendations that
are integrated into care processes, rather than on early detection alone when such detection
does not reliably translate into improved patient outcomes. Healthcare professionals and
healthcare systems prefer to avoid alert fatigue, workflow disruption, cognitive overload, and
false reassurance or inappropriate escalation, which may undermine electronic decision
support systems.
Resource use and costs
Significant information technology resources are required to develop, implement, and
maintain electronic AKI alerts. These systems may incur substantial costs related to
informatics infrastructure, software development, validation, ongoing maintenance,
healthcare professional training, and governance without corresponding reductions in AKIrelated morbidity, mortality, or resource utilization.

R

In addition to direct implementation costs, interruptive alerts may generate indirect
resource use through increased laboratory testing, consultations, documentation burden, and
workflow inefficiencies triggered by nonactionable or low-specificity alerts. Alert fatigue
may further diminish the efficiency of electronic clinical decision support systems (CDSS),
potentially reducing the effectiveness of other high-value alerts and leading to downstream
costs associated with missed or delayed responses to clinically important events.

D

Considerations for implementation
Implementation of this recommendation requires careful evaluation of existing EHR–
based AKI detection tools and the clinical contexts in which they are deployed. Healthcare
systems should assess whether current SCr-based alerts are linked to clearly defined response
pathways, including standardized evaluation, management recommendations, escalation
criteria, and accountability for follow-up. Where interruptive alerts are already in use without
such supports, deimplementation or modification should be considered.
When electronic AKI detection is pursued, it should be embedded within a broader,
systematic strategy for care. This may include integration with care bundles, clinical decision
support tools, or consultative services, as well as clear delineation of roles and
responsibilities among healthcare professionals. Local resources, staffing, and informatics
capacity should be considered, as effective implementation requires ongoing monitoring of
80

alert performance, healthcare professional engagement, and unintended consequences such as
alert fatigue or inequitable burden across care settings. Education and training of healthcare
professionals are important to ensure appropriate interpretation of AKI detection signals and
alignment with recommended care processes in the settings they are delivered.

AF
T

Rationale
The implementation of alerts is attractive because it theoretically provides a
mechanism for increasing the rapidity of recognition and response to AKI. However, there is
consistent evidence that electronic alerts for AKI based solely on SCr changes do not result
in improvements in patient-centered outcomes in routine clinical care. While such alerts may
increase recognition or documentation of AKI, detection alone does not ensure timely,
appropriate, or effective clinical responses, particularly in the absence of standardized
evaluation and management pathways.
Creatinine-based alerts are limited by delayed SCr kinetics, variable availability of
baseline SCr measurements, and susceptibility to false-positive or clinically inconsequential
triggers, which may reduce specificity and healthcare professional confidence. When
delivered in an interruptive manner, these alerts may contribute to alert fatigue, workflow
disruption, and desensitization to electronic decision support, potentially diminishing the
impact of other high-priority alerts.

R

The Work Group judged that the balance of benefits and harms is unfavorable for
isolated interruptive or non-interruptive alerts. Potential benefits related to earlier AKI
identification are uncertain and inconsistent, whereas potential harms—including inefficient
resource use, unnecessary testing or consultation, and erosion of healthcare professional
trust—are well recognized.

D

This recommendation does not preclude the use of AKI alerts when embedded within
multifaceted intervention strategies that include clear response pathways, education, and
ongoing evaluation.
2.3. Kidney functional testing
Practice Point 2.3.1: Assessment of kidney functional reserve before a potential
exposure may help to identify children and adults with an increased likelihood of
developing AKI.
Kidney functional reserve is the kidney’s capacity to increase GFR in response to
physiological stress.157 It has gained recognition as a measure for identifying people at
increased risk of AKI, even when baseline GFR appears normal. Multiple methods exist
to quantify kidney functional reserve, each with distinct advantages and limitations
81

depending on the clinical setting (Table 16). Oral protein loading is a physiological,
noninvasive approach suitable for stable outpatients or preoperative evaluation; however,
its variability in protein content and delayed GFR response can limit reproducibility.
Creatinine clearance, measured before and after protein loading, has traditionally been
used to detect kidney functional reserve; however, its reliance on accurate urine
collection limits its practicality.

D

R

AF
T

In a study by Husain-Syed et al., adults undergoing elective cardiac surgery
underwent preoperative kidney functional reserve assessment following a standardized
oral protein load.158 Although all participants had a normal resting GFR, those who
developed postoperative AKI were characterized by significantly reduced kidney
functional reserve. A kidney functional reserve ≤15 ml/min per 1.73 m² conferred an
11.8-fold increased risk of AKI, and the test had a high discrimination (AUC: 0.83) that
was greater than observed for traditional kidney function markers.158 In a prospective
study of hematopoietic stem cell transplant recipients with normal pretransplant GFR,
Mancianti et al. demonstrated that lower kidney functional reserve was associated not
only with a higher risk of AKI but also with a reduced likelihood of kidney recovery.159
A kidney functional reserve <20% was associated with a 5-fold increase in the risk of
persistent abnormal kidney function. In children with prior kidney injury, such as those
recovering from hemolytic uremic syndrome and posterior urethral valves, and children
with congenital heart disease undergoing cardiac surgery with cardiopulmonary bypass,
lower kidney functional reserve predicted long-term renal sequelae, including
proteinuria.160
