a
Pseudo-AKI via inhibiting organic anion transporters 1 and 3 in proximal tubule has also been
reported with antibiotic combinations such as piperacillin/tazobactam or vancomycin plus
piperacillin/tazobactam.529, 530 Nevertheless, interstitial nephritis due to piperacillin/tazobactam has
been documented in the literature.531, 532 Thus, it seems prudent not to place piperacillin/tazobactam
into the “neither dysfunction nor damage” category. bSulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim should be
discriminated from trimethoprim alone and viewed as an independent agent because of described
interstitial nephritis or crystal nephropathy cases induced by sulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim.533

196

Therefore, sulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim may not match the “neither dysfunction nor damage”
category. AKI, acute kidney injury. Reproduced from Karimzadeh et al. 2023.527

b) Damage without dysfunction: Many drugs commonly referred to as nephrotoxic can
fit within this category. There are multiple mechanisms of kidney damage (e.g.,
vascular/glomerular damage, tubular injury, crystal formation, interstitial nephritis
etc.; Table 27). As kidney function is not affected, SCr will not change. Reliance on
SCr alone as a marker of AKI means that these events may go undetected until they
progress and affect kidney function (and SCr) as well.

AF
T

Table 27 | Examples of mechanisms and nephrotoxins that cause damage (structure changes to
the kidneys) without dysfunction (reduction in GFR)
Mechanism
Examples
Acute tubular injury
Aminoglycosides, amphotericin, colistin, vancomycin,
nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs
Acute interstitial nephritis
Antibiotics, proton pump inhibitors
Glomerular disorder
Hydralazine
Tubular obstruction
Acyclovir
Vascular damage
Mitomcyin, tacrolimus
GFR, glomerular filtration rate

c) Dysfunction without damage: This category refers to the effects of drugs through
systematic hypoperfusion and/or a decline in the intraglomerular pressure that lead to
a decrease in GFR and increase in SCr without structural damage. Examples are
ACEi, ARBs, and SGLT2i. Notably, the acute reduction in GFR can be anticipated
and desirable as in the case of ACEi, ARB, or SGLT2i use for renoprotection of
people with CKD and proteinuria.

D

R

d) Dysfunction with damage: These include many drugs exerting more than one effect of
structural damage or prolonged damage leading to reduced GFR. Examples include
nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs), calcineurin inhibitors,
aminoglycosides, and amphotericin B.
Of relevance to clinical decision making, some classes of drugs can cause an acute
reduction in GFR but confer important long-term kidney and cardiovascular health benefits
(e.g., ACEi, ARB, and SGLT2i). This is particularly relevant to management of people with
heart failure and CKD, who may have a rise in SCr meeting criteria for AKI or AKD
following initiation or dose escalation of such medications. Small declines in eGFR in such
situations are expected and have been associated with better long-term outcomes. Similarly,
diuretics for acute heart failure may cause hemoconcentration and changes in SCr
concentration meeting AKI criteria. However, achieving decongestion in this situation is
crucial for improving both short- and long-term outcomes.

197

4.2. Kidney protective strategies
Recommendation 4.2.1: For children or adults with or at risk of AKI, we recommend
drug and nephrotoxin stewardship using a multimodal approach (1C).

AF
T

This recommendation places high value on patient safety by seeking to minimize the risk of
adverse reactions to drugs eliminated by the kidney and by reducing the incidence and
severity of AKI related to drug exposures. It emphasizes the principles of drug stewardship
which refers to the use of multiple strategies to promote responsible, effective, and safe use of
medicines to ensure they are initiated when needed at the right dose, monitored and dose
adjusted as needed to prevent or minimize harm, and are discontinued when no longer
beneficial to a person’s health. A clinical pharmacist often leads this form of multimodal
stewardship, but other healthcare professionals may take on this role. This recommendation
places a lower value on the additional resource use associated with the multimodal drug
stewardship. Although the certainty of evidence for a multimodal strategy is low (or very
low), the Work Group felt that a Level 1 recommendation was warranted given the potential
for harm and cost reduction in applicable populations.

D

R

Key information
Balance of benefits and harms
Drug and nephrotoxin stewardship strategies offer the benefits of safer and more
effective use of medications. Potential harms include avoidance of otherwise effective
medications for the treatment of acute and chronic illnesses due to fears of causing AKI.
Other challenges are differences in financial costs between different drugs with different
nephrotoxic potential. The balance between risk and benefit comes into consideration when
prescribing potentially nephrotoxic medications or medications cleared by the kidney and
deciding when benefits of treatment exceed the risk of causing or worsening AKI, and
whether it is appropriate to initiate, dose-adjust, or discontinue such medications. Drug
stewardship can help support these decisions by improving recognition of potential
medication harms and involving multidisciplinary healthcare professionals in medication
stewardship decisions to balance protection of kidney health while ensuring adequate
treatment of underlying diseases with acceptable levels of AKI risk.
Vancomycin has been the focus of several studies. A review of 9 studies (1 RCT and
8 nonrandomized controlled studies), including a total of 1973 adults showed that
vancomycin administered via continuous infusion was associated with a significantly lower
risk of AKI (OR: 0.47; 95% CI: 0.34–0.64) compared to intermittent dosing but there was no
difference in mortality (Supplementary Table S64).534-536
A systematic review of 12 nonrandomized controlled studies including 7548 adults
concluded that vancomycin dosing based on AUC was associated with a significantly lower
risk of AKI development compared to trough-only guided dosing (RR: 0.68; 95% CI: 0.59–
0.77; Supplementary Table S65).537

198

Based on data of 9 RCTs, including 18,355 adults, the use of CDSS with therapeutic
vancomycin monitoring compared with not using CDSS was not associated with prevention
of AKI (RR: 1.11; 95% CI: 0.99–1.24; Supplementary Table S66).151
Data from 35 studies (1 RCT, 15 nonrandomized studies, and 19 pre-post reports),
including 2 studies in children, concluded that pharmacist interventions for vancomycin
monitoring reduced the risk of AKI (RR: 0.59; 95% CI: 0.48–0.72; Supplementary Table
S67).538, 539 The 2 studies in children provided a consistent, but highly imprecise estimate of
risk of AKI (RR: 0.30; 95% CI: 0.04–2.34).540, 541

AF
T

Other studies evaluated monitoring of beta-lactam and other antibiotics. A review of 3
studies in adults (1 RCT and 2 nonrandomized controlled studies) concluded that therapeutic
drug monitoring of beta-lactam antibiotics (in 2 studies542, 543) or of various antibiotics
(vancomycin, ciprofloxacin, meropenem, and ceftriaxone (1 study155) did not affect the
development of AKI (Supplementary Table S68). A single RCT compared conventional
versus prolonged infusion of meropenem in 102 neonates and showed a lower risk of AKI
with more prolonged infusion (RR: 0.25; 95% CI:0.07–0.83; Supplementary Table S69).544
The role of therapeutic drug monitoring to prevent aminoglycoside nephrotoxicity has
only been investigated in observational studies. A meta-analysis of 5 observational studies
involving 615 participants were reported for gentamicin, and 2 observational studies
involving 159 participants were identified for amikacin. The analysis concluded that target
trough concentrations (Cmin) <2 mg/l for gentamicin and Cmin <10 mg/l for amikacin may
reduce the risk of nephrotoxicity.545

D

R

The role of pharmacist-led interventions in prevention of AKI outcomes has been
investigated in children and adults. As mentioned earlier, pharmacist interventions based on
vancomycin monitoring reduced the risk of AKI in children and adults.538, 539 A single RCT
in 150 adults in the ICU compared the involvement of a clinical pharmacist (i.e., drug
selection, drug administration routes, drug dose adjustment, drug-drug interactions, drug-food
interactions, drug side effect management, and drug incompatibility) with no interventions by
a clinical pharmacist and showed a lower risk of drug induced AKI in participants
randomized to the intervention group (RR: 0.39; 95% CI: 0.19–0.79; Supplementary Table
S70).546
Overall, these studies demonstrate that drug stewardship including therapeutic drug
monitoring and informed interventions can prevent drug induced AKI. However, additional
resources (laboratory, experts, costs) are needed. Also, in some situations, administration of
nephrotoxic drugs may be needed to treat the underlying illness. Importantly, drug
stewardship is only 1 strategy to protect kidney health and may not be sufficient to prevent
multi-factorial AKI.

199

Certainty of evidence
There is moderate certainty of evidence that continuous versus intermittent infusion of
vancomycin reduces the risk of AKI in adults but does not affect mortality. The certainty was
downgraded for some methodological limitations and sparseness of data.534-536 There were no
eligible studies comparing vancomycin infusion in children.
There is low certainty of evidence that AUC dosing of vancomycin versus troughbased dosing reduces the risk of AKI in adults but does not affect mortality (with very low
certainty of evidence). The certainty was downgraded for methodological limitations of
observational studies and, for mortality, sparseness of data.537 There were no eligible studies
comparing vancomycin infusion in children.

AF
T

There is low certainty of evidence regarding CDSS interventions (including
therapeutic monitoring) for vancomycin in adults. The overall certainty of evidence was
based on prioritizing incident AKI, but there is moderate certainty of evidence for a possible
increase in RRT and mostly moderate to high certainty of evidence for mortality and other
sequelae of AKI.151, 547 The certainty of evidence for incident AKI was reduced due to
sparseness of data (adequately powered single study). The certainty of evidence for RRT was
downgraded for imprecision. There were no eligible studies of CDSS interventions for
vancomycin in children.

R

For adults with AKI, with low certainty of evidence, pharmacist involvement in
vancomycin dosing is associated with reduced risk of death and incident AKI. The certainty
of evidence was downgraded for methodological quality (almost all data come from
observational studies).538, 539 From a subset of these studies, there is very low certainty of
evidence of a similar effect in children; however, the certainty of evidence for children was
also downgraded for inconsistency and imprecision.

D

Antibiotic monitoring (other than for vancomycin) in adults with AKI has low
certainty of evidence of no effect on AKI.155, 542, 543 The certainty of evidence was
downgraded for sparseness (for monitoring of specific antibiotics), methodological
limitations of the studies, and imprecision. There were no eligible studies of antibiotic
monitoring overall in children.
There is very low certainty of evidence that slow infusion of meropenem in neonates
reduces the risk of all-cause mortality and AKI compared with conventional infusion.544 The
certainty of evidence was downgraded for methodological limitations and sparseness of
evidence from a single study.
There is low certainty that pharmacist monitoring of all drugs in adults in the ICU
may reduce the risk of AKI. The certainty was downgraded for some methodological
limitations and sparseness of data.546 There were no eligible studies of pharmacist monitoring
of all drugs in children.
200

Values and preferences
Drug and nephrotoxin stewardship are predicated on the understanding that there are
no specific treatments available for AKI and that patients and healthcare professionals prefer
prevention. This recommendation values diligent monitoring through stewardship efforts to
avoid thinking that drug-related kidney disease is an accepted risk for all. It is a preference
towards an active approach to patient-centered care designed to avoid medication-related
harm.

AF
T

Resource use and costs
Available resources are an important consideration in the selection of stewardship
strategies and require that institutions commit resources to medication safety (Table 28).548
Resources to accomplish drug and nephrotoxin stewardship include support for healthcare
professionals committed and assigned to providing the set of coordinated patient care
management strategies. When deciding the cost of personnel, it is important to consider the
immediate and long-term cost of drug-related acute kidney diseases. The additional costs of
dedicating personnel and resources to support drug stewardship could be offset by reduced
total drug costs, shorter lengths of stay in ICU and hospital, and improved downstream health
status by avoiding AKI.549

R

Considerations for implementation
Drug and nephrotoxin stewardship encompass a set of coordinated patient care
management strategies to improve the appropriate and safe use of drugs for the kidneys.
Medication safety requires the provision of appropriate drug dosing (avoidance of overdosing
or underdosing) to people at risk or with AKI and appropriate monitoring for kidney
dysfunction or damage. Examples of the elements of multimodal drug and nephrotoxin
stewardship are provided in Table 28. Successful implementation requires strategic planning,
multidisciplinary collaboration, effective education, technological support, and ongoing
evaluation.

D

201

AF
T

Rationale
There are multiple approaches to prevent the development or progression of drug- or
nephrotoxin-related AKD or adverse events from drugs eliminated by the kidney. As no
single strategy has demonstrated superiority over others, the best option for surveillance and
mitigation of drug related adverse events in people of all ages is to employ a set of multiple,
coordinated management strategies (multimodal approach) in people at risk for these
complications.550 Selecting a less nephrotoxic drug in people at-risk for AKI, reducing
nephrotoxin burden, or applying dose reduction/trough reduction to minimize exposure as
part of a stewardship strategy can result in improved clinical outcomes and reduce drug use
and related costs. Therapeutic drug monitoring, and surveillance for drug-drug interactions
have also been included in effective interventions. Table 31 of the KDIGO 2024 Clinical
Practice Guideline for the Evaluation and Management of CKD provides suggestions for
non-nephrotoxic alternatives to nephrotoxic drugs.17 These alternatives can also be applied to
people with AKI or AKD. The impact of medication burden and the principles of medication
reviews and reconciliation to ensure medication safety that are outlined in Figure 46 of the
KDIGO 2024 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Evaluation and Management of CKD also
apply to people with AKI or AKD.17 However, in the context of AKI and potentially
fluctuating GFR, closer monitoring and more frequent adjustments may be required.

R

Practice Point 4.2.1: Strategies to manage drug-related acute kidney disease include:
d) discontinuation of offending nephrotoxic agents known to cause kidney damage,
if possible;
e) provision of a therapeutic equivalent alternative that is less nephrotoxic, if
available and necessary;
f) reduction in dose if drug continuation is necessary for those drugs with a dose
dependent direct nephrotoxic effect.

D

An assessment of risk versus benefit is required when determining whether to
discontinue or continue medications in situations where ongoing exposure can possibly lead
to development or progression of AKI. This decision should be made with consideration of
the availability of therapeutically equivalent, alternative agents, and the relative effectiveness
of alternative agents for the clinical condition the drug is being prescribed for. Another factor
to consider is whether to opt for dose reduction versus discontinuation which may be affected
by the mechanism of nephrotoxicity. If toxicity is dose dependent, a dose reduction may be
beneficial as opposed to dose-independent toxicity (e.g.,, in drug-induced acute interstitial
nephritis) where drug discontinuation is required.
Practice Point 4.2.2: Therapeutic drug monitoring is a component of multimodal
stewardship that involves serial assessments of drug concentrations in children and
adults receiving potentially nephrotoxic drugs, drugs excreted by the kidneys, and
drugs with narrow therapeutic margins or low therapeutic indices.
Therapeutic drug monitoring is the clinical practice of measuring plasma
concentrations of specific drugs at designated intervals to optimize drug dosing. It can be
202

used to assess drugs with narrow therapeutic indices that cause nephrotoxicity, are eliminated
by the kidneys, or both. (Table 29).551, 552 Therapeutic drug monitoring is appropriate for
potentially nephrotoxic drugs that require vigilant monitoring to prevent AKI development or
progression.553 An appropriately trained member of the care team or a CDSS is needed to
interpret therapeutic drug monitoring results and make dosage adjustments.551 Follow-up
monitoring after drug discontinuation may be needed if supratherapeutic drug concentrations
are observed.554 Importantly, there is a strong association between nonadherence to
therapeutic drug monitoring guidelines for antibiotics and the development of
nephrotoxicity.555 Therapeutic drug monitoring should also be considered for classes of
medications that depend on renal clearance and may accumulate in case of reduced kidney
function (Table 29).

R

AF
T

D

Practice Point 4.2.3: Surveillance for pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic drugdrug interactions that increase the risk of AKI is a component of multimodal
stewardship.
There are several pharmacokinetic drug-drug interactions that potentiate the
likelihood of developing AKI (Table 30).556 Some of these interactions can be avoided by
selecting alternative drugs that do not alter the metabolism of another drug (i.e. selecting
azithromycin instead of erythromycin and clarithromycin to reduce the risk of
rhabdomyolysis when coadministration with a statin is necessary).
Also, several pharmacodynamic drug combinations can result in an increased risk of
developing AKI, for instance the coadministration of NSAIDs, diuretics, and RASi. 556, 557
While it may be necessary to continue coadministration of drugs such as ACEi and diuretics
for people with congestive heart failure, coadministration of NSAIDS can often be avoided.

203

The likelihood of developing AKI increases depending on the number of nephrotoxin
exposures for an individual. Nephrotoxic burden refers to the cumulative or aggregate
exposure to nephrotoxins, with consideration to the nephrotoxic potential for each drug,
evaluated within a relevant time frame.550, 558-560 Specifically, each additional nephrotoxin has
been estimated to contribute to a 1.2–1.8-fold increase in odds of AKI per drug in children
and a 1.2–3.2-fold increase in odds per drug in adults.561 Calculations for assessing
nephrotoxic burden have been proposed.558, 562, 563 Surveillance for the nephrotoxic burden of
≥3 drugs with notification to the healthcare professional using CDSS has been shown to
reduce AKI incidence and severity in children.420, 564, 565

D

R

AF
T

Practice Point 4.2.4: Intravenous hydration strategies may be used for the prevention of
drug-induced crystalline nephropathies as part of multimodal stewardship.

D

AF

Intravenous crystalloid fluid administration is commonly used for the prevention of
AKI when drugs are indicated that cause crystalline nephropathies.566, 567 However, there is
no consensus on optimal hydration protocols. Typical prescriptions in adults include
administration of 1–2 l of intravenous isotonic crystalloids pre- and post-medication
administration. Table 31 describes strategies for prevention of drug-induced crystalline
nephropathies, including isotonic fluids and urinary alkalinization.568

207

4.3. Implementation of kidney protective strategies
Practice Point 4.3.1: Pharmacists should perform medication reconciliation and
medication regimen reviews and perform drug and nephrotoxin stewardship for
hospitalized children and adults with or at risk of AKI.

AF
T

Hospitalized children and adults are at risk for medication errors, adverse drug events,
and therapeutic failures of inappropriately prescribed medications. Several studies have
evaluated the relationship between pharmacist oversight of people in hospital with AKI.
Pharmacist interventions in adults have been associated with better processes of care and
clinical outcomes, including lower risks of medication errors,420, 564, 565, 569-571 lower incidence
of AKI,90, 420, 539, 546, 564, 565, 572 and lower AKI severity.90, 420, 564, 565 One systematic review and
meta-analysis of 34 cohort studies or RCTs (n=19,298 participants) evaluating pharmacist
interventions for vancomycin management reported a higher rate of attaining drug target
concentration, improved SCr monitoring, a lower incidence of vancomycin-induced AKI, and
lower mortality associated with pharmacist involvement.539
Dedicating pharmacists to medication reconciliation and regular medication regimen
reviews can increase healthcare system costs; however, some studies have reported these
costs may be offset by lower drug costs resulting from dose modifications.573 In low-resource
hospital settings, where pharmacist services are unavailable, education and training of other
available healthcare professionals in drug and nephrotoxin stewardship is an alternative
(Table 32).

D

R

Medication review and reconciliation have been specifically suggested: i) at
ICU/hospital admission and discharge, ii) at time of AKI or AKD identification or change in
severity, and iii) when a patient’s clinical condition changes.574 The Work Group agrees with
practice points from Section 4.3 Polypharmacy and drug stewardship of the KDIGO 2024
Clinical Practice Guideline for the Evaluation and Management of CKD and believes the
statements below apply to people with AKI and AKD:17
i.
ii.

iii.

iv.

Perform thorough medication review at transitions of care.
If medications are discontinued during an acute illness, communicate a clear
plan of when to restart the discontinued medications to the affected person and
healthcare professionals, and ensure documentation in the medical record.
If medications such as metformin, ACEi, ARBs, and SGLT2i are discontinued
as a precautionary measure to prevent complications, failure to restart these
medications after the event or procedure may lead to unintentional harm.
Educate and inform people with high risk of AKI regarding the expected
benefits and possible risks of medications.

D

210

Practice Point 4.3.2: Clinical decision support systems (CDSS) can be an effective tool to
facilitate drug and nephrotoxin stewardship strategies in children and adults to prevent
drug-associated AKI or the development of adverse reactions from drugs excreted by
the kidney.

AF
T

CDSS can aid in identification of people at risk of adverse drug events, optimization
of medication management, recognition of drug interactions, and prompt kidney function
monitoring through rule-based alerts.420, 564, 565, 576, 577 CDSS may be useful in preventing
medication dosage errors of drugs excreted by the kidney by alerting to overdosed or
contraindicated dosages for people in hospital with a decreased GFR.578-580 Implementation of
an alert-based CDSS that focused on concurrent administration of ≥3 nephrotoxins pediatrics
in hospital has been shown to result in improved patient outcomes. Further, this CDSS
achieved a sustained impact on clinical practice over 3.5 years and remained effective when
scaled across multiple pediatric hospitals.420, 565 CDSS supporting clinical risk stratification
enables the early identification of people at risk for development or progression of AKI,
allowing for proactive monitoring and intervention. The effectiveness of CDSS may be more
effective when alerts are integrated into healthcare professional workflows at the time and
place of prescribing decisions, when actionable recommendations are included, and when
computer-based.581, 582
4.4 Biomarker characterization of drug-related AKI
Practice Point 4.4.1: Biomarkers of tubular damage are approved for monitoring of
drug-induced AKI.

D

R

Several of the kidney stress and damage biomarkers are detectable within hours after
drug-related AKI and may be used to detect AKI before the rise in SCr. The U.S. FDA has
qualified a composite of 6 biomarkers for monitoring of drug-induced kidney injury in earlyphase clinical trials. This panel, composed of KIM-1, NGAL, clusterin (CLU), cystatin C
(cys), N-acetyl-beta-D-glucosaminidase (NAG), and Osteopontin (OPN), is used to detect
kidney tubular injury when there is concern about the nephrotoxicity of a drug. This is
intended to help in the development of safer and more effective medicines by providing an
earlier indicator of kidney response to drug-induced injury than changes in SCr.
Practice Point 4.4.2: Biomarkers may be useful for differentiating mechanisms and
etiologies of drug-related kidney diseases in children and adults.
Biomarkers may aid in differentiating among mechanisms and etiologies of AKI
among people receiving drugs associated with AKI (Table 33), which may influence
management.241, 583, 584 Serum cystatin C can distinguish non-GFR related increases in SCr
(pseudo-AKI) due to the effects of a drug (Table 33) from true AKI because it is unaffected
by drug-induced changes in creatinine secretion. An increased SCr but normal cystatin C
indicates a drug-induced effect on creatinine generation or tubular secretion (pseudo-AKI),
whereas an increase in both SCr and cystatin C is consistent with AKI.585 Correct
differentiation may guide the decision whether to continue or discontinue suspected drugs.
211

Urine CXCL9 may offer a reliable, noninvasive biomarker for identification of
AIN.241 High urine CXCL9 levels strongly correlate with biopsy proven AIN. Urinary
CXCL9:creatinine values <14.2 ng/g could be used to rule out AIN with negative predictive
values exceeding 94% while values >58.9 ng/g could be used to rule in AIN. However further
validation and approval for clinical use is needed.

AF
T

4.5. Management of drugs eliminated by the kidneys
Practice Point 4.5.1: Dosing of drugs excreted by the kidneys should not be based on
creatinine clearance or glomerular filtration rate (GFR) estimated using equations that
assume a steady state of creatinine production and clearance in children and adults
children with developing or resolving AKI or AKD.

D

Practice Point 4.5.2: Trends in SCr and urine output should be used to guide dosing of
drugs excreted by the kidney. In adults with AKI, calculation of a KeGFR may be
useful when sufficient SCr measurements are available.
Ideal methods for measuring GFR use urinary clearance of exogenous compounds
such as inulin or iohexol but these approaches are not practical to guide drug dosing in
routine clinical practice due to costs and technical challenges. Measurement of urinary
creatinine clearance with timed urine collections is an option to estimate GFR but is limited
by overestimation of true GFR by 10%–20% due to tubular secretion of creatinine and
inaccuracies in timing of collections.
The Work Group agrees with the following practice point related to drug dosing from
the KDIGO 2024 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Evaluation and Management of CKD
and believes it also applies to people with AKI and AKD.

212

Practice Point 4.5.3: Consider and adapt drug dosing in people where GFR, non-GFR
determinants of the filtration markers, or volume of distribution are not in a steady
state.

AF
T

In the context of AKI, it is important to recognize that GFR estimating equations such
as the Cockcroft-Gault, MDRD, and CKD-EPI equations were developed and validated for
people with CKD and assume a steady state of SCr.586, 587 They will overestimate a person's
GFR when SCr is rising (potentially resulting in drug overdosing) and underestimate GFR
when SCr is falling (potentially resulting in drug underdosing). For people with AKI, it is
important to consider trends in SCr and urine output when dosing drugs excreted by the
kidney. Initial dosing may start with a standard dose based on a person's eGFR before the
AKI episode, or a dose that would be appropriate for severely abnormal kidney function,
depending on the clinical situation. A person who is anuric should be considered to have a
GFR <10 ml/min.588 Changes in SCr and urine output along with therapeutic drug monitoring
if available, can be used to guide and adjust subsequent doses.
KeGFR accounts for the rate of change in SCr over time, providing a more accurate
real-time estimate of GFR that can be used to guide drug doses with changing SCr values
accompanying AKI.589 In 1 study, use of a KeGFR equation resulted in changes in drug
dosing in approximately 20% of participants compared to non-kinetic eGFR equations.590 At
this time, no one equation can be endorsed over another and all have limitations.574, 587

D

R

Practice Point 4.5.4: Assess and adjust drug dosing in children and adults receiving
CRRT based on drug-, patient-, and RRT-related factors (Table 34).

213

Table 34 | Drug-, patient-, and RRT-related factors to consider when determining drug doses for people receiving CRRT
Drug-related
• Medications eliminated by the kidney with volume of distribution <0.7 l/kg and/or protein-binding <80% are likely
factors
to be removed with CRRT and may require supplemental dosing with CRRT use.

•
•
•
•
•
•

D
R

Patientrelated
factors

Molecular weight of a drug is an important consideration when using CRRT diffusive techniques compared to
convective techniques because the diffusion coefficient, or saturation coefficient (Sd or SA) is inversely
proportional to the molecular weight such that, as the SA decreases, the molecular weight increases. Conversely,
convective transport across modern membranes is independent of molecular weight in convective hemofiltration
techniques.591, 592
Most drugs have a molecular weight <2000 Daltons and are removed to some extent with newer high-flux and super
high-flux dialyzers that have a pore size between 10,000–30,000 (likely diffusion technique, as well)
Administering supplement doses for drugs with >30%–40% post-hemodialysis removal is suggested.
Optimal dosage regimens for medications eliminated by the kidney and nephrotoxic medications (e.g., digoxin,
vancomycin, aminoglycosides, and calcineurin inhibitors) can be achieved by performing therapeutic drug
monitoring in people who are critically ill receiving RRT.
For antibiotic dose adjustments during AKI and RRT, be mindful of pharmacokinetic target concentration
attainment and optimizing pharmacodynamic effects with consideration to the antibiotic concentration and time
dependent bactericidal effects.
Hypoalbuminemia affects drugs that are highly bound to albumin, by increasing free fraction of the drug leading to
increased clearance and volume of distribution.
Fluid resuscitation, and capillary leak increase the volume of distribution of hydrophilic drugs, for example in
people with sepsis. This may require a loading dose in the range of 25% more than usual.
People with significant urine output (e.g., >20 ml/hr or 500 ml/day), should have clearance from the residual urine
volume added to RRT clearance calculations to determine total drug clearance for the person.
Non-kidney drug clearance can be affected by RRT for AKI. Drugs eliminated by the liver and formed metabolites
may accumulate during AKI, while RRT can possibly increase CYP3A4 activity. Estimating non-kidney clearance
in people who are critically ill is challenging without assessing serum drug levels due to the various pathways
involved in drug metabolism and clearance.

AF
T

•

•
•

214

•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•

Effluent flow rate determines drug clearance in CRRT given it is slower than the blood flow rate and more drug
removal occurs with faster effluent flow rates.593
If RRT is <25% of the patient’s clearance (sum of residual kidney and non-kidney), then RRT appears not to add
significantly to person’s drug elimination process.592
RRT modality affects drug clearance such that when a similar blood flow rate is used, drug elimination by RRT can
be ordered from the highest to lowest as follows: CVVHDF > CVVH > IHD. 591
For CRRT, clearance can be estimated by checking the run time and subtracting the down time.
Replacement fluid is commonly provided in convective techniques of RRT, either pre- or post-dilution modes. Predilution mode reduces drug clearance more than post-dilution by approximately 15%.591
Hemofilter lifespan can affect solute clearance (i.e., older filters provide less clearance).
Membrane types affect adsorption and adsorption is inversely correlated to effluent flow rate. 591, 594, 595
Drug removal estimates for IHD are not equivalent to CRRT. When suing drug removal estimates from primary or
secondary sources, it is important to confirm that the RRT modality, technique, and settings are the same as the
patient scenario being evaluated.

215

4.6. AKI following other nephrotoxic exposures
Practice Point 4.6.1: In children and adults presenting with community-acquired AKI,
perform a thorough social, occupational, and demographic history to understand
potential exposures to pesticides and environmental exposures as a possible cause of
AKI.

AF
T

Each year, millions of pounds of pesticides are used in agriculture to manage a variety
of threats to crops. These chemicals present potential occupational exposure risks that
healthcare professionals should be aware of, particularly in relation to environmental toxicity
to the kidney. It is known that occupational exposure, even for short durations, can increase
the incidence of AKI.596, 597 Also, residential exposure for people living close to agricultural
lands can be a concern for adverse effects.598, 599 The incidence of AKI is increasing in
younger adults in low-to-middle resource settings due to accidental and intentional
organophosphate poisoning.600 Awareness by healthcare professionals can promote detection
of pesticide-associated AKI, and educate people about occupational risks and reduce
exposures.
Practice Point 4.6.2: Determine and limit the use of natural products that have the
potential to contribute to AKI and AKD through patient-centered discussions.

R

Over 80% of the population in low- and middle-resource settings use herbal and
traditional medicines because of cultural beliefs, economic considerations, and
accessibility.601 Regulations surrounding the approval of natural products for public
consumption are less stringent than prescription medications, and adverse events often go
unreported. The true incidence of AKI attributable to natural products is unknown. There
have been several case reports and reviews suggesting a causation between several natural
products presented in Table 35.

D

4.7. Contrast-associated AKI (CA-AKI): Recognition
Practice Point 4.7.1: Consider the differential diagnosis of post-contrast AKI, which
may have causes other than contrast exposure.
Contrast-associated AKI (CA-AKI) refers to AKI following parenteral administration
of iodinated radiocontrast media. The reported incidence of CA-AKI has varied in the
literature with use of different definitions, with some reviews reporting it as the third most
common cause of AKI in hospitalized adults. The attribution of contrast exposure to AKI
continues to be a source of debate; however, CA-AKI has been consistently associated with
adverse short- and long-term outcomes.

D

R

The Work Group agrees with the suggestion from Section 4.4 of the KDIGO 2024
Clinical Practice Guideline for the Evaluation and Management of CKD that the term
“contrast-associated AKI” be used to recognize the broader differential diagnosis for the
etiology of AKI in many such cases, which may or may not be solely attributable to
contrast.17 The presumption that all AKI following contrast administration is CA-AKI has led
to a lack of specificity in case definitions and associated epidemiology; indeed, some have
questioned the existence and clinical importance of CA-AKI.603 Nonetheless, in 2023, the
American College of Radiology (ACR) Contrast Manual stated the position of the ACR
Committee on Drugs and Contrast Media that CA-AKI is a real, albeit rare, entity.604 The
Work Group concurred with this statement while recognizing that CA-AKI includes other
etiologies of AKI following radiocontrast exposure, including multifactorial causes
(combined with radiocontrast).
4.8. Contrast-associated AKI: Risk assessment
Practice Point 4.8.1: Use validated tools to assess the risk of AKI in adults receiving
intra-arterial radiocontrast for cardiac procedures.
The Work Group endorses Practice Point 4.4.1 from the KDIGO 2024 Clinical
Practice Guideline for the Evaluation and Management of CKD to assess the risk of CA-AKI
with intra-arterial contrast for cardiac procedures using validated tools.17 Many models
217

include patient-related risk factors for CA-AKI in addition to eGFR, and these models have
been validated for estimating risk in people with or without CKD.

AF
T

The reported risk of CA-AKI is higher with procedures involving intra-arterial
administration compared with intravenous administration of radiocontrast. This difference in
risk may be due to diversities in patient populations or to variations in the nephrotoxicity of
intra-arterial delivery of contrast material. Commonly reported patient-related risk factors for
CA-AKI are age, baseline kidney function, medical history (diabetes, cardiovascular diseases,
and heart failure), presenting with acute coronary syndrome, and cardiac function (New York
Heart Association Class, left ventricular ejection fraction, and Killip class). 605 Risk also
varies with procedural factors, with highest risk associated with interventional (rather than
diagnostic) coronary angiography (i.e., percutaneous coronary interventions with balloon
angioplasty ± coronary stent placement) for acute myocardial infarction (MI). In addition to
intra-arterial dye administration and arterial instrumentation, this may relate to the higher
volume of contrast used in interventional procedures and hemodynamic instability associated
with the clinical situation. Other procedure-related risk factors include the osmolarity and
volume of dye used (including repeated studies).

D

R

There are several comprehensive systematic reviews of validated risk scores that can
be used to predict the risk of CA-AKI following coronary angiography (Table 36).605-607
Validated models are available to predict AKI defined by KDIGO SCr criteria, as well as to
predict RRT.606 Newer models have generally demonstrated better calibration in more recent
validation studies.607 Most recently, Feng and colleagues conducted a systematic review of
the published scores to predict CA-AKI after coronary angiography;605 their analysis of 45
studies found a pooled C-statistic of 0.83 (95% CI: 0.82–0.84), with pooled sensitivity of
0.74 (95% CI: 0.70–0.78) and pooled specificity of 0.78 (95% CI: 0.75–0.82), with
substantial heterogeneity in discrimination between prediction model studies. Notably, there
has been no meaningful improvement in estimates of model discrimination despite
publication of new models. The variables that have been repeatedly included in different
prediction models are listed in Table 36.

218

220

Practice Point 4.8.2: Assess children and adults being considered for intravenous
radiocontrast administration for risk factors for contrast-associated AKI, and in
particular screen for preexisting impairment of kidney function.

AF
T

The KDIGO 2012 Clinical Practice Guideline for AKI suggested assessment of the
risk of CA-AKI, and specifically screening for “pre-existing impairment of kidney function”
in all people for whom a procedure requiring intravascular (intra-arterial or intravenous)
radiocontrast is being considered.608 The Work Group continues to support the assessment of
risk for CA-AKI in all people being considered for intravascular contrast. However,
intravenous contrast administration for computed tomography (CT) scans generally has
lower risks for CA-AKI than intra-arterial administration for coronary angiography for
several reasons. Cardiac angiography differs from intravenous contrast medium
administration in 4 major ways: 1) the injection is intra-arterial and suprarenal instead of
through a peripheral vein, 2) the injection requires a catheter which can dislodge
atheroemboli, 3) the contrast medium dose to the kidneys will be more abruptly delivered
and concentrated resulting in greater “first-pass” exposure, and 4) people undergoing
coronary angiography may have other chronic comorbidities and acute insults (e.g., acute
ventricular dysfunction during MI) that predispose to the development of CA-AKI.
Accordingly, risk estimates from models developed to predict the risk of CA-AKI following
coronary procedures can be inaccurate for people receiving intravenous radiocontrast.

D

R

Multiple current published guidelines for risk assessment for CA-AKI following
intravenous radiocontrast administration in adults identify kidney function (i.e., reduced
eGFR in CKD or presence of AKI), along with some other clinical variables as important risk
factors. The 2020 joint Consensus statement from the ACR and National Kidney Foundation
(NKF) defined risk factors for CA-AKI following intravenous contrast as eGFR<30 ml/min
per 1.73 m2 or AKI, or eGFR 30–44 ml/min per 1.73 m2 with multiple risk factors;609 this
criteria was also included part of the KDIGO 2024 Clinical Practice Guideline for the
Evaluation and Management of CKD.17
The KDIGO 2024 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Evaluation and Management of
CKD listed commonly accepted risk factors for CA-AKI in 2 broad categories,17, 610
including abnormal kidney function (CKD or AKI), but also a variety of other patient-related
and procedure-related risk factors (Table 37). Among published reports of risk factors for
CA-AKI in adults following CT scans, CKD was the major risk factor, particularly CKD
with cancer (CKD G4–G5: OR: 3.76; CKD G3b: OR 1.93) and CKD G3b–G4 without
cancer (OR: 1.42–1.9; CKD G5: 1.45; NS). Other significant risk factors included shock
(OR: 2.15), and congestive heart failure (CHF) or proteinuria with cancer (OR: 1.7).611, 612

221

AF

4.9. Contrast-associated AKI: Prevention
4.9.1. Choice of imaging technique
Practice Point 4.9.1.1: Following a risk assessment, the decision to administer
intravascular contrast for a diagnostic or interventional procedure should be made
weighing the risks versus benefits of the procedure. Where the perceived benefit
exceeds the risk, contrast procedures should not be deferred or delayed. However, when
possible, alternative imaging methods should be used for people with or at high risk of
contrast-associated AKI (CA-AKI) (Figure 12).

R

An assessment of risks versus benefits is important with all diagnostic and
interventional procedures involving radiocontrast administration. It is desirable to avoid
iodinated contrast media in people with or at high risk of AKI if adequate diagnostic tests or
therapeutic interventions can be obtained without contrast administration. However,
procedures involving the administration of contrast may be necessary for the management of
many clinical syndromes. When selecting imaging methods, it is important to evaluate the
potential impact of limiting or postponing access to diagnostic and therapeutic contrast
imaging procedures.

D

This practice point aligns with Practice Point 4.4.1 from the KDIGO 2024 Clinical
Practice Guideline for the Evaluation and Management of CKD, approaching the decision
for contrast administration on an individualized basis, in which the risk of CA-AKI following
administration of radiocontrast should be balanced against the risk of inadequate imaging and
therapy following alternative imaging tests.17

222

AF
T

Figure 12 | Approach to risk assessment for CA-AKI with diagnostic or interventional
contrast imaging procedures. CA-AKI, contrast-associated acute kidney injury

R

Practice Point 4.9.1.2: Iso-osmolar or low-osmolar iodinated contrast media may be
used in children and adults at risk of CA-AKI (1B).

D

High-osmolar contrast agents are no longer used for intravascular imaging in clinical
radiology and have been replaced globally by safer, more tolerable low-osmolar and isoosmolar alternatives with a lower risk of AKI. Whether iso-osmolar agents (~290 mOsm/kg)
have a lower risk of AKI than low-osmolar agents (322–900 mOsm/kg) remains uncertain. A
meta-analysis of 25 RCTs comparing the risk of AKI between iso- and low-osmolar agents
reported a pooled RR of 0.80 (95% CI: 0.61–1.04). Subgroup analyses of trials restricted to
people with abnormal kidney function receiving intra-arterial contrast administration
suggested potential for differences between specific low-osmolar versus iso-osmolar agents.
The risk of AKI was greater for the low-osmolar agent iohexol than for the iso-osmolar agent
iodixanol (RR: 0.38; 95% CI: 0.21–0.68), while there was no difference between iodixanol
and other (non-iohexol) low-osmolar agents (RR: 0.95; 95% CI: 0.50–1.78). A subsequent
meta-analysis of 10 RCTs restricted to people with CKD reported no difference in AKI with
iso-osmolar compared to low-osmolar agents (RR: 0.72; 95% CI: 0.50–1.04). The effect
estimate was attenuated when the analysis was restricted to large RCTs with more than 250
participants, used as a surrogate of trial quality (RR: 0.93; 95% CI: 0.66–1.30).
223

The existing evidence base therefore has not established a benefit to use of isoosmolar over low-osmolar agents. Accordingly, the choice between low-osmolar or isoosmolar contrast agents may depend on availability and cost, particularly in lower- and
lower-to-middle- resource settings. It is appropriate to use the least nephrotoxic agent
available for people at increased risk of CA-AKI. This practice is consistent with statements
published by the ACR,604 Canadian Association of Radiology,613 and European Society of
Urogenital Radiology.614
Practice Point 4.9.1.3: For children and adults at high risk of CA-AKI, use the lowest
possible dose of intravascular radiocontrast to achieve adequate imaging.

D

R

AF
T

Contrast dose is associated with risk of CA-AKI; however, this relationship is
complex and modified by patient and procedural factors. While risk generally increases with
dose, small increases in contrast volume may lead to disproportionate increases in AKI risk
in high-risk people, while even moderate-to-high contrast volumes may not cause AKI in
low-risk people (Figure 13). Furthermore, recent studies illustrate that the relationship
between contrast dose and CA-AKI is non-linear across the range of contrast volumes and
heterogeneous according to a patient’s preprocedural risk. A variety of procedural strategies
can minimize contrast administration, including imaging and guidance technologies (e.g.,
intravascular ultrasound on optical coherence tomography), fluoroscopy optimization,
injection techniques, and catheter selection. Automated contrast injectors can lower contrast
volumes but the effect depends heavily on how they are used with a need for training and
optimization of settings.615 Procedural planning and staging of procedures (limiting the
extent of contrast used in a single session) can further help to limit the dose of contrast
exposure.

Figure 13 | Prediction of risks of AKI as a function of contrast volume and
preprocedural AKI risk in percutaneous coronary intervention. Risks of absolute
increase in creatinine were calculated for patients with preprocedural AKI risks of 5%, 45%,
and 80%. Odds ratios (ORs) and absolute risk differences (RDs) of AKI risks are given for
increases in contrast volume from 100 to 300 ml, from 400 to 600 ml, and from 700 to 900
224

ml. The colored bands indicate 95% CIs. To convert creatinine to micromoles per liter,
multiply by 88.4.616 AKI, acute kidney injury

AF
T

Several calculations and multivariable modeling approaches have been developed to
estimate the risk of CA-AKI.617, 618 Estimation of a safe contrast limit is a strategy to prevent
excessive exposure to contrast media. These limits are often calculated based on patientspecific parameters, such as preprocedural kidney function in addition to other patient risk
factors, and are intended to give individualized, actional estimates of safe contrast volume
dose for planning procedures (Table 38). The Contrast Reducing Injury Sustained by Kidneys
(RISK) trial, a large, multicenter, stepped-wedge, cluster RCT in people undergoing coronary
angiography and intervention, demonstrated that a multimodality approach providing CDSS
accompanied by audit and feedback on contrast volume significantly reduced the incidence
of CA-AKI.153 Secondary outcomes included a significant reduction in the contrast dose
administration, suggesting this may have mediated the reduction in AKI incidence observed.
Subsequent trials confirmed that multimodality interventions that incorporate strategies to
reduce contrast dye dosing along with intravenous hydration strategies can be implemented
in health systems successfully and achieve a lower incidence of CA-AKI.619

D

4.9.2. Contrast-associated AKI Prevention: Fluid administration
Practice Point 4.9.2.1: For children and adults at high risk of CA-AKI, incorporate
volume status assessment into intravenous hydration management strategies to reduce
the risk of AKI.

225

Volume depletion is a risk factor for CA-AKI. Accordingly, the prophylactic
administration of intravenous crystalloids to avoid hypovolemia in people at high risk of CAAKI has been a standard practice for decades. However, the clinical significance of small
acute changes in SCr that define CA-AKI has been questioned in recent years. This has led to
reassessment of the benefits versus harms of empiric prophylactic volume expansion to
prevent CA-AKI, even in higher risk groups. Furthermore, the growing literature
demonstrating that volume overload and congestion can cause or worsen AKI (in addition to
potentially causing acute pulmonary edema) has also raised awareness of the potential
importance of personalized fluid management in this setting. Recent evidence suggests that
strategies that provide more precise assessment of volume status may reduce the incidence of
CA-AKI by individualizing fluid management.

AF
T

In standard clinical practice, the assessment of volume status uses medical history,
physical examination, and clinically available tests (including B-type natriuretic peptide,
chest x-ray) to determine if volume overload is present, in which case prophylactic hydration
may be contraindicated. Multiple trials concerning the approach to volume assessment and
fluid management to reduce the risk of CA-AKI have been conducted recently. Findings
suggest the use of hemodynamic monitoring or volume assessment to guide fluid
administration for CA-AKI prophylaxis may reduce CA-AKI incidence, including with both
invasive monitoring623, 624 and noninvasive assessment methods.625, 626

D

R

A systematic review and network meta-analysis examined the effects of different
hydration strategies for the prevention of CA-AKI. From analysis of 60 eligible RCTs,
hemodynamic-guided hydration (OR: 0.41; 95% CI: 0.18–0.93), and device-guided matched
hydration with forced diuresis guided by urine flow rate (UFR) (OR: 0.32; 95% CI: 0.14–0.7)
significantly reduced the incidence of CA-AKI.625 A subsequent meta-analysis by Cossette et
al. included 13 RCTs of participants undergoing coronary angiography or percutaneous
coronary intervention (PCI) evaluating tailored intravenous hydration strategies using either
hemodynamic monitoring or UFR replacement to guide crystalloid administration. There
were significant reductions in incidence of CA-AKI (RR: 0.56; 95% CI: 0.46–0.69,
P<0.00001), and of major adverse cardiac events (MACE) (RR: 0.57; 95% CI: 0.42–0.78,
P=0.0005) with tailored compared to non-tailored intravenous hydration.627 This recent
evidence suggests that the efficacy and safety of intravenous crystalloid administration for
CA-AKI may be improved when guided by these tailored approaches.
Hemodynamic-guided hydration may be achieved using invasive approaches,
including left ventricular end diastolic pressure (LVEDP) measurement during cardiac
catheterization623 or continuous central venous pressure monitoring624 or noninvasive
approaches, including bioimpedance (Table 39).626 Reports of the effects of matched
hydration to urine output with forced diuresis are from studies completed using a specific
medical device. This strategy may be challenging to implement without such a device.
Prophylactic diuretic administration is not recommended to prevent CA-AKI, and caution is
226

warranted if attempting to implement these strategies without an established protocol or
device.

R

AF
T
