CA-AKI, contrast-associated acute kidney injury; i.v. intravenous

D

Recommendation 4.9.2.1: For adults at high risk of AKI without fluid overload
undergoing intravascular iodinated contrast-based procedures, we recommend
prophylactic periprocedural volume expansion with 0.9% saline over hypotonic
solutions (e.g., 0.45% saline), sodium bicarbonate, or no intravenous hydration (1B).
This recommendation places a high value on reducing the risk of CA-AKI, specifically when
patient- or procedure-associated risk factors or risk prediction models identify people as
being at high risk of CA-AKI. This recommendation is supported by systematic reviews that
demonstrate a lower incidence of CA-AKI among adults who received intravenous isotonic
crystalloids compared to no hydration, particularly among high-risk adults receiving
coronary procedures. Sodium bicarbonate did not reduce the incidence of CA-AKI compared
to 0.9% saline in high quality trials. The large landmark Prevention of Serious Adverse
Events Following Angiography (PRESERVE) trial was conducted in adults undergoing
coronary angiography with intra-arterial contrast and showed no difference in a composite
of death, dialysis, or persistent abnormal kidney function at 90 days between the 0.9% saline
versus bicarbonate treated cohort.629 Similarly, there was no difference in the largest trial in
227

critically ill participants receiving intravenous contrast for CT scanning or arteriography.630
This recommendation places a lower emphasis on the additional resource use and costs of
providing intravenous hydration before and after contrast-imaging procedures.

AF
T

Key information
Balance of benefits and harms
For this guideline, we performed an updated systematic review and meta-analyses of
RCTs that compared various forms of hydration, including no hydration for CA-AKI
prevention. These were built on a systematic review of hydration versus no hydration and a
network meta-analysis of different fluids.625, 631 The majority of studies were conducted in
participants undergoing coronary angiography. There is less evidence regarding the risk of
AKI in people undergoing intravascular contrast imaging. The certainty of evidence was
downgraded due to some methodological issues with the trials and a very imprecise effect
estimate.

D

R

We identified 7 RCTs that compared intravenous crystalloids versus no hydration,
which included 3258 participants, that found consistent evidence of a reduction in CA-AKI
incidence with intravenous crystalloid hydration (RR: 0.64; 95% CI: 0.54–0.76)
(Supplementary Table S71).625, 631, 632 Across 6 of the RCTs with 2475 participants, mortality
was also lower with intravenous hydration (RR: 0.57; 95% CI: 0.33–0.98), but the estimate
for risk of RRT was imprecise (across 3 RCTs with RRT events). Included in the metaanalyses, the A MAastricht Contrast-Induced Nephropathy Guideline (AMACING) trial was
a noninferiority RCT that compared pre- and post- procedure hydration with intravenous
0.9% saline versus no hydration in 660 adults with an eGFR between 30–59 ml/min per 1.73
m2 receiving elective intra-arterial (48%) or intravenous (52%) contrast.633 The AMACING
trial concluded noninferiority of no hydration compared to intravenous hydration in
preventing CA-AKI and found that hydration increased costs in this population. However,
this noninferiority trial recruited fewer participants than originally planned and had a lower
incidence of CA-AKI (2.6%) than projected. Trials incorporated in our systematic review
and others included higher risk participants undergoing coronary intervention with an
average incidence of CA-AKI of 14% across all studies. Given the heterogenous findings
between the lower risk cohort included in the AMACING trial compared with the other trials
conducted in high-risk groups receiving coronary intervention, it was the Work Group view
that the recommendation for intravenous crystalloids for CA-AKI prophylaxis should be
applied to people deemed at high risk of CA-AKI to warrant preventive intervention.
The optimal intravenous fluid type for CA-AKI prevention remains uncertain due a
lack of direct comparisons. No direct trial comparisons between oral hydration versus no
hydration or 0.45% sodium chloride versus no hydration were available; however, in indirect
comparisons by network meta-analysis, neither oral hydration nor 0.45% saline solution
significantly reduced CA-AKI compared to no hydration (Supplementary Tables S72 and
S73).625 In contrast, the network meta-analysis found lower risks of CA-AKI with
228

intravenous 0.9% saline (2 trials with direct comparisons) and sodium bicarbonate (1 direct
trial) versus intravenous 0.45% saline (Supplementary Tables S74 and S75).625 Thus, for
people identified at high risk of CA-AKI warranting periprocedural hydration, the Work
Group deemed that these findings supported the practice of using intravenous isotonic
crystalloid solutions in preference to hypotonic intravenous solutions or oral hydration.

R

AF
T

Well-conducted RCTs, including the PRESERVE trial,634 have demonstrated no
superiority of sodium bicarbonate over 0.9% saline in preventing CA-AKI or improving
kidney outcomes within 90 days (Supplementary Table S76).615, 629, 630, 635-648 Across low- or
moderate risk of bias RCTs (those without serious limitations) there is no evidence of
differences in incident AKI (RR: 0.99; 95% CI: 0.88–1.13; 18 RCTs, N=10.080) or of need
for RRT (RR: 1.21; 95% CI: 0.81–1.82; 16 RCTs, N=9175); although, the estimate for RRT
was imprecise due to the low incidence of RRT (approximately 1.0%). As has been
previously noted, high risk of bias studies favored sodium bicarbonate over 0.9% saline to
prevent incident AKI.649, 650 However, in the largest RCT, the landmark PRESERVE trial
conducted in 5177 participants with CKD G3–G4 undergoing arteriography, sodium
bicarbonate versus 0.9% saline did not reduce the primary endpoint of a composite of death,
need for dialysis, or a persistent SCr increase of ≥50% at 90 days, nor reduce the incidence of
CA-AKI.629 Similarly, in the multicenter HYDRAREA trial involving critically ill
participants, Valette et al. found that isotonic sodium bicarbonate did not reduce CA-AKI
incidence following contrast administration compared to 0.9% saline.630 Both solutions are
generally safe, but sodium bicarbonate may carry a slightly higher risk of electrolyte
disturbances, such as hypokalemia, and is more prone to compounding errors from manual
preparation. Given similar effectiveness in studies without high risk of bias and a slightly
more favorable safety and cost profile, 0.9% saline is often preferred in clinical practice
unless specific patient factors, such as metabolic acidosis, suggest a potential advantage with
administration of bicarbonate.

D

The optimal fluid volume and timing of administration for prevention of CA-AKI
remain uncertain. In the cluster randomized Contrast RISK trial of multimodal intervention
for CA-AKI prevention in coronary angiography and intervention, the hydration approach
guided by LVEDP promoted use of isotonic 0.9% saline or sodium bicarbonate at 3 ml/kg for
1 h before the procedure followed by an LVEDP-guided rate during and for 4 hours postprocedure.153 This approach resulted in a mean volume of approximately 850 ml of
prophylactic isotonic crystalloid administration, and, in concert with a reduction in contrast
dose, significantly reduced the incidence of CA-AKI without increasing the risk of MACE,
including heart failure. A secondary analysis of intravenous crystalloid (isotonic 0.9% saline
or sodium bicarbonate) administered with angiography in the PRESERVE trial suggested
that a total of 1 l of crystalloid delivered over 1 hour pre-contrast to 6 hours post-contrast
administration was associated with a similar risk of death, dialysis, or persistent abnormal
kidney function at 90 days to larger volumes administered over longer periods.651

229

The harms of fluid administration can include complications from volume overload
such as pulmonary edema if excessive fluids are administered, which may occur in people
with heart failure or compromised cardiac function. Thus, caution is warranted applying this
recommendation to people with or at high risk of volume overload and people with a low risk
of CA-AKI who may not benefit.
Certainty of evidence
There is high certainty of evidence that prophylactic volume expansion with
intravenous isotonic crystalloid solution (versus no hydration) prior to intravascular contrast
reduces the risk of incident AKI, RRT, and mortality in adults at increased risk of AKI. 625, 631,
632 Trials in children are lacking.

AF
T

There is very low certainty of evidence regarding prophylactic volume expansion
with either oral hydration or intravenous 0.45% saline versus no hydration. No RCTs have
been found that directly compared either these interventions to no hydration. Conclusions are
based on a network meta-analysis of RCTs of adults at receiving intravascular contrast; the
network meta-analysis evaluated only incident AKI.625
There is low certainty of evidence that prophylactic volume expansion with
intravenous isotonic (0.9%) saline reduces the risk of incident AKI compared with
intravenous hypotonic (0.45%) saline in adults.625 The certainty of evidence was downgraded
for some methodological limitations and imprecision (since the summary estimate was near,
but not statistically significant).

D

R

There is low certainty of evidence that prophylactic volume expansion with
intravenous sodium bicarbonate reduces the risk of incident AKI compared with intravenous
hypotonic (0.45%) saline in adults.625 The certainty of evidence was downgraded for some
methodological limitations and sparseness of data. A single study directly compared the fluid
interventions, but the network meta-analysis was consistent.
There is moderate certainty of evidence that in adults at increased risk of AKI who
are undergoing intravascular contrast imaging, the risk of incident AKI, and possibly RRT, is
similar with prophylactic hydration with either intravenous isotonic sodium bicarbonate or
0.9% saline solutions.615, 629, 630, 635-648, 652-665 While meta-analysis of all studies suggests a
benefit for preventing AKI with sodium bicarbonate, we found that high risk of bias trials
were more likely to report this finding. Our conclusion is based on sensitivity analyses
excluding high risk of bias trials, which found no statistically significant difference in risk of
incident AKI. For AKI, the certainty of evidence for studies with no methodological
limitations (low risk of bias) was downgraded to moderate due to imprecision and the
certainty of evidence for studies without serious methodological limitations (low or moderate
risk of bias) was downgraded to moderate due to some methodological limitations. The

230

certainty of evidence for RRT was very low due to imprecision, rare events in most studies,
and methodological limitations.

AF
T

Values and preferences
People at high risk of CA-AKI and healthcare professionals generally value
interventions that protect kidney function without introducing significant additional risk or
burden. Strategies that are simple, evidence-based, and have a favorable safety profile are
preferred. Prophylactic periprocedural volume expansion with intravenous crystalloids is
noninvasive, low cost, and well established in clinical practice. Preferences often lean toward
0.9% saline due to its widespread availability, lower cost, familiarity, and relative simplicity
of preparation and use. People at high-risk and their healthcare professionals typically
prioritize kidney protection and accept the inconvenience of fluid administration if it can
meaningfully reduce the risk of CA-AKI. However, people with conditions like heart failure
may place a high value on avoiding volume overload and may prefer tailored or more
conservative hydration approaches. For people with or at risk of complications from volume
overload, shared decision-making is important, with preferences influenced by perceived
risk, severity of comorbidities, prior experiences, and trust in the recommendation of the
clinical team.

D

R

Resource use and costs
Prophylactic intravenous crystalloids for people at high risk of CA-AKI is generally
considered a low-cost intervention, especially when compared to the potential expenses
associated with managing CA-AKI, including prolonged hospitalization, dialysis, and longterm care for CKD. The direct costs involve the solutions themselves, intravenous
administration supplies, and healthcare professional time for monitoring and delivery. Saline
(0.9%) is inexpensive, widely available, and already integrated into most hospital formularies
and workflows, making it a low-cost and low-resource option. In contrast, isotonic sodium
bicarbonate is generally more expensive and may require additional resources to support onsite compounding or special preparation. This can increase pharmacy and nursing workload
and resource requirements, as well as the potential for error.
Indirect costs may arise from extended pre- or post-procedure observation,
particularly in outpatient settings where additional staffing or bed occupancy is required.
While the intervention is relatively inexpensive, the cost-effectiveness depends on
appropriate patient selection. Targeting those at high risk of CA-AKI can avoid unnecessary
resource use in people at low risk. In settings with limited capacity or staffing, such as
outpatient settings, the logistics of administering fluids pre- and post-contrast may also
contribute to operational strain. Overall, when used judiciously in people at high risk,
prophylactic intravenous fluid administration is a resource-efficient strategy that may prevent
costlier complications.

231

AF
T

Considerations for implementation
Implementing this recommendation requires coordination across clinical teams to
identify people at high risk, and standardized protocols and infrastructure to support timely
fluid administration. Key considerations include use of strategies for identifying people at
high-risk using risk assessment tools. Protocols should promote the use of 0.9% saline, with
guidance on rate and duration of fluid administration with adjustments for comorbidities like
heart failure where fluid overload is a concern. Decision support tools that are integrated into
clinical workflows in radiology, cardiology, and emergency departments may aid
implementation.153 Education and training for staff, accompanied by audit and feedback, can
facilitate effective implementation. Access to day units or infusion spaces may be needed for
outpatient procedures. Overall, successful implementation depends on institutional
commitment, interdisciplinary collaboration, and mechanisms to monitor adherence and
outcomes.
Rationale
The use of iodinated radiocontrast media has been associated with the occurrence of
AKI, with varying rates reported depending on the population studied, the type, route and
dose of contrast agent being used, and the presence of other risk factors for AKI. Existing
data support the use of periprocedural fluid administration in people at high risk without fluid
overload but there is no evidence that a particular type of fluid is superior for AKI prevention
to 0.9% saline which is available worldwide. This recommendation is aligned with the joint
ACR/NKF Guideline.609 This recommendation is specific to fluid administration for CA-AKI
prevention, and distinct from Recommendation 3.1.2 favoring buffered crystalloids over
0.9% saline for other patient groups at risk of AKI/AKD.

R

Practice Point 4.9.2.2: In the absence of volume overload, it is appropriate to hold
diuretics when administering isotonic crystalloid to prevent CA-AKI in adults at high
risk.

D

Several trials have suggested that prophylactic diuretic administration increases the
incidence of CA-AKI, potentially by causing hypovolemia,666-668 although this has been an
inconsistent finding.669 Furthermore, several trials have found that matching hydration to
forced diuresis using furosemide can reduce the incidence of CA-AKI, when delivered using
of an integrated device matching saline fluid replacement to urine flow rate.625, 627
In a recent Cochrane review, prophylactic furosemide did not decrease the incidence
of CA-AKI in an analysis of 8 trials involving 1886 participants (RR: 0.87; 95% CI: 0.57–
1.34, P=0.54). Findings were similar in a subanalysis of 5 of these trials (n=1604
participants) that reported RRT incidence post-contrast (RR: 0.76; 95% CI: 0.35–1.65;
P=0.48).427 This review also included trials that compared the use of furosemide together
with an integrated device to match saline administration to urine flow rate versus saline
alone. A total of 1302 participants were included in 2 trials of people receiving coronary
232

AF
T

angiography, and 3 trials of transcatheter aortic valve replacement. There was no difference
in CA-AKI between furosemide-forced diuresis with device-guided urine output replacement
versus saline hydration (RR: 0.83; 95% CI: 0.48–1.44; P=0.51). Further, among the subgroup
of 8 trials (n=930 participants) comparing furosemide without an integrated urine flow rate
monitoring and fluid replacement device to saline hydration, the former did not significantly
change the incidence of CA-AKI (RR: 0.83; 95% CI: 0.53–1.28; P=0.39).427 However, it
should be noted that the cardiac procedures across these trials were significantly
heterogeneous, including 3 trials in participants having coronary angiography, 2 trials in
people undergoing cardiac surgery, 1 trial using furosemide to manage volume overload in
people in the ICU with respiratory failure, and a trial comparing atrial natriuretic peptide
versus saline (no furosemide). Thus, a personalized approach to decisions about the use of
diuretics in people at risk of CA-AKI and that the practice of withholding diuretics was
rational when administering prophylactic isotonic crystalloid for volume expansion in the
absence of volume overload.
4.9.3 Prophylactic therapies: Medication management
The Work Group agrees with the following practice point and radiology guideline
statements related to the intravenous administration of radiocontrast from the KDIGO 2024
Clinical Practice Guideline for the Evaluation and Management of CKD.
Practice Point 4.9.3.1: The intravenous administration of radiocontrast media can be
managed in accordance with consensus statements from the radiology societies in
people with AKI or GFR <60 ml/min per 1.73 m2 (CKD G3a–G5) undergoing elective
investigation.

D

R

The Work Group refers the reader to the most recent radiology guidelines specifically
noting the difference between intravenous and intra-arterial radiocontrast:
• Use of low-osmolality contrast media and iso-osmolarity contrast media
• Use of minimum radiocontrast dose to achieve a diagnostic study
• Withdrawal of nonessential potentially nephrotoxic medications (e.g., NSAIDs,
diuretics, aminoglycosides, amphotericin, platins, zoledronate, and methotrexate) in
people with AKI or eGFR <30 ml/min per 1.73 m2 for 24–48 hours before and 48
hours after radiocontrast exposure
• In people with eGFR >30 ml/min per 1.73 m2 and without evidence of AKI,
metformin need not be stopped before iodinated contrast media (ICM)
administration, and there is no need for testing to evaluate GFR afterward. For
people with AKI or an eGFR ≤30 ml/min per 1.73 m2, it remains appropriate to stop
metformin at the time of or before ICM injection and should not be restarted for at
least 48 hours and only then if GFR remains stable and the ongoing use of
metformin has been reassessed by the clinical team.670
• Consideration of avoiding dehydration for people not undergoing dialysis and who
have eGFR <30 ml/min per 1.73 m2 or AKI and receiving intravenous contrast.671,
672

233

•

•

Use of N-acetylcysteine, ascorbic acid, furosemide, dopamine, fenoldopam, or
calcium channel blockers as preventative measures of CA-AKI has not been shown
to be a consistent benefit.610
Prophylactic pericontrast hemodialysis has been shown to be potentially harmful
and is not recommended.610

Recommendation 4.9.3.1: We suggest not routinely withholding renin-angiotensin
system inhibitors (RASi) before procedures with iodinated radiocontrast administration
in adults (2C).

AF
T

This recommendation places a high value on evidence synthesis of RCTs that have not
demonstrated significant differences in CA-AKI risk between the strategies of withholding or
continuing RASi in the periprocedural period for radiocontrast procedures. A systematic
review of 4 RCTs found no significant difference in CA-AKI incidence following temporary
ACEi or ARBs discontinuation versus continuation in participants undergoing elective
coronary angiography. This recommendation also places value on the potential harms of
inappropriate RASi withdrawal and medication errors that may result from the practice of
routine withdrawal.

D

R

Key information
Balance of benefits and harms
Four trials of withholding RASi were identified, including a total of 2042 participants
who underwent elective coronary angiography and/or a PCI with contrast (Supplementary
Table S77).673-676 There was no significant difference in CA-AKI incidence following
temporary ACEi or ARB discontinuation versus continuation in participants undergoing
elective coronary angiography (OR: 0.76; 95% CI: 0.48–1.19). Given the lack of precision in
the effect estimate, this result does not exclude the potential of a small reduction in CA-AKI
with ACEi or ARB withdrawal. However, potential harms may result from RASi withdrawal,
including exacerbation of heart failure, and medication management errors resulting in
failure to resume these medications for people with guideline recommended indications, such
as heart failure, diabetes, and CKD. However, the trials were generally underpowered to
detect significant differences in harms. It may be appropriate to withhold RASi during periprocedural management of adverse events such as hypotension, hyperkalemia, and AKI,
which requires an individualized rather than systematic approach. When RASi are withheld
due to short term periprocedural adverse events, healthcare professionals should reassess the
appropriateness of resuming treatment and restarting these agents, when possible, to improve
long-term outcomes.
Certainty of evidence
There is low certainty of evidence that, in adults receiving intravascular contrast for
cardiac procedures, there are not differences in incident AKI whether RASi are withheld or
not, with no evidence of differences for other benefits or harms.673-676 For incident AKI, the
234

certainty of evidence was downgraded for methodologic limitations of the studies and for
imprecision. For most other critical outcomes, including poor control of underlying
differences due to withholding RASi, events were rare resulting in imprecise or very
imprecise estimates.

AF
T

Values and preferences
Patient and healthcare professional values and preferences typically focus on
minimizing the risk of AKI while maintaining stable control of chronic conditions like
hypertension, heart failure, or CKD. Many people prioritize avoiding CA-AKI, especially if
they have preexisting abnormal kidney function, and may be open to temporarily stopping
medications if it is clearly explained as a precautionary measure. However, others may be
concerned about destabilizing their BP or worsening heart failure symptoms, particularly if
they have experienced complications from medication changes in the past. Patients also tend
to prefer clear, individualized guidance from their healthcare team, rather than routine or
automatic discontinuation of important long-term medications. Overall, preferences often
lean toward a personalized approach that balances potential kidney risks against the
importance of maintaining cardiovascular and hemodynamic stability with decisions made in
collaboration with trusted healthcare professionals.

D

R

Resource use and costs
Withholding RASi incurs minimal direct costs, as it primarily involves clinical
decision-making and patient counseling rather than additional medications or interventions.
However, indirect resource use can arise from the need for preprocedural medication
reviews, coordination among specialists (e.g., cardiology, nephrology, radiology), and
follow-up to reassess and restart therapy post-procedure. In some cases, discontinuation may
lead to adverse events such as uncontrolled hypertension, fluid retention, or heart failure
exacerbations, which can result in increased healthcare utilization, including emergency
visits or hospital admissions. Additionally, system-level protocols or electronic alerts may be
required to standardize the withholding and reinitiation process, adding to administrative and
information technology resource demands. Therefore, while the act of withholding RASi is
low-cost, the broader resource implications depend on the infrastructure needed to support
this practice.
Considerations for implementation
Implementing this strategy requires careful consideration of patient selection,
workflow integration, and communication across healthcare teams. A key challenge is
identifying which people might benefit from temporary RASi discontinuation, as the
evidence supporting routine withholding is uncertain and the risk-benefit balance varies by
individual factors such as baseline kidney function, cardiovascular and volume status. Clear
protocols and decision-support tools may help to guide healthcare professionals, but rigid
implementation could lead to inappropriate discontinuation in people for whom these
medications are critical for managing heart failure or hypertension. Effective implementation
235

also requires timely medication reconciliation, patient education about holding and restarting
the drugs, and coordination between procedural teams, primary care, and specialists. Without
such systems in place, there is a risk of miscommunication, prolonged interruption of
therapy, or adverse clinical outcomes. As such, a flexible, patient-centered approach with
clinical judgment is essential for safe and effective implementation.

AF
T

Rationale
The routine withholding of RASi such as ACEi or ARBs before exposure to iodinated
radiocontrast has been proposed as a strategy to reduce the risk of CA-AKI, based on the
theoretical concern that these medications may impair kidney autoregulation and exacerbate
contrast-induced nephrotoxicity. However, overall, evidence does not support a clinical
benefit from withholding RASi. While temporary discontinuation may modestly reduce the
risk of transient declines in kidney function in some people at high risk, it also carries
potential harm, including loss of BP control, exacerbation of heart failure, and interruption of
cardiovascular or kidney protection in people with chronic conditions. The balance of
benefits and harms therefore depends on individual risk factors, such as baseline kidney
function, volume status, and cardiovascular stability, supporting a more individualized
approach rather than a standard policy of withholding RASi before contrast exposure.

R

Accordingly, the Work Group suggested against routine withdrawal of these drugs
before elective contrast imaging procedures, due to lack of evidence for benefit with this
strategy and risk of potential harms if broadly applied. This suggestion differs from
statements to hold RASi before elective CT scans in people at risk of CA-AKI provided by
the ACR/NKF Guidelines and included within Practice Point 4.4.1.2 in KDIGO 2024
Clinical Practice Guideline for the Evaluation and Management of CKD.17, 609

D

4.10 Temporary withdrawal of medications to prevent AKI during major surgery
Recommendation 4.10.1: We suggest not routinely withholding renin-angiotensin
system inhibitors (RASi) to prevent AKI in adults having elective major cardiac (2D) or
non-cardiac surgery (2B).
This recommendation places a high value on evidence-based strategies to reduce the risk of
AKI and related perioperative complications following cardiac surgeries. There is evidence
that use of RASi increases the risk of hypotension, but there is no evidence that their use
significantly increases the risk of major perioperative complications or AKI following major
non-cardiac surgery. The effects of RASi use on AKI following cardiac surgery are uncertain.
This recommendation places value on the potential harms of inappropriate RASi withdrawal
that may result from a strategy of routine withdrawal and highlights the need for
individualized decision-making and management.

236

AF
T

Key information
Balance of benefits and harms
Use of RASi in the perioperative period has the potential to impair renal
autoregulation and contribute to hypotension during anesthesia. RCTs have shown a higher
risk of perioperative hypotension in adults who continue versus stop RASi before major
elective non-cardiac surgery; however, these trials have not demonstrated a reduction in
incidence of AKI risk when these agents are held preoperatively. The benefits of withholding
RASi to avoid perioperative hypotension must be carefully weighed against the potential
harms of discontinuation, including exacerbation of underlying conditions such as heart
failure or uncontrolled hypertension, and the risk of rebound hypertension. Thus, decisions
regarding RASi withholding should be individualized, balancing patient-specific
cardiovascular risks against potential kidney benefits, ideally guided by multidisciplinary
perioperative planning.

D

R

Major surgery can be defined as surgery with an expected duration >2 hours from the
surgical incision, and a post-operative hospital stay of ≥3 days.677 A systematic review, with
16 studies of perioperative ACEi or ARB cessation around elective major non-cardiac
surgeries included 8 RCTs that enrolled over 8000 subjects (Supplementarty Table S78).678
Although hypotension in the intraoperative and post-operative period was more common
among people continuing ACEi or ARB therapy compared to those withholding them,
mortality and major post-operative complications were no different with continuing versus
stopping RASi. In 2 RCTs (n=2476 participants) that included AKI as an outcome, the
incidence of AKI was not increased in the group that received perioperative RASi (OR: 1.01;
95% CI: 0.78–1.30) (Supplementary Table S78).678 Specifically, AKI incidence was 11% in
both groups in the “STOP or not STOP” trial by Legrand et al., and 10% in both groups in
the Strategies for Prescribing Analgesics Comparative Effectiveness (SPACE) trial. 679
Further, a published a prespecified secondary analysis of the PeriOperative ISchemic
Evaluation-3 (POISE-3) trial, a partial factorial RCT, compared a “hypotension avoidance
strategy” in which RASi were replaced with alternative antihypertensive agents in the
protocol versus a “hypertensive avoidance strategy” that continued usual antihypertensives
including RASi during major non-cardiac surgery.408 Although less intraoperative
hypotension was reported in the hypotension avoidance group, the incidence of AKI did not
differ between these groups (15.1% vs. 14.4%, RR: 1.05; 95% CI: 0.93–1.19), including
among subgroups with CKD .
It is uncertain based on current evidence whether continuing RASi perioperatively
during cardiac surgery is beneficial or harmful. The current literature has predominantly
included observational studies (Supplementary Table S79),680-682 and there has only been a
single RCT comparing temporary withdrawal versus continuation of RASi during cardiac
surgery that was underpowered for AKI-related outcomes.683

237

Certainty of evidence
There is moderate certainty of evidence that temporarily withdrawing RASi prior to
elective major non-cardiac surgery in adults does not alter the risk of AKI, but also without
evidence of increased risk of poor cardiac outcomes.678 The certainty of evidence for AKIrelated outcomes was downgraded due to imprecision. The certainty of evidence related to
poor control of underlying cardiac conditions was further downgraded due to some
inconsistency, imprecision, and sparse data for some events.

AF
T

There is very low certainty of evidence, from 1 small trial, that temporarily
withdrawing RASi prior to elective cardiac surgery (elective or urgent coronary artery bypass
graft and/or valve surgery) in adults does not alter the risk of AKI-related outcomes or death,
but also without evidence of increased risk of poor cardiac outcomes.683 The certainty of
evidence was downgraded for some methodological limitations, sparse evidence, and highly
imprecise estimates.

R

Values and preferences
Many people value the avoidance of serious complications like AKI, especially if it
could lead to prolonged hospitalization, dialysis, or CKD. As such, they may be willing to
temporarily withhold a familiar medication if there is a reasonable chance of reducing this
risk. However, people with conditions like heart failure or poorly controlled hypertension
may place a higher value on cardiovascular stability and continuity of therapy, expressing
concern about potential decompensation if RASi are withheld. Healthcare professionals,
meanwhile, must balance these patient preferences with clinical evidence, often erring on the
side of caution in stopping RASi in high-risk surgeries or in those with labile BP. Shared
decision-making, guided by a clear explanation of the potential risks and benefits, is crucial
to aligning treatment decisions with individual patient values.

D

Resource use and costs
Withholding RASi prior to major surgery involves relatively low direct costs, as it
typically requires only temporary discontinuation without the need for substitute
medications. However, the broader resource implications can vary. If withholding these
agents reduces the incidence of perioperative hypotension, this may lead to cost savings by
preventing complications such as AKI that require extended hospital stays, intensive
monitoring, or interventions like dialysis. On the other hand, if discontinuation leads to
decompensated heart failure, severe hypertension, or other cardiovascular complications, this
could increase healthcare utilization and associated costs. Additional resources may also be
needed for preoperative assessment, patient counseling, and post-operative monitoring to
manage the risks of withholding therapy. Overall, while the act of withholding is
inexpensive, its downstream effects on resource use and cost are highly dependent on
individual patient risk profiles and surgical complexity.

238

AF
T

Considerations for implementation
Implementing a strategy to withhold RASi before major surgery requires careful
coordination across the perioperative care team, including surgeons, anesthesiologists,
internists, and pharmacists. Clear protocols should be established to identify which people
are appropriate candidates for temporary discontinuation, typically those undergoing highrisk surgeries or with elevated AKI risk, while ensuring that those with contraindications to
withholding, such as heart failure or poorly controlled hypertension, are appropriately
managed. Timing is also critical; RASi are generally withheld 24 hours prior to surgery, and
plans must be in place for timely reinitiation post-operatively based on hemodynamic
stability and kidney function. Patient education is another key component, as people must
understand the rationale for withholding medications, they may have been taking long-term.
Implementation may be facilitated by clinical decision support tools within electronic
medical records to prompt appropriate withholding and follow-up, minimizing variability in
practice and supporting safe, individualized care.

R

Rationale
Current evidence does not support a routine strategy of withholding RASi
perioperatively during major non-cardiac or cardiac surgeries to reduce the incidence of postoperative AKI. However, surgical and anesthetic teams may choose to withhold RASi to
minimize the development and severity of perioperative hypotension by avoiding druginduced vasoplegia, thus reducing increased fluid and vasopressor requirements, and other
complications. These considerations may be more clinically relevant in people undergoing
emergency surgeries, particularly in people who are hemodynamically unstable, have
evolving AKI, or are developing hyperkalemia. Decision making may also differ for people
with reduced heart function. Finally, if these drugs are held for reversible periprocedural
adverse events (AKI, hyperkalemia, hypotension, and hyperkalemia), RASi should be
restarted, when possible, to improve long-term outcomes.

D

4.11 Medication management during intercurrent illness or “sick day protocols”
Recommendation 4.11.1: We suggest temporarily stopping specific classes of drugs in
the context of acute illnesses that predispose to volume depletion, to prevent AKI caused
by hypovolemia (2D).
The Work Group also agrees with the following practice point from Section 4.3 of the
KDIGO 2024 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Evaluation and Management of CKD and
believe it also applies to people with AKI or AKD:17
Practice Point 4.11.1: If medications are discontinued during an acute illness,
communicate a clear plan of when to restart the discontinued medications to
the affected person and healthcare professionals, and ensure documentation
in the medical record.

239

This recommendation places a high value on strategies that can mitigate the adverse effects
of specific medications during acute illnesses that result in hypovolemia and reduced GFR.
RASi, diuretics, and NSAIDs can exacerbate volume depletion, reduce eGFR, and contribute
to the development of AKI. There is also a risk of adverse events from hypoglycemic agents
such as sulfonylureas and metformin when GFR declines. It is therefore rational to
temporarily stop these medications during an acute illness that can lead to AKI. This
recommendation acknowledges there is a lack of evidence of effective implementation
strategies for “sick-day protocols” and potential harms of inappropriate medication selfmanagement. Individualized decision-making, patient counselling, and self-management
strategies are required to implement this recommendation.

R

AF
T

Key information
Balance of benefits and harms
Temporarily stopping certain classes of medications during an acute illness that
predisposes to volume depletion (such as vomiting, diarrhea, or febrile illnesses) may help to
prevent AKI caused by hypovolemia. Medications commonly held in this context include
RASi diuretics, NSAIDs, and metformin, often referred to as part of a "sick day" protocol.
The benefit of this approach lies in reducing the risk of further compromising renal perfusion
or exacerbating volume depletion, which can precipitate or worsen AKI. However, there are
potential harms associated with this strategy. Interrupting these medications, particularly in
people with heart failure, diabetes, hypertension, or CKD may lead to destabilization of
chronic conditions, resulting in uncontrolled hypertension, hyperglycemia, or fluid overload.
Moreover, unclear instructions or inconsistent messaging may lead to inappropriate
discontinuation or delays in restarting therapy, potentially increasing the risk of adverse
outcomes. Therefore, while withholding these drugs during acute volume-depleting illnesses
may protect kidney function, it must be balanced with careful patient education,
individualized risk assessment, and close follow-up to ensure timely reinitiation once the
acute illness resolves.

D

Our systematic review of the use of “sick-day protocols” identified only 1 RCT that
evaluated the effect of an intervention to implement “sick day rules” on the outcome of AKI
(Supplementary Table S80).684 This 6-month trial followed 280 adults with CKD G3–G5
receiving a diuretic, RASi, NSAID, or metformin to management with a sick day protocol
versus standard management.684 The primary endpoint of eGFR change after 6 months did
not differ between groups nor was there any difference in the very low incidence of AKI or
hospitalization over this short follow-up period. Notably, usability testing of the sick-day
protocol revealed frequent errors in protocol use and a high rate of failure by participants in
identifying the correct medications to be withheld. This highlights safety concerns for patient
self-management, including the risk of applying sick-day protocols to inappropriately
classified illnesses, such as heart failure, where holding medications might be deleterious.
Our systematic review also identified 6 trials that evaluated withholding RASi in people with
acute COVID-19 (Supplementary Table S81).685-689 In these trials, withholding versus
240

continuation of RASi similarly failed to demonstrate effects on AKI-related events, mortality,
or other clinical endpoints; although, estimates were mostly very imprecise due to rare
events.

AF
T

Certainty of evidence
There is very low certainty of evidence that stopping various medications in adults
with acute illnesses or COVID-19 does not affect the risk of AKI but may increase the risk of
unplanned hospital admissions.684,685-689 Evaluated medications included RASi, loop, or
thiazide diuretics, mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists, metformin, and NSAIDs. The
evidence for non-COVID-19 illnesses is based on a single study, which was downgraded for
methodological limitations, imprecision, and sparse data. The evidence for COVID-19 was
downgraded primarily for highly imprecise estimates of effect for AKI-related outcomes and
for some methodological limitations and inconsistent findings.

R

Values and preferences
People generally prioritize avoiding serious complications such as AKI, particularly if
they understand the potential for long-term consequences like CKD or dialysis. Many are
willing to make changes to their medications temporarily if clearly advised that doing so can
prevent harm during periods of acute illness. However, people with chronic conditions such
as heart failure, hypertension, diabetes, or CKD may also value stability and continuity in
managing these conditions and may feel anxious about stopping medications they have been
counselled to take regularly. Preferences may vary based on past experiences, health literacy,
and trust in their healthcare professionals. Healthcare professionals must consider these
factors and engage in shared decision-making, providing clear, personalized guidance on
when and how to pause and resume medications, to align care with an individual patient’s
values, goals, and understanding of risk.

D

Resource use and costs
Temporarily stopping medications is a low-cost intervention in itself; however,
effective implementation may incur indirect costs related to the patient education, clinical
monitoring, and follow-up care required. Resources are needed to ensure people understand
when and which medications to pause, including healthcare professional time, educational
materials, or decision-support tools. Inadequate communication can lead to inappropriate
discontinuation or failure to restart therapy, potentially resulting in increased healthcare
utilization due to poorly controlled chronic conditions or adverse drug events. On the other
hand, by preventing episodes of AKI, temporary drug cessation may reduce hospital
admissions, diagnostic testing, and long-term complications, offering potential cost savings
to the healthcare system. Overall, while the intervention is simple and inexpensive, its costeffectiveness depends on coordinated implementation and effective self-management when
attempted in community settings.

241

AF
T

Considerations for implementation
Key considerations for implementation include identifying which medications should
be paused and establishing clear criteria for when to withhold and restart them. Research
suggests that errors in implementing sick day guidance protocols are frequent. Successful
implementation depends heavily on patient education and empowerment, as people must be
able to recognize symptoms of volume depletion (e.g., vomiting, diarrhea, fever) and
understand how to respond appropriately. Healthcare professionals must ensure consistent
messaging across care settings, which can be supported by integrating “sick day rules” into
routine care through printed materials, pharmacist counseling, and digital health tools.
Attention is needed for high-risk populations, such as older adults or those with cognitive
impairments, who may require caregiver involvement. Additionally, systems should be in
place for timely follow-up to reassess medication needs once the acute illness resolves,
minimizing the risk of inappropriate drug dosing or prolonged treatment interruption.

D

R

The PAUSE Preventing Medication Complications During Acute Illness Through
Symptom Evaluation and Sick Day Guidance (PAUSE) Medication Safety Advisory Panel
developed consensus-based recommendations for sick day medication guidance (SDMG)
through a Delphi process. These recommendations (with a ≥80% voter rating in the Delphi
survey) are provided in Table 40 and highlight that SDMG should focus on patient, or
caregiver for children, self-management in conjunction with support from their healthcare
professional.690

242

R

AF
T

Table 40 | Sick day medication guidance for adults
Triggers to initiate sick day medication guidance:
• Vomiting or diarrhea, resulting in significant fluid losses
• Anorexia or nausea, resulting in significant decrease in fluid intake
• New light-headedness, dizziness, or fainting, particularly with sitting or standing up
• Decreased weight (3 kg in 2 days)
• Decreased urine output
When self-management is appropriate:
• There is an absence of severe symptoms
• Patients (and carers) feel they can cope
• Patients can keep up with their fluid intake
Medications that should be temporarily stopped (until symptoms are resolved or up to 3 days,
whichever is earliest):
• Angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors (ACEi)/angiotensin II receptor blockers (ARBs)
• Loop diuretics
• Thiazides/thiazide-like diuretics
• Potassium-sparing diuretics
• Sodium-glucose cotransporter-2 inhibitors
• Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs
• Metformin
• If blood glucose is low, hold insulin/sulfonylurea/meglitinide until blood glucose recovers
• If blood glucose is more elevated than usual, empirical 10%–20% increase in basal and bolus
insulin doses (if unsuccessful at lowering blood glucose, contact healthcare professional)
• Angiotensin receptor neprilysin inhibitors
• Direct rein inhibitors
When assistance/support from healthcare professional should be sought:
• Patients feel they are not coping
• Signs and symptoms have not resolved within 72 hours
• Patients cannot keep up with intake of foods or fluids
• Patients have recurrent low blood glucose readings
• Patients experience significant increase in blood glucose not coming down with selfadjustment after 24 hours
Adapted from Watson et al.690

D

Rationale
The Work Group supports the position that “sick day protocols” that involve
temporarily withholding select medications can be a useful approach,690 as discussed in
Section 4.3 of the KDIGO 2024 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Evaluation and
Management of CKD.17 This may be applied to appropriately selected people with chronic
conditions treated with the following medications: sulfonylureas, ACEi, diuretics, direct
renin inhibitors, metformin, ARBs, NSAIDs, and SGLT2i (often described with the acronym
“SADMANS”).691 However, there is a paucity of evidence of strategies to implement sick
day protocols effectively,684, 687-689 and there are significant safety concerns with previously
described approaches. Healthcare professionals should ensure consistent education and
messaging across care settings and provide adequate self-management support through
printed materials, pharmacist counselling, and digital health tools. Caution applying selfmanagement strategies is warranted in younger or older people and those with cognitive
243

impairment, who may require caregiver involvement. Mechanisms should be in place for
timely follow-up to support people making medication changes, including resuming
medications appropriately once the acute illness resolves.

AF
T

There is potential for harm if people make mistakes in recognizing an acute illness
associated with risk of volume depletion, identifying which drugs are appropriate to stop ,
and when to restart them.692 A failure to restart appropriate medications is a commonly
reported problem. Accordingly, the Work Group endorses Practice Point 4.3.2 from the
KDIGO 2024 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Evaluation and Management of CKD
repeated above17 emphasizing that a plan to restart medications that have been temporarily
discontinued should be clearly communicated to patients and other healthcare professionals.
Documentation of this plan in the medical record may improve consistency and continuity of
care. Patients may additionally benefit from medication review within a month to ensure
appropriate medications are restarted at the correct dose.
4.12. Considerations specific to children
4.12.1 Biomarker characterization of drug-related AKI
Practice Point 4.12.1.1: Urine biomarkers of kidney damage can be helpful in detecting
nephrotoxin-associated AKI in newborns..

D

R

Kidney damage biomarkers may help in identifying AKI in neonates exposed to
multiple nephrotoxic drugs.693 Urinary NGAL can be utilized for AKI detection without the
need for blood draws which contribute to neonatal anemia. It also avoids the interference of
maternal SCr on AKI detection. The negative predictive value of a urine NGAL value ≤250
ng/ml was 96.8% (95% CI: 93.3%–100%) and urine NGAL ≥400 ng/ml demonstrated a
positive likelihood ratio of 2.76 (95% CI: 1.39–4.13) when compared against daily SCr
testing in neonates exposed to nephrotoxic drugs. This suggests that urine NGAL may have a
role for detection in drug-related AKI in neonates, replacing blood draws in those exposed to
nephrotoxic medications. However, more research is needed and the ideal urine NGAL
thresholds require further validation.694
4.12.2. Management of drugs eliminated by the kidney
Practice Point 4.12.2.1: Trends in SCr and urine output can be used to guide dosing of
drugs excreted by the kidney. The validity of KeGFR equations to inform drug dosing
in children is uncertain.
Like in adults, the GFR estimating equations developed for children with CKD are
unreliable in AKI because they assume a steady state of SCr. Similar to equations in adults,
these formulas will overestimate GFR when SCr is rising and underestimate GFR when SCr
is falling. For children with AKI, it is important to consider trends in SCr and urine output
when dosing drugs excreted by the kidney. There is a lack of data on the utility of KeGFR
equations for drug dosing in the pediatric population. Until more evidence is available,
244

D

R

AF
T

healthcare professionals should exercise caution and adjust dosing based on clinical response
and drug levels when possible.

245

CHAPTER 5. RENAL REPLACEMENT THERAPY

AF
T

5.1. Starting renal replacement therapy (RRT)
RRT is an important kidney and organ support intervention for patients with AKI in
modern clinical care. RRT may be required in a substantial proportion of acutely ill patients
with AKI, depending on the population-at-risk and clinical context (e.g., septic shock, liver
failure, cardiogenic shock).695 Recent trends show increasing utilization, particularly in highresource settings.696, 697 Decisions to start and discontinue RRT in acutely ill patients with
AKI are complex and influenced by a range of issues, including overall prognosis (i.e.,
potential for survival, resolution of acute illness, and RRT independence), the relative
balance of risks to benefits, the availability of suitable expertise and resources, and patient
preferences (Figure 14).528

R

Figure 14 | Schematic diagram of RRT continuum. RRT, renal replacement therapy.
Adapted from Ostermann et al.528

D

Practice Point 5.1.1: For children and adults with AKI, a process of informed shared
decision-making should be used with patients, family, caregivers, and healthcare
professionals on the relative merit and risk of treatment with RRT.
An essential consideration for treatment with RRT is the tailoring of decisions based
on the values and preferences of patients and/or surrogate decision makers (e.g., family,
caregiver) through an engaged process of informed, ethically acceptable, shared decisionmaking.698 Shared decision-making can be defined as “a collaborative process that allows
patients, or their surrogates, and clinicians to make health care decisions together, taking into
account the best scientific evidence available, as well as the patient’s values, goals, and
preferences”.698
Shared decision-making is widely endorsed by numerous health care organizations,
including critical care and nephrology.698, 699 Patients/surrogate decision-makers report
greater satisfaction with care when confronted with transparent, consistent, and frequent
246

opportunities to communicate and share in the creation of the care plan with the healthcare
team.700 Shared decision-making can provide greater clarity on the direction of care.701, 702 In
selected circumstances, this may include foregoing RRT or escalations in care. 703, 704
Provision of short-term RRT when benefits are uncertain may involve a time-limited-trial, to
build trust with the patient and/or surrogate decision-makers, strengthen prognostic certainty,
and shape consensus on decision-making for a care plan.705, 706 Time-limited trials can
facilitate a process of shared decision-making and have been shown to improve the quality of
family meetings and reduce resource use, including RRT.707

AF
T

While RRT can be life-sustaining for patients suffering complications of AKI, access
to RRT services can be a challenge, particularly in low-resource settings. Barriers to RRT
include disparate hospital resources, geographic remoteness to a center with dialysis services,
and patient-incurred costs (i.e., inability to pay for any or a sustained period of dialysis).
Similarly, women are disproportionately less likely to access and receive RRT, especially in
low- and middle-resource settings.708, 709 In sub-Saharan Africa, mortality of children and
adults with AKI and indications for RRT but no access (i.e., did not receive) was reportedly
72%–79% and 86%, respectively, far in excess of the observed mortality of 24%–31% and
32%–31%% when RRT was available.709

R

There are numerous validated decision-making strategies (i.e., approaches; tools) that
are available and that can be adapted to facilitate complex decision-making around the
provision of RRT.698 The use of decision-making algorithms may improve consistency and
standardization of approaches and facilitate a framework for shared decision-making.154, 710
Key aspects of such a framework broadly include; i) clarifying available treatment options,
ii) weighing the relative risks and benefits, iii) understanding individual patient values and
preferences, and iv) articulating a schedule to engage in partnered decision-making with the
healthcare team.710

D

Recommendation 5.1.1: For adults with AKI, we recommend a strategy of deferred
timing of RRT initiation as preferred over a strategy of early (pre-emptive) RRT
initiation (1C).
This recommendation places a high value on avoiding the potentially unnecessary burden,
resource use and costs, and potential harms associated with earlier initiation of RRT. There
is low overall certainty of evidence of no differences in mortality, long-term RRT
dependency, or other serious harms with deferred versus early RRT initiation in adults with
AKI. A deferred RRT initiation strategy is based on the emergence of clinical indications for
RRT (medically refractory metabolic or volume-related complications of AKI), rather than
relying solely on creatinine or urine output-based criteria (Table 41). Deferred RRT
initiation requires the capacity for prompt RRT initiation when indicated, and healthcare
professionals should be mindful of local logistical factors when considering RRT timing.

247

Key information
Balance of benefits and harms
All forms of RRT are invasive organ support interventions that carry inherent risks,
which may increase with prolonged use. These complications can arise from the insertion and
ongoing maintenance of dialysis catheters (e.g., bloodstream infection, peritoneal infection),
therapy-specific adverse events (e.g., bleeding, hemodynamic instability, arrhythmias,
electrolyte abnormalities), and long-term adverse outcomes (e.g., delayed or nonresolution of
AKI).

AF
T

The timing of starting RRT in the absence of conventional indications for RRT has
long presented a central dilemma for healthcare professionals (Table 41; Figure 14) and been
the focus of several RCTs in acutely ill adults.124, 128, 711-713 In these RCTs, acutely ill adults
with Stage 2–3 AKI and no conventional indications (e.g., metabolic, toxic or fluid-related
complications) were allocated to an early (“accelerated” or “pre-emptive”) or a deferred
(“standard” or “delayed”) strategy for starting RRT.

D

R

Deferred RRT initiation involves waiting until patients have medically refractory
complications or persistent Stage 2 or 3 AKI exceeding 48–72 hours. Most recent large
multicenter RCTs in adults have largely aligned around this definition of deferred RRT
initiation (alternatively termed delayed, late, or standard initiation),124, 712-714 however, there
has been some variation.128 This variation can present challenges when interpretating the
evidence across trials and applying it to individual patients. For example, of the 231
participants in the Early vs. Delayed Initiation of Renal Replacement Therapy in Critically Ill
Patients with Acute Kidney Injury (ELAIN) trial, 46.8% were enrolled after having
undergone cardiac surgery.128 AKI after cardiac surgery is common and may be associated
with a unique pathophysiology and constellation of complications when compared with other
groups of patients with AKI.715 This may include nuanced differences in the capacity to
tolerate complications related to AKI (e.g., fluid accumulation in the setting of reduced heart
function, electrolyte/metabolic abnormalities in a setting of increased risk of arrhythmias).
Systematic reviews have focused on synthesizing evidence on timing of initiation of RRT in
participants after cardiac surgery.716, 717 These have included relatively few RCTs128, 718, 719
and observational studies with high risk of bias.720-724 While pooled analysis implied there
may be benefit to earlier RRT initiation in participants after cardiac surgery with AKI, this
evidence has very-low to low certainty.

248

D

R

AF
T

• Anticipated solute burden (tumor lysis syndrome;
rhabdomyolysis; intravascular hemolysis)
• Limited physiological reserve to tolerate the consequences
of AKI (i.e., demand-supply mismatch, severe non-kidney
comorbidities)
• Inability to provide target nutritional support due to fluid
restriction
Relative contraindications
• Low likelihood for benefit (futile prognosis)
• Receiving palliative care and/or approaching end-of-life
• High likelihood of non-recovery of kidney function in
patients who are not long-term dialysis candidates
Adapted from Ostermann et al.725 AKI, acute kidney injury; CKD, chronic kidney disease; ICU,
intensive care unit; KDIGO, Kidney Disease: Improving Global Outcomes; pCO2, partial pressure of
carbon dioxide; RRT, renal replacement therapy.

No differences in 90-day mortality, 90-day dialysis dependency, length of stay (ICU
and hospital), and serious adverse events were found between early versus deferred RRT
initiation (Supplementary Table S82).125, 726-728 This finding would imply that a deferred
strategy to starting RRT, characterized as a “watch and wait” strategy, where RRT is delayed
until confronted with worsening, persistent or medically refractory complications of AKI in
acutely ill patients, can be recommended as the default standard.
In a substantial proportion of those participants allocated to the strategy of deferred
RRT initiation across these RCTs, RRT was not started (range 10%–49%), due either to
kidney recovery or death, potentially contributing to lower resource use and costs of care.124,
128, 712, 713 However, in the Initiation of Dialysis Early Versus Delayed in the Intensive Care
Unit (IDEAL-ICU) trial, 17% of participants with septic shock allocated to the deferred RRT
249

initiation strategy did receive emergent RRT for medically refractory complications within
48 hours of randomization.124 This observation reinforces the need for regular reassessment
of the patients and also the further need for development and validation of clinical risk
prediction tools (e.g., diagnostic tests, dynamic assessments, etc.) to improve real-time
identification of patients most likely to derive benefit from starting RRT earlier.171, 180, 729

AF
T

It is further plausible that accumulation and persistence of AKI-related complications
may exert the risk for adverse outcomes.730 In the Artificial Kidney Initiation for Kidney
Injury 2 (AKIKI-2) trial, acutely ill participants with persistent Stage 3 AKI without urgent
RRT indications were allocated to either start RRT immediately (aligned with the “delayed”
strategy in the Artificial Kidney Initiation in Kidney Injury [AKIKI] trial) or further deferral
(“more-delayed”), starting RRT only for a serum urea >50 mmol/l or an urgent conventional
indication.125 While there was no difference in the number of RRT-free days at 28 days
between the groups (12 days for delayed vs. 10 days for more-delayed, P=0.93), in a
prespecified analysis, 60-day mortality was higher in the more-delayed strategy compared to
the delayed-strategy (55% for more-delayed vs. 44% for delayed; adjusted HR: 1.65; 95%
CI: 1.09–2.50; P=0.07; Supplementary Table S83).125 In addition, participants who were in a
coma at randomization had a lower chance of transitioning from coma to awakening and
remained comatose for longer.731 These findings support the concept that prolonged delays
should be avoided within a deferred RRT initiation strategy.

R

Individual studies provide further insights not captured in the formal syntheses. In the
AKIKI trial, the earlier RRT initiation strategy conferred a higher risk of catheter-related
bloodstream infections compared with a standard RRT initiation strategy (10% vs. 5%,
P=0.03).712 In the Standard (deferred) versus Accelerated (early) Initiation of RenalReplacement Therapy in Acute Kidney Injury (STARRT-AKI) trial, more people receiving
the earlier RRT initiation strategy experienced adverse events (23.0% vs. 16.5%, P<0.001),
including hemodynamic instability and electrolyte abnormalities (e.g., hypophosphatemia).

D

Finally, there may be important differences between specific groups of patients that
have not been the specific focus or have been underrepresented in RCTs (e.g.,
neonates/children732; cardiogenic shock733; solid-organ transplantation734, 735; extracorporeal
life support [ECLS]736), where there may be differences in the development of medically
refractory complications, differences in tolerating these complications, and/or differences in
relative indications for starting RRT and potential benefits with early or deferred initiation.
Certainty of evidence
The certainty of evidence in adults for the comparison of early versus standard (or
delayed) RRT is low.125, 726-728 The certainty of evidence was downgraded for imprecision
across outcomes, with inconsistency across studies pertaining to mortality and reporting
issues pertaining to adverse events, with sparse data for several outcomes.

250

One trial with methodological concerns provided very low certainty of evidence for
comparison of delayed (standard) versus more-delayed (very late) RRT.125, 726-728 The trial
had mostly very imprecise estimates of effect, but did find a significantly higher risk of death
with more-delayed RRT.
Values and preferences
Survivors of critical illness treated with RRT report impaired quality-of-life (QoL)
and function (e.g., new or worsened disability).737-740 This recommendation placed high value
on the potential for avoidance of RRT when applicable.

AF
T

Resource use and costs
RRT represents an escalation in the complexity of care and is both resource intensive
and costly.741 In many low-resource settings, receipt of RRT is paid out-of-pocket, putting
patients at risk of accelerated economic decline. A deferred strategy for RRT initiation may
translate into a meaningful proportion of people with moderate-to-severe AKI averting use of
RRT and lead to concomitant reductions in resource use and costs.742

R

Considerations for implementation
The adoption of a deferred strategy for RRT initiation is likely suitable for most
acutely ill adults with moderate-to-severe AKI. However, variation in case-mix (e.g.,
cardiorenal syndrome, hepatorenal syndrome, post-cardiac surgery, concomitant organ
support with ECLS), and rapid changes in illness acuity and clinical status, and timeliness
and availability of essential resources (e.g., vascular access insertion, dialysis equipment,
staffing) may influence decision-making. Sound clinical judgement that considers these and
other local contextual factors is essential and requires frequent reassessment, especially in
rapidly evolving clinical scenarios. Adherence to guidance on RRT initiation can be
integrated into best practice advisories and monitored through quality improvement
methodology.743

D

Rationale
The decision to start RRT can be complex, given the need to balance perceived
benefits and risks, the escalation in resources (e.g., patient transfer, dialysis disposables,
human resources), and the importance of shared decision-making that integrates the values
and preferences of patients. A fundamental consideration for healthcare professionals when
considering whether to start RRT is to ensure clarity in defining the objective(s) and the
goal(s) of therapy. Initiation of RRT is appropriate for acutely ill patients with complications
attributed to or worsened by AKI, especially if refractory to medical therapy (e.g.,
hyperkalemia, metabolic acidosis, fluid overload), whose preferences and goals of care are
aligned with receipt of RRT. In the absence of urgent indications for RRT, the evidence
supports a strategy of deferred timing of RRT initiation over early RRT initiation.

251

Practice Point 5.1.2: For children and adults with AKI and high probability of
requiring RRT, planning for RRT should be considered before indications are met,
including, where applicable and available, transfer to centers with nephrology and RRT
services.
Observational studies imply delayed referral to and consultation with specialist
nephrology services for selected patients with AKI may portend greater risk for poor
outcome, including the risk of life-threatening complications of AKI and need for emergency
dialysis.744-747 There is uncertainty whether this may relate to the availability of specialist
nephrology services, including the availability of RRT for acutely ill patients.708
Nephrologist referral and consultation provide opportunity for long-term continuity of care,
follow-up for recognized complications associated with AKI and planning of RRT.748

AF
T

Practice Point 5.1.3: For children and adults with AKI and medically refractory fluid,
electrolyte, acid-base, and/or metabolic complications (e.g., uremia), initiate RRT if
consistent with patient preferences and goals of care.
There is long-standing general acceptance for the acute initiation of RRT among
patients with AKI and medically refractory complications independent of SCr values. 608, 749
RRT in this context is beneficial by effectively treating acutely reversible complications
(e.g., hyperkalemia; metabolic acidosis; uremia; fluid overload) and offering opportunity for
AKI to resolve. Delay or failure to provide therapy for refractory complications of AKI can
portend a high risk for mortality.124, 711, 730, 750

R

Practice Point 5.1.4: Among patients with hepatic encephalopathy (grade ≥2) due to
acute or acute-on-chronic liver failure, initiation of continuous RRT (CRRT) to manage
hyperammonemia may be considered even when conventional RRT indications are
absent.

D

CRRT can effectively clear ammonia, may reduce cerebral edema, and may be
associated with a reduction in mortality among patients with hepatic encephalopathy due to
acute or acute-on-chronic liver failure.751-754 The Work Group concurred with the guidance
on the use of CRRT among patients with acute liver failure that were included in the 2023
American Society of Gastroenterology Acute Liver Failure Guidelines.755
Practice Point 5.1.5: For critically ill children and adults with AKI, a time-limited trial
of RRT can be started if uremic and/or metabolic complications are plausibly
confounding neurological evaluation or chances of awakening from coma.
In a secondary analysis of the AKIKI-2 trial, among those people with coma at
enrollment, allocation to the more-delayed strategy conveyed lower likelihood of awakening
and fewer days awake compared with the delayed strategy.731 These findings provide
252

AF
T

evidence of the potential risks from complications of persistent severe AKI with longer
delays to RRT. A time-limited trial of RRT may be used to facilitate the evaluation and
management of potentially modifiable/reversible complications of AKI (Figure 15).
Furthermore, a time-limited trial enables an evaluation of the goals of treatment in line with
shared-decision making.

Figure 15 | Proposed algorithm for initiation RRT in critically ill patients with AKI.
AKI, acute kidney injury; RRT, renal replacement therapy. Adapted from Ostermann et al.756

R

Practice Point 5.1.6: For children and adults with AKI, particularly those receiving
vasoactive support, identify and treat modifiable factors and mitigators of
hemodynamic instability at the commencement of RRT.

D

Hemodynamic instability during RRT occurs with relative frequency among acute
and critically ill children and adults and may portend greater risk of adverse outcomes,
including mortality and non-recovery of kidney function.757-761 Hemodynamic instability
commonly occurs upon circuit connection and start of RRT.757, 759, 762 Patients with greater
illness acuity, those receiving vasoactive support, patients exposed to high ultrafiltration rates
and neonates and children (where the extracorporeal volume of the circuit is relatively large
in proportion to total blood volume) may be more susceptible.762 Table 44 lists potential RRT
related interventions to limit hemodynamic instability during RRT.
5.2. RRT modality
RRT modalities include CRRT, intermittent RRT (IRRT) including the spectrum
from conventional intermittent hemodialysis (IHD) to prolonged intermittent RRT (PIRRT),
and peritoneal dialysis (PD). All modalities have a role in AKI but vary in their spectrum of
benefits and potential complications (Table 42).763

255

Practice Point 5.2.1: The initial selection of RRT modality in children and adults with
AKI, including CRRT, intermittent RRT (IRRT), or acute peritoneal dialysis (PD),
should integrate patient characteristics, clinical context, local expertise, and resource
availability.

AF
T

There is a spectrum of RRT modalities used to support acute and critically ill patients
with severe AKI who develop indications for RRT. These RRT modalities include
extracorporeal support with CRRT and IRRT as well as acute PD. RRT modalities are
characterized by differences in application (e.g., access, treatment schedules, dosing) and
mechanisms of solute clearance and ultrafiltration (Tables 42 and 43). There are variable
practices across clinical settings and geographic regions for the selection of RRT modality
for treatment of patients with AKI, due to differences in patient characteristics, clinician
expertise and preferences, institutional/organizational factors, and resource availability.
Further, patients may transition from one modality to another, for instance from CRRT to
IRRT. The ideal timing and clinical circumstances for RRT modality transition are uncertain
but are also influenced by patient characteristics and organizational factors.
Recommendation 5.2.1: For critically ill adults with AKI and hemodynamic instability,
we suggest using CRRT or acute PD over conventional IRRT (2C).

R

This recommendation places a high value on the advantages of greater hemodynamic
stability reported with continuous forms of RRT, including CRRT and acute PD, over
conventional intermittent RRT. The recommendation is focused on the selection of RRT
modality for patients with hemodynamic instability because it may exacerbate kidney damage
and impair or prolong resolution of AKI. It places a high value on the shorter duration of
RRT and ICU stay among patients treated with acute PD over conventional intermittent RRT.
This recommendation places a lower value on the greater resource use and costs of CRRT
compared with conventional intermittent RRT.

D

Key information
Balance of benefits and harms
CRRT, IRRT, and PD have specific advantages and potential complications.
Compared with IRRT, CRRT and PD enable more gradual ultrafiltration and solute removal
and may be particularly advantageous in patients with hemodynamic instability. (Table 43)
A large network meta-analysis found no difference in all-cause mortality, time on
RRT, recovery of kidney function, or length of hospital and ICU stay when comparing CRRT
with IRRT (Supplementary Table S84).764 However, studies included in these pooled
estimates comprised heterogeneous populations with varying levels of hemodynamic
instability (Supplementary Table S82). Moreover, many studies comparing CRRT with IRRT
excluded participants with hemodynamic instability because of the perceived risk of harm in
randomizing a hemodynamically unstable patient to IRRT. Although the available pooled
256

evidence suggested no differences in benefits or harms when comparing CRRT and IHD, the
majority of panel members perceived that the theoretical risk of harm with IRRT outweighed
the increased incremental cost with CRRT and, therefore, favored CRRT for critically ill
patients with hemodynamic instability.
The same network meta-analysis found that compared with IRRT, acute PD reduced
the length of time on RRT and ICU stay (with large apparent treatment effects), with no
difference in all-cause mortality or recovery of kidney function (Supplementary Table
S85).764 Collectively, this body of evidence suggests important potential benefits with no
apparent risk of harm, recognizing that acute PD may not be available in all care settings.

D

R

AF
T

There have been few small, largely single center RCTs765-770 and systematic
reviews771, 772 that have compared the efficacy and outcomes of acute PD versus
extracorporeal RRT, including CRRT and IRRT, for critically ill participants with AKI.
Their findings have been largely supported by real-world evidence from observational
studies.773-775 One single center RCT compared acute PD to CRRT in 70 adults with sepsisassociated AKI.766 While participants allocated to acute PD had worse outcomes, the
technique for acute PD was not aligned with current guideline-directed standards (e.g., use of
rigid catheters, use of manual exchanges and open drainage, acetate buffered solutions). 776
More recent RCTs have suggested that acute PD can achieve similar and acceptable efficacy
and outcomes when compared with extracorporeal RRT; and PD may be associated with less
costs and resource use (Supplementary Tables S85 and S86).765, 767-769, 777
