258

Certainty of evidence
The certainty of evidence among critically ill adults for selection of initial RRT
modality for the treatment of AKI is mostly low to moderate for comparisons of different
pairs of modalities. The comparison of CRRT versus IRRT has moderate certainty of
evidence,764 with downgrading mortality and recovery of kidney function due to
methodological concerns of the studies and downgrading of time on RRT due to imprecision.
The comparison of IHD and PD has low certainty of evidence,764, 765 with
downgrading of mortality and recovery of kidney function for methodological concerns and
sparseness of studies, but upgrading since network meta-analysis was concordant with the
findings of the single study; time on RRT was downgraded for a lack of direct comparisons
(conclusion was based on network meta-analysis), but with upgrading for a large effect size.

AF
T

The comparison of CRRT versus PD also had low certainty of evidence,764 with
downgrading of time on RRT for methodological concerns, downgrading of mortality for
both methodological concerns and inconsistency across studies, and downgrading of
recovery of kidney function for methodological concerns, inconsistency, and imprecision.
The certainty of evidence for time on RRT was moderate, but the overall assessment was low
certainty of evidence since the certainty for mortality was low.

D

R

Values and preferences
This recommendation recognizes the high value and importance that patients and
their surrogate decision-makers convey to evidence-based practices that foster resolution of
AKI, shorter duration of RRT, and recovery to RRT independence. Resolution of AKI has
implications for risk of long-term dialysis dependence, which can contribute to impaired
quality-of-life, social disruption, loss of work productivity and independent living, and can
be perceived as a burden to patients, including adults who commenced RRT during
childhood, caregivers and surrogate decision-makers.740, 778, 779 Achieving freedom from RRT
was perceived as important to patients and associated with greater health-related quality-oflife.780-782
Resource use and costs
RRT is generally resource intensive and expensive. Acute care RRT programs,
particularly CRRT or RRT in ICU settings, generally demand specialized training and
infrastructure, which may not be widely available. Studies have suggested IRRT may be
associated with lower costs,783, 784 due largely to greater disposable costs for CRRT (e.g.,
circuits, anticoagulation, replacement solutions).763 However, RRT-specific costs are contextspecific and variable and further depend on local procurement and practices.763 Acute PD is
more cost effective compared with continuous or intermittent RRT, particularly when
supported by evidence of shorter duration of RRT and ICU length of stay.785-787 Acute PD is
a suitable and important option for selected populations (e.g., children with AKI) and in lowresource settings where CRRT and IRRT may not be available.771, 776, 788, 789
259

AF
T

Considerations for implementation
While there is a spectrum of RRT modalities that can be employed to support acutely
and critically ill patients with severe AKI with indications for RRT, the real-world
implementation of a spectrum of modalities can be challenging. The key factor influencing
the availability of selected RRT modalities is equitable access to resources, where low-to
middle-resource settings may have limited access to some forms of RRT or, more
specifically, to more expensive and resource intensive RRT modalities, or may not have
available resources or infrastructure.788, 790 This may necessitate procedures to organize
transfer of acutely ill children or adults, if feasible, to settings where acute RRT services can
be provided (Practice Point 5.1.2). The Saving Young Lives program has focused on enabling
provision of simple acute PD (low cost, no electricity or complex equipment) for acutely ill
patients in low-resource settings.788 This has included education, training, and mentoring of
healthcare professionals and serves as a model to broaden access to acute PD globally in lowresource settings.790 Several practice points for the provision of acute PD have been
integrated into the 2020 International Society of Peritoneal Dialysis (ISPD) clinical practice
guidelines.776 Implementation of the Saving Young Lives or similar programs should integrate
monitoring and data capture aligned with quality improvement methodology.791

R

Rationale
Evidence from RCTs has shown no differences in mortality or RRT dependence with
intermittent RRT, including PIRRT), versus CRRT; however, not all comparative trials have
been restricted to treatment of patients with hemodynamic instability, and some trials
excluded such patients. Evidence from RCTs has shown a shorter duration of RRT with acute
PD versus CRRT or IHD, with no differences in mortality or RRT dependence.

D

Healthcare professionals providing RRT for AKI should appreciate the advantages
and limitations of each modality and tailor treatment according to the specific needs of each
patient. RRT modality choice should also be informed by the available resources and local
expertise.
Practice Point: 5.2.2: CRRT is preferable to conventional IRRT for critically ill
children and adults with AKI and acute brain injury or other causes or risks of
increased intracranial pressure (ICP) or cerebral edema.
Patients with acute brain injury or cerebral edema may be susceptible to secondary
insults that can further compromise cerebral perfusion, exacerbate cerebral edema, and
extend existing injury,792-794 especially in the penumbra surrounding the acute injury where
brain tissue may be recoverable. Patients treated with RRT, particularly IRRT, are
susceptible to intradialytic hypotension and rapid shifts in serum solute concentration and
osmolality.795 Treatment with IRRT in patients with acute brain injury can alter ICP and
provoke new or worsening cerebral edema, the degree of which correlates with the frequency
260

of therapy and ultrafiltration.796 CRRT may be better suited to mitigate rapid fluctuations in
solute clearance or fluid removal and reduce the risk of exacerbating cerebral edema. Indeed,
acute changes in brain density consistent with new or worsening cerebral edema have been
shown to be more likely among patients receiving conventional IHD compared with
CRRT.797 Alternatively, PD may serve as a suitable option for selected adult patients with
brain injury, particularly if there are contraindications to systemic anticoagulation. There is
limited evidence that has directly compared CRRT and PIRRT in patients with brain injury
or acute liver failure and the risk of disequilibrium syndrome and worsening neurological
profile. In circumstances where CRRT or acute PD is not feasible or available, the
prescription for RRT should be carefully adapted to minimize the risk of intradialytic
hypotension and rapid solute clearance associated with shifts in serum osmolality (Table 44).

AF
T

Practice Point 5.2.3: For children and adults with AKI, particularly those requiring
vasoactive support, identify and treat modifiable factors and mitigators of
hemodynamic instability during RRT.

R

Hemodynamic instability during RRT occurs frequently among acute and critically ill
children and adults and may portend greater risk of adverse outcomes, including mortality
and non-recovery of kidney function.757-761 Hemodynamic instability commonly occurs upon
circuit connection and start of RRT.757, 759, 762 Patients with greater illness acuity, those
receiving vasoactive support, patients exposed to high ultrafiltration rates, and neonates and
children (where the extracorporeal volume of the circuit is larger in proportion to total blood
volume) may be more susceptible.762 The use of a passive leg raise (PLR) test in adults and
other dynamic hemodynamic measures can further aid in predicting the risk of intradialytic
hypotension prior to initiation of IRRT.798-800

D

Greater hemodynamic tolerance to IRRT in acute and critical care settings may be
achieved by using strategies to mitigate intradialytic hypotension (Table 44).761, 801 These
strategies can be applied to acute or critically ill patients with AKI upon initiation of IRRT or
following transition to IRRT from CRRT. They have largely been adapted from measures to
prevent intradialytic hypotension among children and adults with kidney failure receiving
maintenance dialysis.802, 803
The risk of hypotension can be further addressed by discontinuation of all
vasodilators or antihypertensive medications and pre-emptive fluid administration before
initiation of RRT. Hyperoncotic albumin solutions may be used in patients with
hypoalbuminemia.804 A further trial is evaluating the role of albumin supplementation to
mitigate the risk of intradialytic hypotension among critically ill adults receiving RRT.805

261

AF
T

R

Practice Point 5.2.4: Prolonged IRRT (PIRRT) is an acceptable alternative to CRRT as
the initial RRT modality for hemodynamically unstable, critically ill adults with AKI.

D

PIRRT refers to intermittently applied RRT for periods longer than conventional IHD
(e.g., 6–12 hours). PIRRT, often referred to as SLED or extended daily dialysis (EDD), aims
to realize the theoretical benefits of both CRRT and conventional IHD while mitigating risks,
particularly intradialytic hypotension (Table 44).806-808
Practice Point 5.2.5: Transition from CRRT to IRRT can be considered when children
and adults who have not recovered from AKI are physiologically and metabolically able
to tolerate IRRT and the risks of intracranial hypertension or cerebral edema, if
applicable, are resolved.
Approaches to transition of patients between RRT modalities during phases of organ
function recovery, particularly CRRT to IHD, have undergone limited evaluation in critically
ill patients with AKI .809-811 A period of receipt of IHD is common following CRRT,
occurring in 36%–50% of instances, usually after hemodynamic stability has been achieved
and care is focused on rehabilitation and mobilization.809, 810 Several patient factors predict
intolerance to transition to IRRT and IHD, including hypoalbuminemia, ongoing fluid
262

accumulation, oliguria, use of vasopressors, and use of mechanical ventilation, which can
guide decision-making about the appropriate timing. 761
5.3. RRT dose-intensity
Recommendation 5.3.1: We recommend delivering an effluent volume of 20-25 ml/kg/h
over higher volumes for solute clearance in adults receiving CRRT, or a Kt/V of 3.9 per
week over higher urea clearance in adults receiving IRRT or PIRRT for AKI (1B).

AF
T

This recommendation places a high value on minimizing harms and unnecessary resource
use and costs associated with augmented (higher) RRT dose-intensity to achieve greater
small solute clearance. While earlier RCTs implied there may be a survival benefit with
augmented RRT dose-intensity in critical illness, more recent RCTs, including 2 large
multicenter RCTs and several systematic reviews have found no improvement in outcomes
with augmented (higher) RRT dose-intensity of solute clearance over standard dose-intensity
in acute and critically ill participants with AKI. Current evidence suggests that standard
dose-intensity may contribute to fewer days of RRT compared with receipt of higher RRT
dose-intensity, with no differences in mortality or long-term RRT dependence.

D

R

Key information
Balance of benefits and harms
This recommendation has not changed since the KDIGO 2012 Guideline.608 The
current evidence demonstrates that augmented (higher) RRT dose-intensity (e.g., CRRT
dose-intensity ≥35 ml/kg/h; daily sessions of SLED/IRRT targeting a Kt/V ≥1.2) does not
improve outcomes compared with a lower RRT dose-intensity (e.g., CRRT dose-intensity
20–25 ml/kg/h or thrice weekly of SLED/IRRT targeting a Kt/V ≥1.2), in terms of improved
survival or greater likelihood of resolution of AKI and RRT independence. Moreover,
augmented RRT dose-intensity may contribute to RRT-associated morbidity. Secondary
analyses of RCTs have suggested that augmented RRT dose-intensity may propagate oliguria
and extend total dialysis treatment time.812, 813 Augmented RRT dose-intensity may increase
the risk of cardiac arrhythmias, contribute to hypophosphatemia and electrolyte losses, 814, 815
and increase the risk of delayed weaning from invasive mechanical ventilation, 815, 816 or risk
of reintubation.814, 816 These findings suggest there is no incremental benefit to augmented
RRT dose-intensity beyond the lower standards used in prior RCTs.812, 815, 817-820
Several small retrospective studies from Japan have evaluated the association of
lower-than-standard CRRT dose-intensity (CRRT dose-intensity 10–20 ml/kg/h) with
metabolic control and outcomes.821-825 These studies have suggested that lower CRRT doseintensity can be tolerated and achieve comparable efficacy for electrolyte, acid-base, and
metabolic control as the current standard; however, the comparative effectiveness of this
lower dose-intensity on clinical outcomes has not been proven in RCTs. In select
jurisdictions (e.g., Japan), lower RRT dose-intensity is routinely delivered and

263

recommended.821-825 There are ongoing RCTs evaluating whether lower RRT dose-intensity
can impact survival and resolution of AKI and dialysis dependence.826-828
Certainty of evidence
Based on evidence current for the KDIGO 2012 Guideline,608 there was moderate
certainty of evidence overall, with moderate certainty regarding mortality, which was
downgraded for inconsistency across studies, and high certainty of evidence for RRT
dependence (duration of RRT or to kidney function recovery)

AF
T

Values and preferences
This recommendation recognizes the importance of adopting the standard RRT doseintensity to minimize harm, reduce practice variation, and mitigate adverse effects that may
be incrementally associated with augmented RRT dose-intensity. The current standard RRT
dose-intensity may contribute to fewer days of RRT compared with receipt of higher RRT
dose-intensity, which is perceived as important to patients and associated with greater healthrelated quality-of-life.780-782
Resource use and costs
Augmented RRT dose-intensity is a low-value practice that is currently not supported
by evidence. Moreover, the routine use of excess RRT dose-intensity may contribute to the
risk of adverse events, greater use of RRT disposables, higher bedside workload, increased
healthcare costs, and a higher burden on the environment. As such, the current standard RRT
dose-intensity will use less resources and translate into relatively lower costs.

D

R

Considerations for implementation
Abundant evidence suggests RRT dose-intensity is poorly monitored and susceptible
to wide practice variation.713, 829, 830 The real-world implementation of RRT dose-intensity
standards should align and be integrated with quality improvement initiatives aimed at
establishing and sustaining high-quality acute RRT programs.743, 791, 831-833 RRT doseintensity is an important quality and performance indicator of acute RRT adequacy.834 In a
single centre quality improvement initiative, Griffin et al. aimed to standardize CRRT doseintensity and reduce practice variation.832 The primary outcome was percentage of weekly
CRRT delivered that was concordant with the guideline-directed standard (20–25 ml/kg/h).
The baseline concordance was 33%; however, following implementation, compliance
increased to 66% over a near two-year period with a substantial reduction in week-to-week
variation.
Rationale
RRT dose-intensity is focused on small solute clearance and the correction of
complications related to derangements in electrolytes, acid-base balance, or metabolic waste
products. The optimal RRT dose-intensity for acutely ill patients with AKI across the
spectrum of illness is unknown. Further, the methods used for RRT dose quantification in
264

patients with AKI have several limitations and have not been fully validated in this
population.
RCTs to date have generally compared augmented (higher) and standard (lower) RRT
dose-intensity regimens.815, 819, 835-837 While earlier RCTs implied there may be a survival
benefit with augmented RRT dose-intensity in critical illness,836, 837 subsequent RCTs,
including 2 large multicenter RCTs and several systematic reviews have found no
improvement in outcomes with augmented RRT dose-intensity in acute and critically ill
participants with AKI.812, 815, 817, 818, 820, 835 While most RCTs have predominantly included
participants receiving CRRT, 3 RCTs had significant proportions receiving IRRT or focused
solely on IRRT.820, 838-840

AF
T

In all these RCTs that focused on RRT dose-intensity, dosing regimens (augmented
vs. standard) have generally been fixed and not adapted or modified after initial stabilization
of acid-base, electrolyte, or metabolic derangements. Whether a strategy of personalized
RRT dose-intensity modification can favorably impact clinical outcomes is unknown.
Practice Point 5.3.1: An initial CRRT dose-intensity for adults with high body mass
index (BMI; e.g., ≥30 kg/m2) based on ideal or adjusted body weight, rather than actual
body weight, can be used to avoid delivery of excess CRRT dose-intensity.

D

R

To date, the preferred reference “weight” (i.e., actual, adjusted, ideal) to calculate the
prescribed RRT dose-intensity across the spectrum of BMI encountered in clinical practice is
uncertain. In the Veterans Affairs (VA)/National Institute of Health (NIH) Acute Renal
Failure Trial Network (ATN) trial, premorbid obesity was considered in the CRRT doseintensity calculation.820 In non-obese participants, the CRRT dose-intensity prescription was
“based on the weight before the onset of acute illness”; however, in obese participants,
defined as a weight exceeding 30% ideal body weight, the prescription was adjusted,
calculated as ideal body weight plus 25% of the difference between ideal and actual weight
before the onset of acute illness. In addition, participants weighing >120 kg were excluded.820
In the Randomized Evaluation of Normal versus Augmented Level (RENAL) trial, the
prescribed CRRT dose-intensity was “based on the patient’s body weight at the time of
randomization” and participants weighing <60 kg or >100 kg were excluded.815
A recent retrospective cohort study of 1033 critically ill patients receiving CRRT
evaluated the association between measures of body weight (i.e., actual, adjusted, ideal),
CRRT dose-intensity, and outcome.841 Ideal body weight was calculated using the Devine
formula (males: 50 kg + [2.3 kg × each inch over 60 inches]; females: 45.5 kg + [2.3 kg ×
each inch over 60 inches]).842 Adjusted body weight was calculated as ([actual body weight–
ideal body weight] × 0.4) + ideal body weight.841 The cohort was stratified by BMI ≥30
kg/m2 and CRRT dose-intensity was further indexed by actual body weight, adjusted body
weight and ideal body weight. The mean (standard deviation [SD]) CRRT dose-intensity
265

varied significantly across measures of body weight, particularly among patients with BMI
≥30 kg/m2 (Table 45).
Table 45 | Summary of varying CRRT dose-intensity stratified by BMI and body weight
calculation
CRRT dose-intensity (ml/kg/h)
BMI <30 kg/m2
BMI ≥30 kg/m2
Actual body weight, (mean [SD])
30 (9)
27 (7)
Ideal body weight, (mean [SD])
37 (10)
48 (14)
Adjusted body weight, (mean [SD])
33 (9)
36 (9)
p<0.001 for each comparison. BMI, body mass index; CRRT, continuous renal replacement therapy;
SD, standard deviation. Adapted from Peters et al841

AF
T

There was suggestion of effect modification of CRRT dose-intensity for mortality by
measures of body weight. Among obese patients, higher CRRT dose-intensity calculated by
actual and adjusted body weight was associated with greater mortality; however, there was
no association with mortality when ideal body weight was used.843
These observations have implications for CRRT dose-intensity prescribing among
patients who are obese (BMI ≥30 kg/m2) and/or have actual body weights exceeding 100–120
kg, who were not represented in landmark trials guiding CRRT dose-intensity.815, 820 For
patients with a high BMI, the calculation of the initial prescribed CRRT dose-intensity based
on ideal or adjusted body weight rather than actual body weight may avoid delivery of excess
CRRT dose-intensity.841 The CRRT dose-intensity can thereafter be adjusted for adequacy
and correction of electrolyte, acid-base and metabolic (e.g., nitrogenous waste) derangements.

R

Recommendation 5.3.2: We recommend not prescribing high-volume hemofiltration
(HVHF) compared with standard CRRT dose-intensity for critically ill adults with AKI
in the setting of sepsis or septic shock (1B).

D

This recommendation places high value on avoiding potential harms and costs in the absence
of any evidence of benefit with HVHF. Compared with standard CRRT dose-intensity (total
effluent fluid volume 20-25 ml/kg/h), HVHF (total effluent fluid volume ≥45 ml/kg/h) does not
improve mortality or kidney recovery, or reduce serious adverse events and may increase the
risk of metabolic complications and excessive clearance of nutrients and medications.
Key information
Balance of benefits and harms
Pooled evidence from systematic reviews of RCTs has found no evidence for benefit
of HVHF compared with standard CRRT dose-intensity for any clinical outcomes, including
mortality and kidney recovery.844, 845 A small RCT of 20 critically ill participants with septic
shock randomly allocated to CRRT at 35 ml/kg/h or 65 ml/kg/h found no significant
difference in any clinical outcome measured.846 In a single center RCT in China, 280
critically ill participants with sepsis and AKI were randomly allocated to receive CRRT at 50
266

ml/kg/h (HVHF) or 85 ml/kg/h (extra HVHF).847 There was no evidence of differences in
survival or resolution of AKI between the 2 HVHF groups, respectively. In the multicenter
hIgh VOlume in Intensive Care (IVOIRE) trial, 140 critically ill participants with septic
shock and AKI were randomly allocated to 96 hours of CRRT at 70 ml/kg/h (HVHF) or 35
ml/kg/h.848 The trial was terminated prematurely for futility with no evidence for differences
in early improvement in organ function or organ support or in the primary outcome of 28-day
mortality. In the Hemofiltration to Rescue Severe Shock following Cardiac Surgery
(HEROICS) trial, focused on 224 post-cardiac surgery participants with vasoplegic shock, of
whom >40% were receiving ECLS, randomization to a strategy of 48 hours of CRRT at 80
ml/kg/h (HVHF) followed by standard CRRT compared with standard of care (where only
57% received CRRT) did not improve time to shock resolution, AKI resolution, or
survival.718

AF
T

Systematic review of RCTs has found no evidence for benefit of HVHF compared
with standard CRRT dose-intensity for any clinical outcomes, including mortality and kidney
recovery (Supplementary Table S87).814, 817, 849-852 Twelve trials conducted in critically ill
adults consistently found no significant difference between intensive and less intensive
(standard) CRRT dose for 30-day mortality (RR: 0.95; 95% CI: 0.85–1.05), with similar
findings among trials that defined intensive CRRT as either ≥40 ml/kg/h (RR: 0.98; 95% CI:
0.86 –1.11) or ≥45 ml/kg/h (RR: 0.93; 95% CI: 0.83–1.06). Smaller numbers of trials also
found no significant differences in likelihood of 30-day complete resolution, or hospital or
ICU length of stay. A single trial reported no significant differences in serious adverse
events.851

D

R

In addition to lack of benefit, there is concern that exposure to HVHF may be
associated with risk of adverse events, including the undue clearance of electrolytes,
nutrients, and medications (e.g., antimicrobials). In the HEROICS trial, more participants in
the HVHF arm experienced hypophosphatemia, metabolic alkalosis, and
thrombocytopenia.718 Moreover, those allocated to the HVFH group had longer median
durations of RRT (5 days vs. 2 days, P<0.0001).
Certainty of evidence
There was moderate certainty of evidence for lack of efficacy of HVFH versus
standard dose-intensity CRRT in adults. The certainty of evidence was downgraded for
mortality and complete resolution for some methodological concerns across studies. The
certainty of evidence for serious adverse events was very low due to methodological
concerns and sparseness of evidence. Sub analyses of studies comparing either HVHF with
≥45 or ≥40 ml/kg/h were further downgraded for imprecision.
Values and preferences
This recommendation places high value on avoiding a low-value practice, both from
the perspective of the patient and the healthcare system, where HVHF has not been proven to
267

improve clinical outcomes, may be associated with uncertain risk, and likely contributes to
greater health resource use and associated costs.
Resource use and costs
The use of HVHF can plausibly increase bedside workload for nursing and other
personnel due to the need for more frequent changes to replacement/dialysate solution bags
and added requirements for electrolyte supplementation. This translates into greater costs for
disposables, medications, and nursing time.

AF
T

Considerations for implementation
The practice of prescribing higher than guideline-directed CRRT dose-intensity
remains common despite no evidence of improvement in outcomes.713, 829, 830 There is no role
for HVHF in routine clinical practice beyond enrollment in a RCT in critically ill patients
with shock states.
Rationale
Several RCTs have explored whether HVHF can more rapidly restore organ
dysfunction (i.e., hemodynamic stability) and improve outcomes.718, 846-848, 853, 854 These trials
have largely focused on critically ill patients with sepsis or following cardiac surgery. Based
on currently available evidence, there is no rationale for treating patients with HVHF outside
a clinical trial.

R

Practice Point 5.3.2: The adequacy of the RRT dose-intensity to correct for electrolyte,
acid-base, and metabolic (e.g., nitrogenous waste) derangements should be reviewed
daily for CRRT and prior to starting each RRT session for IRRT and the prescription
adjusted when needed.

D

Review of a patient’s clinical, physiological, and laboratory values, including
checking for resolution of AKI, is important before prescribing RRT. For patients who are
receiving CRRT or PD, these variables can be reviewed daily (or more often).791, 834 For
patients receiving IRRT, these variables can be reviewed prior to each scheduled session.
CRRT dose-intensity, a surrogate for small solute clearance, is represented by total
effluent volume (i.e., replacement fluid + dialysate fluid + ultrafiltration) normalized for
patient weight and treatment time (Table 46).855

268

Table 46 | Summary of calculation of CRRT dose-intensity across CRRT modes855
CRRT mode
Calculation of total effluent volume rate (ml/h)
CVVH
Sum of total replacement fluid rate (ml/h) + fluid removal rate (ml/h)
CVVHD
Sum of dialysate fluid rate (ml/h) + fluid removal rate (ml/h)
CVVHDF
Sum of replacement fluid rate (ml/h) + dialysate fluid rate (ml/h) + fluid
removal rate (ml/h)
Total replacement fluid rate = prefilter replacement fluid rate (ml/h) + postfilter replacement fluid
rate. Calculation of the dilution due to prefilter replacement fluid = plasma flow rate (ml/h)/(plasma
flow rate [ml/h] + prefilter replacement fluid rate [ml/h]). CRRT, continuous renal replacement
therapy; CVVH, continuous venovenous hemofiltration, CVVHD, continuous venovenous
hemodialysis; CVVHDF, continuous venovenous hemodiafiltration.

AF
T

Evidence from clinical trials, observational studies, and surveys of practice have
found that the delivered RRT dose-intensity for solute clearance is often lower than the
prescribed dose.815, 839, 856-861 This discrepancy can be attributed to reduction in hemofilter
efficiency and function over time, which may correlate with increases in transmembrane
pressure indicating filter clogging. Other reasons include therapy disruptions from alarms
that suspend the blood flow pump (e.g., catheter dysfunction or high-pressure alarms),
scheduled changes to replacement solutions, hemofilter/circuit loss due to clotting or
scheduled change, and other unplanned interruptions (e.g., downtime for diagnostic imaging;
transfer to operating theatre, etc.). For these reasons, the KDIGO 2012 Guideline
recommended that a higher effluent volume should be prescribed to deliver the recommended
effluent volume.608

D

R

In IRRT, Kt/V is a measure used to quantify dialysis adequacy, defined as the blood
clearance of urea (K), multiplied by the treatment time (t), divided by the volume of
distribution for urea in the body (V). The urea reduction ratio (URR) has also been used as a
measure of adequacy of an IRRT session, defined as the percentage difference between the
blood urea concentration before and after a dialysis session. Early clinical studies in patients
receiving maintenance dialysis found a dose-gradient relationship with mortality, whereby
increasing dose-intensity increased survival to an upper boundary of a Kt/V 1.3 and URR
70%, beyond which no further improvement was evident.862, 863 Clinical practice guidelines
for maintenance hemodialysis recommend delivering a Kt/V 1.2 thrice per week. 864 In AKI,
the recommended dose-intensity for IHD is extrapolated from these recommendations but
has undergone limited validation.862
Discrepancy between prescribed and delivered dose-intensity has also been shown in
patients receiving IRRT. The lower delivered than prescribed dose-intensity is associated
with use of temporary vascular catheters necessitating reduced blood flow rates, and no
anticoagulation use contributing to filter clotting.
Recent advances in IRRT platforms have enabled online clearance monitoring. 865
Online clearance monitoring uses conductivity measurements of the dialysate to estimate
Kt/V, by measuring the difference in conductivity between the dialysate entering and exiting
269

the dialyzer, which is related to the concentration of sodium ions (as a surrogate for urea).
Although online clearance monitoring can provide a non-invasive, real-time method for
assessing IRRT adequacy, the Kt/V estimated by this method has been shown to
underestimate the delivered Kt/V.865, 866
Practice Point 5.3.3: When using acute PD for adults with AKI, deliver a weekly Kt/V
of 2.2 (18-24 l PD fluid/day) over higher doses.

AF
T

The Work Group concurs with the guidance on prescribing and achieving adequate
clearance in acute PD that were specifically addressed in the 2020 updated International
Society for Peritoneal Dialysis (ISPD) guidelines for PD in AKI (Adults).776 Acute PD
delivered at a dose-intensity targeted to achieve a weekly Kt/V of 2.2 (18-24 l PD fluid per
day) can achieve comparable metabolic control (i.e., urea, creatinine, bicarbonate, potassium,
sodium concentrations), duration of therapy, and mortality to that of extracorporeal RRT and
higher intensity PD.

R

In a single center RCT from 2008, Gabriel et al. compared high-volume PD (36-44 l
PD fluid/24 hours–delivered Kt/V 3.6) with daily IHD (Kt/V 4.7) in 120 critically ill
participants with AKI.768 Metabolic control (i.e., urea, creatinine, bicarbonate, potassium,
sodium, glucose) and mortality [58% vs. 53%, P=0.71] was similar; however, participants
treated with high-volume PD had shorter duration of therapy (5.5 days vs. 7.5 days, P=0.02)
and earlier resolution of AKI (7.2 days vs. 10.6 days, P=0.04) compared with daily IHD. An
RCT comparing lower dose-intensity PD (18-24 l PD fluid/day during the first 2 days;
delivered weekly Kt/V of 2.11) with IHD thrice per week (delivered weekly Kt/V of 2.81)
found no difference in outcome. A multicenter RCT compared acute lower dose-intensity PD
(18–24 l/day) with IRRT among participants with AKI and found no significant differences
in 28-day mortality or RRT-free survival between groups.765

D

Several studies have also applied lower-volume PD with acceptable results.867-870 In a
single center RCT, Ponce et al. randomly allocated 61 critically ill participants to high (Kt/V
0.8 per session) versus low dose-intensity PD (Kt/V 0.5 per session).869 Metabolic control,
duration of therapy, and mortality were similar, implying a lower Kt/V target could be
applied. This translates into lower fluid volumes use, fewer resources, and lower costs. In
another single center RCT, 75 acutely ill participants with AKI were allocated either to lower
dose-intensity PD (<20 l PD fluid/day; delivered Kt/V 2.26) or higher dose-intensity PD (>30
l PD fluid/day; delivered Kt/V 3.30) using a single-bag open system (1.5 l PD fluid) for the
first 2 days.870 There were no significant differences in metabolic control, hospital mortality
(72% vs. 63%, P=0.8), or resolution of AKI; however, those allocated to higher doseintensity PD had a trend towards more peritonitis (15.3% vs. 8.3%, P=0.34). Whether acute
PD is as effective as standard extracorporeal RRT dose-intensity among patients with marked
hypercatabolic or shock states with splanchnic hypoperfusion is unknown.

270

873

AF
T

Delivering a standard dose-intensity of acute PD is a feasible, safe, and comparably
effective form of acute RRT that can extend treatment options for patients with AKI in lowresource settings or among selected populations where conventional extracorporeal RRT is
less feasible or practically challenging. If acute PD is the only option available to patients
with severe AKI confronted with severe electrolyte, acid-base, metabolic, and fluids
complications, it can be lifesaving.788, 790, 871-873 Using standard dose-intensity acute PD also
necessitates fewer resources and can realize cost savings when compared to extracorporeal
RRT and higher dose-intensity PD that requires greater volumes of PD solutions and more
intensive nursing support for delivery.777, 867 In general, acute PD requires less infrastructure
(i.e., water; electricity), simpler training for healthcare personnel, and can be performed
using less resources and costs.777, 867 This makes acute PD using standard dose-intensity an
appealing choice for implementation in low-resource settings, where it has been proven
feasible and effective through initiatives such as the Saving Young Lives program.788, 790, 871-

Practice Point 5.3.4: In adults with AKI, the initial PD prescription, including duration
of cycle time and glucose concentration of PD fluid, may need to be modified to rapidly
correct acid-base, electrolyte, metabolic, or fluid complications in adults with AKI.

D

R

The Work Group concurred with the guidance regarding the initial prescription and
delivery of acute PD among adults with AKI that was detailed in the 2020 updated ISPD
guidelines for PD in AKI (Adults) (Figure 16).776 In adults with AKI, the initial PD
prescription, including, duration of cycle time and glucose concentration of PD fluid, may
need to be modified to rapidly correct acid-base, electrolyte, metabolic, or fluid
complications in adults with AKI, particularly in critically ill or hypermetabolic patients. To
achieve target fluid removal during the initial prescription, variable glucose-containing PD
fluid and/or shorter cycle times may be used. The cycle time can then be extended and
modified based on patient and clinical circumstances (4-6 hours).

271

AF
T

Figure 16 | Suggested algorithm for use of acute PD among adult patients with AKI.
AKI, acute kidney injury; HS, Hartmanns solution; MRL, modified Ringers lactate; PD,
peritoneal dialysis. Reproduced from Cullis et al776.

D

R

Automated PD using 25 l/day with 70% tidal volume in adults with AKI is
approximately equivalent to a CRRT effluent volume of 23–24 ml/kg/h. This estimate of
dose equivalence was derived from a single RCT including 125 critically ill adults with AKI
in whom tidal acute PD (25 l PD fluid/day; fill volume 2.0 l; tidal volume 70%) was
compared with continuous venovenous hemodiafiltration (CVVHDF; prescribed total
effluent fluid rate 30 ml/kg/h).767 Survival (69.8% vs. 46.8%, P<0.01), AKI resolution
(60.3% vs. 35.5%, P<0.01) and infectious complications (9.5% vs. 17.7%, P<0.01) were
significantly better in those allocated to acute tidal automated PD compared with CVVHDF.
The delivered CVVHDF dose-intensity was 23.5 ml/kg/h.608, 776
5.4 Catheters for RRT
Practice Point 5.4.1: Vascular access for RRT, including type, size, length, and site of
catheter placement, should consider feasibility and weigh the risks of catheter
colonization/infection or bleeding with the desire to maintain catheter/circuit patency in
children and adults.

272

The selection of dialysis catheters for acute RRT in critically ill children and adults
should be guided by a balance between the feasibility of insertion, the risk of complications
(e.g., catheter colonization, infection, and bleeding), and the need to maintain circuit patency
and achieve adequate blood flow. In acute settings, nontunneled, uncuffed catheters are
recommended due to their ease of insertion, immediate usability, and suitability for shortterm use. This is particularly relevant in ICU settings, where rapid initiation of therapy is
often required. Cuffed tunneled catheters are not typically necessary unless prolonged RRT
access is anticipated or a transition to chronic maintenance dialysis is planned for patients
with AKI superimposed on advanced CKD.608, 820
The right internal jugular vein is preferred for catheter placement in both children and
adults.874 This site provides the most direct route to the superior vena cava and right atrium,

AF
T

reducing the risk of catheter malfunction, kinking, and malposition.875 Additionally, the right
internal jugular vein is associated with lower rates of catheter-related bloodstream infection
and thrombosis compared to other sites.876
The subclavian vein should be avoided, particularly in adults, due to the high risk of
central venous stenosis, which can compromise future vascular access options. This is critical
for patients who may later require longer-term or chronic maintenance dialysis.820, 877 In
children, central venous stenosis also occurs after central line placement, but reported rates
are generally lower and are influenced by factors such as the number and type of catheters,
device size, and prior central venous access exposure.878-880 Femoral access may be
considered when internal jugular access is not feasible, although it carries a higher risk of
infection, particularly in obese or immobilized patients.877, 881

D

R

For adults, catheters of at least 12 French (F) in size are recommended to allow
sufficient blood flow rates (ideally ≥200–250 ml/min) and reduce the risk of circuit clotting
or inadequate RRT delivery.608, 820 Adequate blood flow is essential for effective solute
clearance, ultrafiltration, and anticoagulation management. Catheter length should be tailored
based on insertion site to ensure the catheter tip reaches the cavoatrial junction for right or
left internal jugular approach and inferior vena cava for femoral access. Peres et al. proposed
formulas based on patient height and insertion site to estimate optimal catheter length, which
were later validated by Czepizak et al. with 90%–97% accuracy.882, 883 Optimal lengths
include 15 cm for right internal jugular placement, 20 cm for left internal jugular, and 20–25
cm for femoral placement (Table 47). Correct tip placement minimizes recirculation, reduces
the risk of mechanical dysfunction, and ensures efficient RRT delivery.
There are no universal French size cutoff values for pediatric dialysis catheters. Size
selection should be proportional to patient weight and vessel diameter to ensure adequate
blood flow while minimizing the risk of vascular injury (Table 48).884, 885

273

Confirming proper catheter tip position is generally recommended, ideally at the
cavoatrial junction for internal jugular catheters or within the inferior vena cava for femoral
access. While chest roentgenography remains the most used method, emerging techniques
such as ultrasound-guided tip visualization or intracardiac electrocardiogram-guided can
enhance tip confirmation in select settings.886-889

Practice Point 5.4.2: Adoption of standardized care bundles for dialysis catheter
insertion and maintenance can reduce the risk of complications.

D

R

Uncuffed non-tunneled dialysis catheters are essential for timely initiation of acute
RRT, but they carry a risk of complications and dysfunction that may impair RRT
effectiveness and negatively impact patient outcomes.875 These complications are broadly
classified into early and late events and are often preventable with use of standardized care
bundles for catheter insertion and maintenance.892, 893 Early catheter dysfunction typically
occurs within the first hours to days following insertion and is most often due to technical
factors, such as suboptimal catheter placement, incorrect positioning of the catheter tip, or
mechanical kinking.875, 894 Misplacement may lead to inadequate blood flow, significant
recirculation, or failure to deliver adequate dialysis dose-intensity. The use of ultrasound and
real-time imaging guidance, along with strict adherence to insertion protocols, can
substantially reduce these risks.886-889
Late catheter dysfunction commonly results from intrinsic or extrinsic obstruction,
including thrombus formation at the catheter tip or within the vessel, and fibrin or biofilm
sheath formation. These obstructions impair circuit flow and often necessitate catheter
exchange.875 Catheter-related bloodstream infections represent a major late complication,
contributing to increased morbidity, prolonged hospitalization, and mortality.895

274

The KDOQI 2019 Clinical Practice Guidelines for Vascular Access strongly advocate
for the implementation of care bundles, which are structured sets of evidence-based practices
that, when performed collectively and reliably, have been shown to improve outcomes. 875
These bundles typically include use of maximal sterile barriers during insertion,
chlorhexidine for skin antisepsis, daily catheter site assessment, proper hub disinfection,
securement techniques, and timely removal of unnecessary catheters. Implementation of such
standardized care bundles has been demonstrated to reduce catheter-related infections,
improve catheter function, and extend catheter survival (Table 49).895, 896

AF
T

In addition to care bundles, catheter lock solutions are being increasingly explored as
adjunctive measures to reduce catheter-related infections. A recent meta-analysis found that
citrate locks showed a trend toward lower infection and complication rates compared to
heparin or saline.897, 898 Taurolidine-heparin lock solution was associated with a 71% risk
reduction in catheter-related bloodstream infections compared to heparin alone, although
most current evidence is extrapolated from evidence from studies conducted in patients with
tunneled or long-term catheter placement for maintenance dialysis.899, 900

D

R

Table 49 | Summary of the elements in the standard care bundle for non-cuffed dialysis
catheters875
Component
Recommendation
Catheter site selection
Prefer right internal jugular vein for insertion; avoid subclavian vein
to prevent central vein stenosis.
Ultrasound guidance
Use real-time point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS) to guide catheter
insertion and confirm placement.
Aseptic technique
Strict maximum sterile barrier precautions during insertion.
Catheter securement
Use sutures or securement device to minimize movement and reduce
infection risk.
Dressing protocol

complications.

Practice Point 5.4.3: Use of existing arteriovenous fistulas/grafts may be suitable for
IRRT in children and adults to avoid new dialysis catheter placement.
Practice Point 5.4.4: It is preferable to avoid using existing arteriovenous (AV)
fistulas/grafts for CRRT in children or adults, due to concern for needle dislodgement
or arteriovenous fistulae/graft damage with prolonged cannulation.

275

In the acute care setting, vascular access choice is a vital component of safe and
effective RRT. While existing AV fistulas or AV grafts are preferred for maintenance
hemodialysis due to their durability and lower risk of infection, their role in short term RRT,
particularly CRRT, is more nuanced. For IRRT in hemodynamically stable children and
adults, the use of a well-functioning, mature AV fistula/grafts may be appropriate. This
approach avoids the insertion of a temporary dialysis catheter, thereby reducing the risk of
catheter-associated complications such as infection, bleeding, thrombosis, and venous
stenosis.875, 901 This is especially relevant in patients who are already established on
maintenance hemodialysis or who have a functioning vascular access created in anticipation
of maintenance dialysis initiation.

R

AF
T

In contrast, the routine use of AV fistula/grafts for CRRT is generally not
recommended due to several concerns. The prolonged cannulation necessary for CRRT poses
a significant risk of needle dislodgement, particularly in critically ill, sedated, or agitated
patients who may move unpredictably. The mechanical stress from 24-hour continuous blood
flow through large-bore dialysis needles may contribute to vessel wall trauma or damage to
the anastomosis site, especially in newly created or maturing accesses. Unnikrishnan et al.
showed that venous needle placement during IHD dramatically increases turbulent shear near
the venous anastomosis, wall shear stress fluctuations increased from ~0.1 m/s to 0.4–0.7
m/s, 2 cm downstream of the needle tip, with turbulence intensities 5–6 times higher,
indicating this may contribute potential endothelial injury and intimal hyperplasia and lead to
AV graft complications.902 Existing AV fistula/grafts were not designed for extended
cannulation duration, and frequent manipulation may increase the risk of infection or access
failure, which could jeopardize long-term dialysis options.903 In addition, safe use of AV
access for CRRT would require continuous bedside supervision.904 For these reasons, dialysis
catheters remain the preferred access for CRRT in both children and adults.

D

Practice Point 5.4.5: The decision on how to replace poorly performing uncuffed
nontunneled dialysis catheters should be individualized. The options include a new
venipuncture or, in select cases, a guidewire exchange of the existing dialysis catheter.
The performance of uncuffed nontunneled dialysis catheters is vital for the effective
delivery of RRT. Inadequate blood flow rates or frequent circuit alarms can compromise
dialysis efficiency, lead to treatment interruptions, and increase the risk of thrombosis and
infection.905 When dysfunction is confirmed and cannot be corrected through flushing,
repositioning, or thrombolytic therapy, the catheter should be replaced. This may involve
either a new venipuncture at a different site, which is preferred when feasible to minimize the
risk of infection and mechanical complications, or a guidewire exchange of the existing
catheter, which can be considered in select circumstances, particularly when vascular access
is limited or when the patient is at high procedural risk.876, 906-908

276

The 2011 Centers for Disease Control (CDC)/ Healthcare Infection Control Practices
Advisory Committee (HICPAC) guidelines recommend caution with guidewire exchanges
for central venous catheters, including dialysis catheters, due to the potential for increased
infection risk.909 When infection is suspected, the catheter should be completely removed,
and if further access is required, a new catheter should be inserted at an alternative
anatomical site.

AF
T

Guidewire exchange may be appropriate in selected cases of non-infectious
dysfunction, particularly when vascular access is limited or new venipuncture carries
elevated risk.876, 906, 908 In such cases, the procedure should be performed using strict aseptic
technique. Additionally, if infection is suspected, the removed catheter tip should be
submitted for microbiological analysis to aid diagnosis and guide treatment.877, 909 Across all
scenarios, adherence to standardized sterile protocols during catheter removal, replacement,
or exchange remains essential to minimize infection risk and ensure safe vascular access
management.875, 896
Practice Point 5.4.6: Do not perform routine or scheduled change of dialysis catheters
to reduce the risk of catheter-related infection.

D

R

Multiple RCTs and observational studies have demonstrated that prophylactic
catheter changes do not reduce infection rates compared to leaving catheters in place until no
longer needed or a complication arises.877, 910 Moreover, routine scheduled replacement
introduces additional procedural risks, including mechanical complications such as arterial
puncture, hematoma/bleeding, or pneumothorax, particularly in patients with coagulopathy;
loss of available vascular access sites, especially in patients with limited central venous
access options; and greater procedural burden and healthcare costs.876, 909 These potential
harms are particularly significant in critically ill patients, who often have impaired
coagulation and require multiple concurrent therapies through central venous access.
However, there are context-specific scenarios in which catheter replacement may be
warranted. For example, femoral catheters may pose a higher infection risk, particularly in
obese patients or those with elevated BMI, due to anatomical and hygiene-related factors.874,
881 In such cases, early catheter replacement or site change may be appropriate, depending on
duration of catheter use, patient mobility, and findings from infection surveillance.
Ultimately, catheter replacement decisions should be individualized, guided by clinical
assessment of risk factors, catheter site, patient characteristics such as BMI and mobility, and
presence of complications.874, 881, 911 A strategy that prioritizes clinical indications over
routine replacement is aligned with evidence-based practice and promotes patient safety.875
Practice Point 5.4.7: In circumstances where prolonged indications for RRT are
anticipated, a cuffed tunneled catheter can be considered when initiating RRT for AKI,
particularly for children.

277

Non-tunneled, uncuffed catheters are not ideal for long-term use. In clinical situations
where prolonged RRT is anticipated or where preservation of central venous access is
particularly important, the placement of a cuffed tunneled dialysis catheter may be
appropriate, even in the acute setting. Cuffed tunneled catheters offer several advantages
when extended use is expected. They are associated with lower rates of catheter-related
bloodstream infections, owing to the presence of a subcutaneous tunnel and a Dacron cuff
that facilitates tissue ingrowth and serves as a barrier to bacterial migration. 875, 911
Additionally, these catheters provide greater mechanical stability and reduce the risk of
accidental dislodgement, which is especially beneficial in children.912

AF
T

In children, the need to preserve central venous access is essential due to smaller
vessel size and fewer available access sites. Repeated catheter replacements in children
imposes risk and can present technical challenges, including increased likelihood of central
venous stenosis, and cumulative procedural burden.913 Therefore, early placement of a
cuffed, tunneled catheter should be considered when resolution of AKI is uncertain or
prolonged RRT is anticipated. Nonetheless, the decision to insert a tunneled catheter must
consider the patient’s clinical stability, procedural feasibility, and institutional resources,
such as access to interventional radiology or surgical/interventional radiology teams. In
unstable patients or in settings lacking fluoroscopic or surgical support, the use of a
temporary non-tunneled catheter may still be necessary initially, with a plan for conversion
once feasible.

R

Practice Point 5.4.8: For patients who remain dialysis dependent with low probability of
resolution of AKI or patients being prepared for hospital discharge, transition to a
cuffed tunneled catheter should be undertaken.

D

In the context of AKI, a subset of patients will remain RRT dependent and continue
to require RRT at the time of hospital discharge. Prolonged use of a non-tunneled, uncuffed
dialysis catheter has been linked to higher rates of catheter-related bloodstream infections,
greater risk of mechanical complications, and poor long-term catheter function.875, 877
Therefore, once it becomes evident that a patient will remain dialysis-dependent with a low
likelihood of resolution of AKI in the short-term, or when discharge planning begins,
transitioning to a cuffed tunneled catheter should be considered. These catheters offer greater
stability and securement, reducing the risk of accidental dislodgement, and enhanced
durability, making them suitable for outpatient dialysis or as a bridge to permanent access
(i.e., AV fistula/graft). Furthermore, their use preserves central veins and minimizes longterm complications in patients who may progress to long-term dialysis.914
5.5. Anticoagulation for RRT
Recommendation 5.5.1: Where available, we recommend using regional citrate
anticoagulation rather than heparin in children and adults receiving CRRT who do not
have contraindications to citrate (1B).
278

Evidence shows that premature filter clotting can interrupt therapy, reduce efficacy, and
increase bedside workload and costs of CRRT. As such, this recommendation places a high
value on preservation of circuit patency to ensure optimal CRRT delivery, while minimizing
the risk of circuit loss. It also places high value on balancing the benefits of anticoagulation
with the risk of adverse events due to anticoagulation, including bleeding and need for blood
transfusions. Note this recommendation applies to patients who are not receiving systemic
anticoagulation for other indications (e.g., venous thromboembolism, atrial fibrillation).

AF
T

Key information
Balance of benefits and harms
The use of anticoagulation during CRRT for patients with AKI who are not already
receiving systemic anticoagulation improves and preserves circuit patency and extends filter
lifespan, which reduces treatment interruptions and associated complications. In patients with
high bleeding risk, anticoagulation with unfractionated heparin may pose a significant
hazard.

R

A systematic review of 6 RCTs comparing regional citrate anticoagulation (RCA)
with unfractionated heparin (UFH) showed that UFH increased the risk of both premature
filter clotting (RR: 1.70; 95% CI: 1.09–2.65) and bleeding events (RR: 3.09; 95% CI: 2.08–
4.60) with no evidence of a difference in discontinuation due to other adverse events
(Supplementary Table S88).915 Another systematic review of 3 RCTs comparing RCA with
regional UFH showed no difference in the risk of filter clotting (RR: 1.01; 95% CI: 0.61–
1.67) with an uncertain effect on bleeding risk (RR: 1.82; 95% CI: 0.23–14.43;
Supplementary Table S89).915

D

RCTs investigating low molecular weight heparin (LMWH) versus RCA showed that
use of RCA greatly decreased the risk of major bleeding and discontinuation due to adverse
events, without evidence of a difference in filter clotting (Supplementary Table S90).915, 916
In a network meta-analysis, RCA in critically ill participants was associated with
significantly longer filter life (weighted mean difference [WMD] +23 hours; p<0.001) and
lower bleeding risk than both UFH and no anticoagulation.915
For circuit anticoagulation in children, systematic review of 6 RCTs comparing RCA
with UFH concluded that use of RCA reduced the risk of systemic bleeding (RR: 0.80; 95%
CI: 0.35–1.85) compared with UFH, with uncertain effects on filter clotting (Supplementary
Table S91). A different systematic review evaluated 1706 pediatric CRRT sessions across 11
studies, of which 38% utilized RCA and 62% used systemic UFH.917 Regional citrate
anticoagulation was consistently associated with significantly longer circuit life and a
reduced risk of clotting, with a pooled risk difference for filter clotting of –20.4% (95% CI: –
14.4% to –26.5%) compared to UFH. Although survival rates did not differ significantly
279

across most studies, RCA demonstrated a more favorable profile in reducing the need for
circuit changes and red blood cell transfusion.918-921
Certainty of evidence
There is moderate certainty of evidence, in adults, that, compared with RCA,
systemic UFH increases the risk of both premature filter clotting and bleeding events, with
no evidence of a difference in discontinuation due to other adverse events. The certainty of
evidence was downgraded due to methodological limitations, but the certainty for the
bleeding outcome was upgraded due to a large effect size.

AF
T

There is low certainty of evidence that regional UFH, in adults, has no difference in
the risk of filter clotting, with an uncertain effect on bleeding, compared with RCA. The
certainty was downgraded for methodological quality and imprecision of estimates of effect.
There is also high certainty of evidence that, compared with LMWH, RCA greatly
decreases the risk of major bleeding and discontinuation due to adverse events, with very low
certainty of evidence regarding a difference in filter clotting. There were methodological
limitations of the studies, but the certainty for harm from bleeding and for discontinuation
due to adverse events were upgraded due to large estimates of effect. Given the high certainty
of evidence for adverse events, but very low certainty of evidence of benefits, overall, it was
determined that there is low certainty of evidence regarding RCA versus LMWH.

R

There is very low certainty of evidence for the comparison of RCA and regional UFH
in children. Certainty of evidence was downgraded for methodological limitations,
sparseness of studies, imprecision, and inconsistencies pertaining to filter clotting.

D

Values and preferences
The Work Group perceived that both patients and healthcare professionals would
prefer anticoagulation strategies that preserve circuit patency to ensure adequate solute
clearance, ultrafiltration, mitigation of interruptions, and minimization of adverse events
(e.g., bleeding events, metabolic complications; need for transfusion). Preservation of circuit
lifespan will also reduce bedside nursing workload, result in fewer dialysis catheter
manipulations, enable delivery of the prescribed RRT dose-intensity, and reduce the amount
of waste. At the same time, there was a strong perception for preferentially using an
anticoagulation regimen that would minimize the risk of bleeding events, particularly among
patients with increased risk (e.g., liver disease; recent surgery; coagulopathy;
thrombocytopenia, etc.). This demands that healthcare professionals balance the relative
benefits of anticoagulation (e.g., longer filter lifespan) with the potential for adverse events.
Among those at high bleeding risk, use of anticoagulant strategies with a lower risk of
bleeding or no anticoagulation may be preferable, despite the expectation of shorter circuit
lifespan. Preferences may also be shaped by institutional expertise, availability of specific
anticoagulants, clinician experience with protocols, and ability to implement appropriate
280

monitoring procedures.922 For example, RCA may be preferred in centers with adequate
training and infrastructure, whereas UFH remains commonly used due to its familiarity and
relative ease of administration.
Among pediatric intensivists and nephrologists, the preference for RCA is
particularly strong due to the frequent use of small circuits and low blood flow rates, which
predispose to clotting when no or suboptimal anticoagulation is used.923-925

AF
T

Resource use and costs
The use of anticoagulants in CRRT has important implications for resource utilization
and healthcare costs, particularly in lower resource settings. Prolonging circuit life through
effective anticoagulation reduces the frequency of circuit changes, which can in turn lower
the consumption of CRRT consumables, reduce nursing workload, and reduce waste. 922 Each
circuit replacement not only incurs material costs but also requires significant clinician time
and interrupts treatment delivery. Regional citrate anticoagulation, while associated with
longer filter lifespan and improved circuit patency, requires additional resources due to more
complex protocols, including clinician education and monitoring of circuit, factors that may
limit its implementation in some institutions.926 Unfractionated heparin remains widely used
due to its familiarity, ease of administration, and relatively low costs, but higher rates of
circuit failure and bleeding-related complications may increase downstream costs, including
transfusions and circuit replacement.916 This is very relevant, in particular in low-resource
settings, where costs associated for CRRT may be limited.

D

R

Regional citrate anticoagulation, though more complex to administer than UFH, may
ultimately offer favorable resource utilization by prolonging filter lifespan, reducing circuit
changes, and minimizing bleeding-related complications. Evidence supports that RCA
increases filter life by 17–23 hours on average compared to UFH, which translates into fewer
interruptions, reduced nursing workload, and less blood loss.915, 924, 927-930 This will reduce
need to replace circuits, for blood transfusions, and emergent catheter manipulations, all
important cost drivers for CRRT.
Considerations for implementation
The successful implementation of anticoagulation protocols in CRRT is influenced by
institutional infrastructure, local expertise, monitoring capabilities, and drug availability. 931
Regional citrate anticoagulation results in superior filter life and bleeding risk reduction, 915,
922 but requires dedicated protocols, calcium and acid-base monitoring, and trained personnel
to manage metabolic complications such as citrate accumulation or dysnatremia. 915 These
requirements may be challenging in low-resource settings, which limits broader adoption
despite its clinical advantages.931 In contrast, UFH is widely available and used due to its
familiarity, ease of use, and relatively low cost,931 though it carries a higher risk of bleeding
and variable circuit lifespan.916, 931 In low-resource settings, some centers continue to operate

281

CRRT without anticoagulation despite shorter circuit life, due to safety concerns and cost
constraints.931

R

AF
T

For successful implementation, institutions should develop standardized protocols,
ensure availability of necessary monitoring tools, and provide regular training for clinical
staff. Tailoring anticoagulation strategy to patient-specific bleeding risk, institutional
capability, and cost-effectiveness is essential to optimize CRRT delivery across diverse care
settings.

D

Figure 17 | Anticoagulation strategy in a patient with AKI requiring RRT
(CRRT/SLED/IHD). AKI, acute kidney injury; CRRT, continuous renal replacement
therapy; HIT, heparin-induced thrombocytopenia; ICU, intensive care unit; IHD, intermittent
hemodialysis; INR, international normalized ratio; LMWH, low-molecular weight heparin;
RCA, regional citrate anticoagulation; RRT, renal replacement therapy; SLED, sustained
low-efficiency dialysis; UFH, unfractionated heparin
Circuit anticoagulation should be applied using institutional protocols and procedures,
considering the individual patient risk of bleeding, coagulation profile, metabolic tolerance,
along with clinician expertise and resource availability (Table 50).

282

D

R

AF
T

Rationale
The rationale for using anticoagulation in CRRT is to prevent premature or unplanned
circuit loss and to optimize filter lifespan and effective treatment time. In patients not
receiving systemic anticoagulation, the addition of anticoagulant prolongs circuit lifespan,
reduces clotting events, and improves the delivery of prescribed RRT therapy. Conversely,
the absence of anticoagulation often leads to circuit clotting, increased blood loss from
discarded circuits, and interruptions in therapy. Based on current data, use of RCA is
recommended over UFH in children and adults who do not have contraindications to citrate.

ACT, activated clotting time; ACDA, acid-citrate-dextrose solution A; aPTT, activated partial thromboplastin time; BP, blood pressure; Ca, calcium;

285

Practice Point 5.5.1: Unfractionated heparin, epoprostenol, or nafamostat mesylate are
acceptable alternatives to regional citrate anticoagulation for children and adults
receiving CRRT if citrate is contraindicated or not available.
In clinical scenarios where RCA cannot be used, due to contraindications (e.g., severe
liver disease, shock states) or logistical barriers, alternative anticoagulation strategies can be
employed to maintain CRRT circuit patency while minimizing the risk of complications
(e.g., bleeding).

AF
T

Unfractionated heparin remains a commonly used alternative. However, in patients at
increased bleeding risk, its use must be weighed carefully due to the well-documented risk of
major hemorrhage with systemic anticoagulation and heparin-induced thrombocytopenia
(HIT). For such patients, nafamostat mesilate (a synthetic serine protease inhibitor) and
epoprostenol (a synthetic prostacyclin) have been suggested as safe and effective alternatives.
Two RCTs in adults receiving CRRT support the efficacy and safety of nafamostat mesylate
in high bleeding risk populations.932, 933 Choi et al. performed a RCT of 51 participants
receiving CRRT at a high risk of bleeding, where nafamostat mesilate was found to reduce
clotting events without increasing bleeding complications compared to no anticoagulation. 932
Lee et al. studied 73 adults receiving CRRT and found use of nafamostat mesilate resulted in
fewer clotting events (1.15 vs. 1.74 circuits/24 hours, P<0.05) and required use of fewer
filters (2.7 vs. 4.5 per patient, P=0.01) compared to those receiving no anticoagulation.933
These findings and a recent systematic review support nafamostat mesilate as an alternative
anticoagulant where RCA and UFH are contraindicated or unavailable.934

D

R

In children receiving CRRT, several studies demonstrate feasibility and safety of
alternative anticoagulation.924, 935 Miyaji et al. retrospectively compared RCA and nafamostat
mesilate in critically ill children receiving CRRT.935 Both groups achieved effective
anticoagulation with no significant differences in major bleeding, suggesting comparable
safety profiles.935 The Worldwide Exploration of Renal Replacement Outcomes
Collaborative in Kidney Disease (WE-ROCK) multinational registry included over 800
children and young adults on CRRT.936 Although anticoagulation strategies varied, centers
with experience using nafamostat mesilate or prostacyclin reported comparable survival and
filter longevity across strategies.937 Haga et al. reported that 40% of Japanese pediatric ICUs
surveyed routinely used nafamostat mesilate as the primary anticoagulant for pediatric
CRRT, reflecting national clinical acceptance in high bleeding risk patients. 938
Epoprostenol, a platelet inhibitor with vasodilatory effects, is another alternative used
in both children and adults, especially when systemic anticoagulation is undesirable. While
high-quality RCT data are limited, its use has been supported by clinical experience and
observational data.918, 921, 939

286

Practice Point 5.5.2: The addition of regional citrate anticoagulation to systematic
anticoagulation can be appropriate for children and adults with hypercoagulable states
receiving CRRT.
Patients with systemic hypercoagulable states, such as severe COVID-19, nephrotic
syndrome, or systemic inflammatory conditions can experience recurrent circuit clotting
during CRRT, despite receiving systemic anticoagulation. In such scenarios, the addition of
RCA to ongoing systemic anticoagulation may enhance filter longevity without increasing
bleeding risk and thus be a reasonable strategy in selected children and adults.

AF
T

A prospective multicenter study by Helms et al. involving critically ill patients with
COVID-19 and acute respiratory distress syndrome reported a high incidence of thrombotic
complications (up to 69%), even in patients already receiving systemic prophylactic or
therapeutic anticoagulation.940 This underscores the need to consider augmented
anticoagulation strategies for selected patients expressing hypercoagulable states receiving
extracorporeal therapies.

D

R

A retrospective cohort study by Valle et al. evaluated 238 ICU patients with COVID19-associated AKI undergoing CRRT and compared outcomes between RCA alone and RCA
plus UFH.941 The combined anticoagulation approach resulted in a ~60% reduction in the
hazard of filter clotting, without a significant increase in bleeding events.941 Similar findings
were reported in non-COVID hypercoagulable states. In patients with nephrotic syndrome,
Wang et al. demonstrated that the addition of LMWH to RCA during CRRT was effective in
maintaining circuit patency, with no increase in bleeding complications.942 In critically ill
children, Zhu et al. retrospectively evaluated whether either RCA alone or in combination
with low-dose UFH was effective for preserving and prolonging filter lifespan.943 The study
found that the combined strategy improved circuit survival time without increasing
hemorrhagic risk, reinforcing the potential safety and benefit of dual anticoagulation in
hypercoagulable pediatric patients.943 These results suggest there may be a synergistic effect
between systemic and regional anticoagulation in preventing premature circuit loss in
hypercoagulable patients receiving CRRT.
Practice Point 5.5.3: Unfractionated or low-molecular-weight heparin should be
considered for children and adults receiving intermittent hemodialysis (IHD) or PIRRT
without increased bleeding when not already receiving systematic anticoagulation.
Unfractionated heparin and LMWH are widely used anticoagulants during
conventional IHD or PIRRT to prevent clotting of the extracorporeal circuit. Their use is
particularly relevant in patients who are not receiving systemic anticoagulation and do not
have an elevated bleeding risk.

287

A study by Mareedu et al. in adult patients with COVID-19 associated AKI
undergoing PIRRT found that LMWH was superior to UFH in preventing extracorporeal
circuit clotting, with a lower incidence of filter clotting events and comparable bleeding
risk.944 LMWH may be preferable due to more predictable pharmacokinetics and lower
clotting rates, but both are acceptable and widely available anticoagulation options. While
flush-only (saline) protocols may be used in patients at high bleeding risk, they are generally
associated with a higher risk of circuit clotting and treatment interruption and increase the
workload of the clinical staff.

AF
T

Further supporting evidence comes from a large observational analysis of patients
receiving IHD and concomitant systemic antiplatelet or anticoagulant therapy.945 The
findings suggest that the use of intradialytic UFH did not significantly increase bleeding
events, even in patients on concurrent antiplatelet agents, and remained effective in
preserving circuit patency.945 This highlights the safety of UFH under routine conditions and
provides reassurance for its use in patients without overt bleeding risk. Although pediatricspecific evidence is more limited, clinical practice patterns align with adults, with UFH being
routinely used in IHD and PIRRT for children, particularly those with stable hemodynamics
and low bleeding risk.
Practice Point 5.5.4: Anticoagulation with argatroban, danaparoid, or bivalirudin,
rather than other non-heparin anticoagulants, should be used in adults receiving RRT
with acute heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) who require systemic
anticoagulation.

D

R

HIT is a serious immune-mediated complication of heparin therapy that necessitates
immediate cessation of all heparin products and transition to alternative anticoagulation. For
patients receiving RRT, choosing an effective and safe non-heparin anticoagulant is essential
to minimize thrombotic risk and maintain circuit patency. Argatroban appears to offer the
most favorable balance of efficacy and safety, with danaparoid and bivalirudin also
considered acceptable alternatives.
Argatroban, a direct thrombin inhibitor metabolized by the liver, has been widely
studied in patients with HIT requiring RRT.946 A retrospective cohort study of 74 patients
receiving CRRT with suspected HIT found that argatroban and bivalirudin were both
effective, but argatroban had more activated partial thromboplastin time (aPTT) levels within
the therapeutic range, indicating better dosing control.946 In a RCT of 66 participants
receiving CRRT with suspected HIT, argatroban was compared with lepirudin.947 Although
both anticoagulants were found to result in similar filter patency, argatroban was associated
with significantly fewer clinically relevant bleeding events (4 vs. 11; OR: 3.9; 95% CI: 1.1–
14.0; P=0.040).947

288

A Bayesian network meta-analysis evaluated various non-heparin anticoagulant
regimens for patients with HIT and suggested that argatroban was associated with the lowest
risk of bleeding and thromboembolism, as well as potentially lower mortality and shorter
hospital stays compared with alternatives, including danaparoid, lepirudin, and bivalirudin. 948

AF
T

Danaparoid, an indirect factor Xa inhibitor with minimal cross-reactivity to heparinantibodies against platelet factor 4 (PF4), also has evidence supporting its use. In a caseseries by Magnani et al. of 122 patients receiving RRT, danaparoid was found effective in
maintaining RRT circuit patency with low rates of bleeding complications.949 Bivalirudin,
another direct thrombin inhibitor, has also been used in RRT, although its renal clearance
necessitates dose adjustments. In comparative studies, bivalirudin has shown similar efficacy
and bleeding risk to argatroban but may be less preferred in patients with severely abnormal
kidney function due to the risk of accumulation.950
Practice Point 5.5.5 Regional citrate anticoagulation rather than heparin or nonheparin anticoagulants is preferable in adults receiving RRT with subacute (HIT-A,
HIT-B) or remote HIT, who are not receiving systemic anticoagulation and require
anticoagulation to prevent clotting of the dialysis circuit.

R

In patients with a history of HIT, particularly those with subacute or remote HIT who
no longer require systemic anticoagulation, the risk of HIT recurrence remains a concern if
heparin is reintroduced. Additionally, non-heparin anticoagulants used systemically may
increase bleeding risk or require complex dosing adjustments. In this context, RCA offers a
safe and effective strategy for maintaining circuit patency during RRT without exposing the
patient to systemic anticoagulation risks.

D

The American Society of Hematology (ASH) 2018 Guidelines explicitly recommend
the use of RCA over heparin or other systemic non-heparin anticoagulants in this scenario.951
For patients with subacute HIT A, subacute HIT B, or remote HIT undergoing RRT and not
requiring systemic anticoagulation, the ASH Guideline panel issued a conditional
recommendation favoring RCA, despite very-low certainty of evidence, due to its favorable
safety profile and lack of systemic anticoagulant effect. The guideline emphasized that RCA
is not appropriate in acute HIT, where systemic anticoagulation is still needed.951
5.6 Hemofilters for RRT
Practice Point 5.6.1: Biocompatible membrane hemofilters and dialyzers should be used
in children and adults with AKI undergoing CRRT or IHD.
All modern hemodialyzers and filters are hollow-fiber devices constructed with
membranes made from cellulose, modified cellulose, or synthetic polymers like polysulfone
and polyethersulfone. Their core function is to clean blood by removing solute and water
through the membrane. While water removal relies on membrane hydraulic permeability, this
289

is no longer a limiting factor clinically due to automated ultrafiltration control systems,
which prevents excessive fluid removal. Consequently, manufacturers have shifted focus
toward optimizing membranes for enhanced solute exchange efficiency by increasing pore
size (e.g., high-flux membranes defined by β₂-microglobulin clearance >20 ml/min) and
engineering membrane surface area, fiber geometry, and material properties to boost
diffusive, convective, adsorptive clearance.952

AF
T

Most modern biocompatible membranes are synthetic and include materials such as
polysulfone, polyethersulfone, polyamide, polycarbonate, polyacrylonitrile (PAN),
polymethylmethacrylate (PMMA), and AN69, a copolymer of sodium methallyl sulfonate
and acrylonitrile (Table 51).953 These membranes are designed to minimize complement
activation and cytokine release, thereby reducing inflammation and improving patient
tolerance during RRT.
