The acrylonitrile 69 (AN69) membrane and its surface-treated variants (e.g.,
AN69ST) have demonstrated favorable biocompatibility and cytokine adsorption
properties.953, 954 There remains uncertainty on the efficacy of the AN69 membrane to
prolong circuit/hemofilter lifespan and this may be dependent on patient characteristics,
clinical circumstances, and parameters of RRT (e.g., modality). Surface-modified PAN
membranes, such as polyethyleneimine-treated hemofilters, have been explored for their
potential benefit in the setting of anticoagulation-free PIRRT.955 A randomized crossover
study in 60 acutely ill adults at high risk of bleeding compared heparin-primed
polyethylenimine-treated AN69 membrane (AN69ST) versus AN69 membranes on the
290

success of RRT, filter lifespan, and severity of filter clotting, along with factors associated
with premature filter clotting.955 The success rate, filter lifespans, and severity of filter
clotting were not significantly different. However, in multivariable analysis, higher platelet
count, and higher serum hemoglobin level were associated with increased clotting risk,
whereas a lower aPTT was associated with longer filter lifespan. In patients with aPTT <35.3
seconds, serum hemoglobin >83 g/l, and platelet count <70×10⁹/l, the AN69ST was
associated with longer filter lifespan by 252 minutes, suggesting potential benefit in select
subgroups.955 Comparative studies, such as those evaluating AN69 ST100 versus oXiris®
membranes in non-anticoagulated patients with liver failure receiving CRRT suggest similar
or modest differences in hemofilter longevity in adult patients.953, 954

AF
T

PMMA membrane dialyzers, first introduced in 1977, continue to hold a unique place
in modern dialysis practice due to their distinctive adsorption properties and
biocompatibility. PMMA membranes are characterized by a uniform pore structure and the
ability to adsorb middle- and large-molecular weight proteins, making them particularly
effective for patients with inflammatory or uremic symptoms. Successive generations of
PMMA dialyzers, such as the BG, BK-F, and NF models have been refined to improve pore
size, antithrombogenicity, and overall hemocompatibility.956
Their protein-adsorptive capacity, improved biocompatibility, and potential to
alleviate dialysis-related symptoms and enhance immune response make them a valuable
option for selected patient populations. Overall, the use of biocompatible synthetic
membranes is considered standard of care and contributes to safer, more effective RRT in
acutely ill patients.

D

R

Practice Point 5.6.2: Non-pharmacologic strategies that may be used to maintain and
maximize circuit/hemofilter lifespan include optimizing catheter position in the right
atrium, use of predilution hemofiltration, and continuous venovenous hemodialysis
(CVVHD) or hemodiafiltration (CVVHDF) rather than continuous venovenous
hemofiltration (CVVH).
Maintaining hemofilter and circuit patency is a key goal in CRRT, where
interruptions due to circuit disruption can compromise solute clearance and ultrafiltration. In
addition to pharmacologic anticoagulation, several non-pharmacologic strategies can
optimize circuit lifespan, especially when anticoagulation is contraindicated or must be
minimized.957 Optimizing predilution strategies, catheter positioning, and CRRT modality
are nonpharmacological strategies that may enhance circuit survival (Table 52). These
measures are especially relevant in postoperative or bleeding-prone patients where
anticoagulants may be associated with bleeding risk or are contraindicated.
Tsujimoto et al. evaluated a spectrum of non-pharmacological interventions to
prevent or mitigate premature clotting of extracorporeal circuits during CRRT (Table 52).957
291

This systematic review found that use of CVVHD or CVVHDF rather than CVVH
significantly increased circuit lifespan by an average of 10.15 hours (95% CI: 5.15–15.15).
Similarly, use of predilution hemofiltration rather than post-dilution was associated with a
mean increase in filter lifespan of 9.34 hours (95% CI: -2.60 to 21.29). Catheter-related
factors played a significant role, and surface-modified double-lumen catheters increased
circuit duration by 16.0 hours (95% CI: 13.49–18.51). Notably, hemofilters designed with a
greater number of shorter hollow fibers were associated with a reduced circuit life of 5.87
hours (95% CI: -10.18 to -1.56).957

AF
T

Catheter position is also essential to maintain circuit patency. Studies show that
proper positioning of the catheter tip in the right atrium improves blood flow dynamics and
reduces recirculation, which may prolong filter lifespan and improve dialysis efficiency.958,
959 In an RCT of 100 critically ill adult participants, Morgan et al. compared long (20–24 cm)
versus short (15–20 cm) soft silicone catheters for CRRT targeting tip placement in the right
atrium versus in the superior vena cava.958 Longer catheters placed in the right atrium were
shown to significantly prolong mean circuit lifespan by 6.5 hours (median 24 h; IQR: 11–32
vs. 17.5 h; IQR: 8–23; P=0.001).958 Additionally, these longer catheters improved the
delivered/prescribed daily RRT dose-intensity to a median of 91% (IQR: 85%–100%)
compared to 81% (IQR: 72%–97%) with shorter catheters (P<0.001), reduced the number of
clotting events per patient from 3.6 to 2.3 (P=0.04), and decreased access-related circuit
failures per patient from 0.53 to 0.19 (P=0.04).958 Placement in the right atrium did not
significantly increase complications, with similar rates of atrial arrhythmias between groups
(28% vs. 21%, P=0.6).958

R

The prescribed blood flow rate during CRRT varies globally in adults, ranging from
80 ml/min to over 300 ml/min.959-961 Evidence from 2 RCTs involving a total of 134
participants receiving CRRT found that higher blood flow rates (200–250 ml/min vs. 100–
150 ml/min) had little or no impact on circuit lifespan compared to standard rates.957, 962, 963

D

Various synthetic membranes have been investigated, such as AN69, AN69ST, and
oXiris® (heparin-grafted membrane), for their capacity to preserve circuit patency and
reduce clotting through enhanced biocompatibility or cytokine adsorption. However, current
evidence does not support a clear advantage of any specific membrane type for preserving
and prolonging filter lifespan or improving clinical outcomes in patients receiving CRRT
without anticoagulation.954, 964, 965,966

D

Practice Point 5.6.3: Where regional citrate or systemic heparin anticoagulation are
contraindicated or not available, priming of the CRRT circuit/filter with a solution
containing unfractionated heparin (UFH) may be used to increase circuit/hemofilter
lifespan.
Priming the circuit with UFH can theoretically precoat the hemofilter surfaces and
tubing and mitigate early thrombus formation by inhibiting platelet and fibrin adhesion.
While the use of regional or systemic anticoagulation (e.g., RCA) often yields longer filter
survival, the use of heparin priming can provide a pragmatic and low-risk alternative,
especially for patients with contraindications to RCA or where bleeding risk limits the use of
systemic heparin.973
Several studies have evaluated the efficacy of heparin-albumin priming to reduce
extracorporeal circuit clotting during RRT, suggesting this strategy may have a potential role
in prolonging circuit lifespan and reducing exposure to systemic anticoagulation. In an in
293

vitro study by Kyrk et al., circuits primed with a combination of 5,000 IU of unfractionated
heparin and albumin (5 ml of 20% solution in 1l saline) were able to complete the full 192minute simulated hemodialysis sessions with only minimal clotting.974 In contrast, circuits
primed with 0.9% saline alone clotted within a median of 21 minutes (IQR: 18–27 minutes),
and those with albumin alone lasted a median of 26 minutes (IQR: 19–35 minutes), implying
the potential superior thrombo-prophylactic effect of the heparin-albumin combination.974

AF
T

Limited clinical evidence from retrospective analyses supports these findings. In a
retrospective cohort study of 321 patients with high bleeding risk receiving 1408 IHD
sessions, use of heparin-albumin priming was associated with a clotting-related session
interruption rate of 1.0% compared to 0.8% in those receiving standard heparin.975
Importantly, patients in the heparin-albumin group required significantly lower systemic
heparin doses, median 1200 IU compared to 5000 IU (P<0.001), with no increase in bleeding
complications.975 In a similar retrospective cohort study of 68 patients receiving IHD (248
sessions), use of a priming solution with heparin-albumin was associated with lower
utilization of heparin during IHD, with no increase in clotting or bleeding complications
when compared with use of systemic anticoagulation.976
5.7. Replacement and dialysate solutions for RRT
Practice Point 5.7.1: Use bicarbonate, rather than lactate, as a buffer in dialysate and
replacement fluid for CRRT in adults with AKI in the following scenarios: circulatory
shock, liver failure or lactic acidemia.

D

R

This practice point is reformatted from Recommendation 5.7.1 from the KDIGO 2012
Guideline.12 The guidance is based on avoiding metabolic acidosis and improving
hemodynamic stability of patients with conditions that may impair lactate metabolism.
Evidence from RCTs consistently shows superior metabolic and hemodynamic effects with
the use of bicarbonate-buffered compared with lactate-buffered solutions for patients
receiving CRRT.
Bicarbonate-buffered solutions are preferred due to their superior metabolic
compatibility in critically ill patients with impaired lactate metabolism. In circulatory shock,
hepatic perfusion is often reduced, impairing the capacity of the liver to convert lactate into
bicarbonate. Consequently, the use of lactate-buffered solutions may result in iatrogenic
lactate accumulation and worsen metabolic acidosis. In contrast, bicarbonate-buffered fluids
provide more predictable acid-base correction and avoid iatrogenic lactic acidosis, leading to
improved hemodynamic tolerance and more accurate interpretation of serum lactate
concentrations as a measure of end-organ perfusion.977
Four notable RCTs support the use of bicarbonate over lactate as a buffer in CRRT.
In a single center RCT by Barenbrock et al., the use of bicarbonate-buffered replacement
fluids in participants undergoing CVVH significantly improved cardiovascular stability
294

compared to lactate-buffered replacement fluids.978 Among the 117 critically ill adults
enrolled, those receiving bicarbonate-buffered solutions had lower mean blood lactate levels
(17.4 ± 8.5 mg/dl) compared to the lactate-buffered group (28.7 ± 10.4 mg/dl; P<0.05), and
higher serum bicarbonate concentrations (23.7 ± 0.4 mmol/l vs. 21.8 ± 0.5 mmol/l; P<0.01).
Importantly, the incidence of hypotensive episodes was significantly lower in the
bicarbonate-buffered group (0.26 ± 0.09 episodes per 24 h) compared to the lactate-buffered
group (0.60 ± 0.31; P<0.05). Furthermore, cardiovascular events occurred less frequently in
participants treated with bicarbonate-buffered fluids (15% vs. 38%; P<0.01).978

AF
T

Similarly, in the study by Kierdorf et al., 12 adults with AKI receiving CVVH were
enrolled in a randomized crossover trial comparing bicarbonate- versus lactate-buffered
replacement fluids.979 The findings suggested that bicarbonate-buffered solutions provided
superior acid-base balance and hemodynamic stability. Specifically, participants receiving
bicarbonate-buffered solutions had significantly higher arterial pH values (mean 7.38 ± 0.04)
compared to those receiving lactate buffers (7.32 ± 0.06; P<0.05), and serum bicarbonate
levels were also higher (22 ± 3 mmol/l vs. 18 ± 4 mmol/l; P<0.05). Importantly, the
bicarbonate-buffered group experienced fewer hypotensive episodes (0.8 ± 0.7 vs. 2.3 ± 1.1
episodes/day; P<0.05) and lower systemic lactate levels, particularly in the context of
impaired tissue perfusion.979

D

R

In a prospective, randomized crossover trial involving 26 critically ill adults
undergoing CVVHD, Zimmerman et al. directly compared a bicarbonate-buffered dialysate
with a lactate-buffered dialysate over 2 48-hour treatment periods in a crossover design.980
Both solutions effectively raised serum bicarbonate from baseline (bicarbonate group:
18.9 ± 2.0 mmol/l to 22.2 ± 1.2 mmol/l; lactate group: 16.3 ± 1.5 mmol/l to 22.2 ± 1.4
mmol/l). Importantly, serum lactate levels differed significantly; patients dialyzed with
lactate-buffered dialysate were found to have higher serum lactate concentrations (mean
2.92 ± 0.45 mmol/l) than those treated with bicarbonate-buffered dialysate
(1.61 ± 0.25 mmol/l; P=0.01). In participants with hepatic dysfunction, lactate increased to
3.39 ± 0.68 mmol/l in participants randomized to lactate-buffered fluids compared to
1.78 ± 0.42 mmol/l in participants treated with bicarbonate-buffered fluids (P=0.036).
The risks associated with bicarbonate-buffered CRRT solutions are minimal when
used appropriately and with adequate monitoring. While lactate-based solutions may be more
readily available or less expensive in some settings, their use in high-risk populations (e.g.,
those with shock) may lead to clinically significant metabolic disturbances. Therefore, the
balance of evidence and physiological rationale favors bicarbonate-buffered solutions in
patients with circulatory shock or liver failure.608 Implementing bicarbonate-buffered
solutions in CRRT requires careful attention to infrastructure, training, and supply chain
logistics. These solutions are typically delivered in two-chamber bag systems to prevent
calcium-bicarbonate precipitation, necessitating compatible CRRT machines with
appropriate software and fluid handling capabilities.
295

In low-resource settings, where commercial CRRT solutions may not be readily
available, the implementation of bicarbonate-buffered solutions can present additional
challenges. Staff must be trained to manage the mixing process and understand the potential
implications of incorrect handling. The requirement for two-chamber bag systems or inhouse preparation to prevent calcium-bicarbonate precipitation demands appropriate
infrastructure, training, and resources that may be limited or competing with other priorities
in such environments.981 Storage requirements are more stringent than for lactate-buffered
solutions due to a typically shorter shelf life and temperature sensitivity. Despite these
considerations, the widespread adoption of bicarbonate-buffered solutions in commercially
available CRRT fluids has facilitated their integration into routine practice.

AF
T

Bicarbonate-buffered solutions are preferred over lactate-buffered solutions for
CRRT in critically ill children and adults with AKI, particularly those with circulatory shock
and/or liver failure. This preference is grounded in their superior physiological compatibility
and improved safety profile in populations where lactate metabolism is impaired.
Practice Point 5.7.2: CRRT dialysate and replacement solutions should be sterile.
Dialysate and replacement solutions for IRRT should comply with American
Association of Medical Instrumentation (AAMI) standards regarding contamination
with bacteria and endotoxins.

D

R

The safety and effectiveness of CRRT are dependent on the composition and quality
of dialysate and replacement solutions. In particular, the use of sterile solutions is imperative
for CRRT, as these fluids are administered directly into the extracorporeal circuit and may
enter the patient’s circulation either through convection or diffusion. Contaminated solutions
pose a significant risk for bloodstream infections, pyrogenic reactions, and systemic
inflammatory responses, which can adversely affect patient outcomes, especially in critically
ill populations.
IRRT should at a minimum conform to the AAMI standards for microbial
contamination and endotoxin levels. The AAMI standards recommend that dialysate used in
hemodialysis contains <200 colony-forming units (CFU)/ml of bacteria and <2 endotoxin
units (EU)/ml, with target values of <100 CFU/ml and <0.25 EU/ml for ultrapure
dialysate.982, 983 These thresholds are designed to minimize the risk of chronic inflammation
and endotoxin-related complications in patients undergoing dialysis.982, 983
In line with the ADQI consensus on precision fluid management in CRRT, the use of
biocompatible, sterile, and metabolically favorable fluids is recommended to reduce
treatment-related complications and optimize patient outcomes.984 The combination of sterile
delivery and bicarbonate buffering aligns with international best practices and enhances both
safety and efficacy in the management of AKI in critically ill populations.
296

Practice Point 5.7.3: Locally compounded dialysate and replacement CRRT solutions
should be prepared using standard quality control processes or commercially available
dialysate and replacement CRRT solutions should be used to allow flexibility in
achieving various goals of plasma electrolyte compositions to address most clinical
circumstances.

983

AF
T

Commercially manufactured CRRT solutions are a preferred option when available,
due to their reliable composition, sterility, and regulatory oversight. In clinical settings where
commercial CRRT solutions may not be readily available or feasible, locally compounded
dialysate and replacement fluids offer a practical and flexible alternative.981, 985 However, to
ensure safety and efficacy, it is essential that such in-house solutions are prepared using
standardized protocols, stringent quality control processes, and appropriate staff training. 982,

D

R

Several studies support the feasibility and safety of this approach. Yimsangyad et al.
evaluated the implementation of an in-house, quality-controlled, dialysis solution preparation
system for CRRT in a low-resource setting.985 Over a 12-month period, 48 critically ill
patients received a total of 352 CRRT treatments using locally compounded dialysate and
replacement fluids.985 The study found that all prepared solutions met microbial safety
standards, with no detectable bacterial contamination or endotoxin levels above acceptable
thresholds. Electrolyte concentrations in the in-house solutions remained within ±10% of
target values, indicating consistency and reliability in preparation. Importantly, there were no
adverse clinical events attributable to the use of these solutions, including catheter-related
infections, metabolic disturbances, or hemodynamic instability. From an economic
perspective, the in-house production reduced the cost per liter of CRRT solution by
approximately 30% compared to commercial alternatives. These findings demonstrate that,
when appropriate quality control measures are applied, locally compounded CRRT solutions
can be a safe, effective, and cost-efficient option in low-resource healthcare settings.985
Similarly, Lumlertgul et al. described the successful deployment of in-house CRRT solutions
during the COVID-19 pandemic, showing that this method could be rapidly scaled in
response to supply shortages without compromising patient safety or treatment efficacy. 981
Practice Point 5.7.4: Base the choice of dialysate and replacement CRRT solutions with
specific compositions upon the goals for serum electrolyte and acid–base parameters.
The use of dialysate and replacement solutions should be individualized and reassessed
at frequent intervals depending upon the clinical condition of the patient to ensure that
they are appropriate.
The composition of dialysate and replacement solutions in CRRT should be
individualized and routinely reassessed to align with the evolving clinical needs of critically
ill children and adults with AKI. Selecting or adapting CRRT solutions with specific
297

electrolyte concentrations, such as phosphate-containing, high- or low-sodium, high- or lowpotassium, or high-bicarbonate fluids, can support targeted correction of metabolic,
electrolyte and acid-base abnormalities.
Phosphate-containing CRRT solutions are increasingly used. Thompson Bastin et al.
reported that the use of phosphate-containing CRRT solutions was associated with shorter
duration of mechanical ventilation and improved phosphate control in adults,986 while Kim et
al. showed more stable serum phosphate levels with the use of phosphate-containing
dialysate compared with standard solutions.987

AF
T

For patients with disturbances in acid-base balance, especially those receiving RCA,
the use of a high bicarbonate-containing CRRT solution may accelerate pH normalization
without compromising safety.988 Additionally, in cases of dysnatremias, adjustments in
sodium concentration through customized CRRT solutions may be necessary to achieve
gradual and safe correction.989 Protocols for sodium-based osmotherapy have been developed
to guide such individualized management.990-993 Frequent reassessment, ideally every 6 to 12
hours, is essential given the dynamic clinical course of critical illness and patient
susceptibility to rapid shifts in fluid, electrolytes, and acid-base balance.

R

For patients receiving glucose-free CRRT solutions, vigilance is also required to
prevent complications like euglycemic ketoacidosis. In a case series of 8 adults with
preexisting diabetes mellitus receiving CRRT with a glucose-free solution, all patients
exhibited worsening high-anion-gap ketoacidosis and elevated serum β-hydroxybutyrate
levels despite stable or improved lactate levels.994 The study underscores the importance of
monitoring patients for the development of euglycemic ketoacidosis when receiving glucosefree CRRT and highlights the need for proactive nutritional support and glucose
supplementation as preventive measures.994

D

5.8. Ultrafiltration in RRT
Practice Point 5.8.1: The target ultrafiltration should be prescribed daily for CRRT and
prior to each IRRT session and be frequently monitored.

R

Fluid accumulation and overload is a common indication for RRT initiation in
children and adults.995, 996 Ultrafiltration, defined as the extracorporeal removal of fluid
during RRT, supports fluid management, facilitating resolution of organ edema and weaning
from organ support. However, evidence extrapolated from patients with kidney failure
receiving maintenance dialysis has shown that rapid ultrafiltration during IHD sessions can
induce systemic stress and can precipitate ischemic organ injury (i.e., heart, gut, kidney, and
brain).997-999 Evidence in children and adults implies there is a U-shaped association between
the rate of ultrafiltration and mortality.1000-1003 While an important observation, there are
notable limitations to these data, which were derived from retrospective, observational
studies or secondary analyses of large RCTs, with inherent challenges to inferences from
such data for clinical practice. The primary exposure, rate of ultrafiltration, was generally
time-averaged, of variable duration, often dichotomized across these studies.1000-1005 In
addition, the relationship with outcomes is vulnerable to confounding by disease severity. 1006

D

The prescription for ultrafiltration should target fluid removal, facilitate weaning
from organ support, and not incite harm by worsening or inciting new organ injury.1000, 1004,
1007 Among critically ill children receiving CRRT, ultrafiltration targeting a negative net fluid
balance while also preserving urine output was associated with fewer CRRT-days and lower
rates of MAKE30.1008
This necessitates regular review of the ultrafiltration prescription every 12–24 hours
(or more frequently), the net ultrafiltration (UFNET), and patient fluid balance goals.1009 There
is rationale for prescriptions to be weight-based, similar to CRRT dose-intensity (e.g.,
ml/kg/h) and evidence for UFNET limits in patients receiving IRRT, rather than use of hourly
or daily absolute volumes, which may inadvertently expose patients to low or high timeaverage ultrafiltration and complications from hemodynamic instability or fluid overload.1010
299

For patients receiving CRRT, healthcare professionals must understand the hourly
CRRT device and patient fluid balances.1011 The CRRT device fluid balance is calculated as
the difference in the volume of fluids infused hourly through the CRRT device (e.g.,
replacement fluids, dialysate solutions, anticoagulant) and the total hourly effluent volume.
The patient fluid balance is calculated as the hourly difference in patient inputs (e.g.,
medications, blood products, nutrition) and outputs (e.g., urine, drains, gastrointestinal),
including ultrafiltration. The integration of CRRT device and patient fluid balances are then
used to determine the net device-patient fluid balance, which is calculated based on the
preceding hour.984, 1012, 1013 This permits the UFNET rate to be determined hourly and to be
aligned with the desired device fluid removal to achieve total fluid balance goals over time
(6–24 hours).

AF
T

Practice Point 5.8.2: The prescribed intensity of ultrafiltration (e.g., timing and rate of
removal) should be individualized and consider patient characteristics, clinical context,
and phase of illness.

R

Heterogeneity in patient characteristics, acuity, and evolving phases of acute illness
demands that prescribed ultrafiltration be patient- and context-specific and regularly
adjusted.984, 1003, 1014 In children and adults, the optimal prescribed ultrafiltration intensity is
uncertain. Existing evidence indicates that the delivery of both low and high ultrafiltration in
children and adults may be associated with harm.1000-1003 As such, the intensity of UFNET
should be tailored to patient-specific fluid balance targets while minimizing the risk of
adverse events (Table 54). The intensity of UFNET may modify patient outcomes (e.g.,
mortality; AKI resolution) and should be prescribed to support organ dysfunction resolution
while also minimizing harm.1000, 1001, 1004, 1008, 1015

D

Table 54 | Strategies to mitigate adverse events associated with ultrafiltration
Strategy
• Reduce unnecessary fluid administration in critically ill patients to avoid excess
accumulation.
• Cool dialysate to ≤36oC.
• Prescribe net ultrafiltration (UFNET) by body weight rather than target total volume.
• Prescribe a slower UFNET and continuous gradual fluid removal.
• Avoid high UFNET while patients are receiving hemodynamic support (i.e., vasopressors).
• Review routinely and regularly the UFNET targets and response to fluid removal.
• Avoid or minimize prolonged exposure to high UFNET (short-term high UFNET may be
indicated and necessary to address life-threatening complications of fluid overload).
Adapted from Murugan et al.1016

5.9 Discontinuation of RRT
Practice Point 5.9.1: Discontinue RRT when it is no longer needed either because of
evidence of resolution of AKI to the point that kidney function is adequate to meet the
patient’s metabolic and fluid balance demands or because RRT is no longer consistent
with the patient’s preferences for care.
300

Most survivors of acute and critical illness complicated by AKI recover sufficient
function to become RRT independent.713, 815, 1017 Healthcare professionals should aim to
frequently and regularly evaluate for indications of AKI resolution, recovery of intrinsic
kidney function, and for the potential to feasibly discontinue RRT. This may minimize the
duration of risk associated with extracorporeal device/circuit exposure.

AF
T

The decision on whether to discontinue RRT integrates several considerations,
including whether there has been sufficient resolution of AKI and recovery in intrinsic
kidney function to meet current demands (e.g., acid-base, electrolyte, azotemic, fluid),
whether there has been improvement in the acute illness that precipitated AKI and start of
RRT, and whether there is a commitment to ongoing RRT support if a trial of RRT
discontinuation is unsuccessful (i.e., change to goals-of-care designation).

R

Clinical trials have used clinical indications, various metabolic parameters, urine
output, and timed creatinine clearance to inform the timing of RRT discontinuation.820 The
Liberation of Acute Dialysis Trial (LIBERATE-D) focused on hospitalized adult patients
with AKI who were hemodynamically stable and not requiring vasopressor support. 1018 The
trial compared a strategy of continuing IHD for AKI only when specific metabolic or clinical
indications were met (e.g., azotemia, hyperkalemia, metabolic acidosis, pulmonary edema,
clinical judgement) versus continuing scheduled IHD session 3 times per week until specific
urine output (>1 l/d in the absence of diuretics or >2 l/d with diuretics) or creatinine
clearance (>20 ml/min) criteria were met.1018 Among the 221 participants enrolled in the
trial, the former strategy resulted in fewer dialysis sessions per week, earlier recovery to IHD
independence, and more participants achieving kidney function recovery by the time of
hospital discharge.

D

The strategy to assess for recovery of intrinsic kidney function during RRT depends
on the modality being used. For patients receiving IRRT, kidney recovery can be assessed
over interdialytic periods, whereas for patients receiving CRRT, relatively steady state can be
achieved after 24–48 hour that may enable clearer assessment (e.g., urine volume; urinary
excretion of creatinine; timed creatinine clearance, etc.).1019-1026 However, the current
literature is challenging to draw strong inferences from because “successful” RRT
discontinuation has been inconsistently defined, ranging from RRT-free between 3–90
days.1024, 1027 As such, decision-making on RRT discontinuation remains subject to wide
practice variation.1020
Practice Point 5.9.2: Clinical and metabolic parameters, interdialytic SCr changes,
urine output, fluid balance, other parameters of resolution of AKI, and residual kidney
function can be used to guide decision-making for RRT discontinuation.

301

Interdialytic periods, whether during an interruption in CRRT or between scheduled
IRRT sessions, provide an opportunity to evaluate for persistence of clinical and metabolic
indications for RRT, as well as signs of AKI resolution and kidney function recovery. In a
single center retrospective study, 448 CRRT-free intervals in 222 critically ill adult patients
receiving CRRT were evaluated to predict the suitability for a trial of CRRT
discontinuation.1025 Three parameters best predicted successful CRRT liberation: shorter total
duration of CRRT, lower Sequential Organ Failure Assessment (SOFA) score, and urine
output. Specifically, a higher urine output in the CRRT-free period predicted successful
liberation (66 ml/h vs. 10 ml/h).1025 Similarly, interdialytic periods between IRRT sessions
can be used to observe urine output and additional parameters (e.g., timed creatinine
clearance).

R

AF
T

There have been few clinical risk prediction scores developed to guide decisions for
RRT discontinuation.1025, 1028-1030 In a single center in Korea, 1158 critically ill adult patients
receiving CRRT were evaluated for factors associated with successful CRRT
discontinuation, defined as being RRT-free ≥7 days after initial discontinuation.1028 Factors
associated with successful CRRT discontinuation were greater urine output (≥300 ml/24h),
MAP (50–78 mm Hg), serum potassium <4.1 mmol/l, and BUN <35 mg/dl (serum urea
<12.5 mmol/l). A clinical prediction score, comprised of only these 4 variables, had an
AUROC 0.73. Another retrospective cohort study in adult patients described the clinical
factors predictive of successful CRRT discontinuation, defined as RRT-free survival ≥72
hours after CRRT discontinuation in 1135 critically ill patients with AKI.1029 Successful
discontinuation was observed in 20%. Factors predictive of successful discontinuation
included urine output, SCr, cumulative fluid balance, CRRT duration, and receipt of
vasoactive therapy. Internal validation showed good discrimination (AUROC: 0.78).

D

In the LIBERATE-D trial, participants received IHD only when one of the following
indications were met: BUN ≥112 mg/dl (serum urea ≥40 mmol/l); hyperkalemia ≥6 mmol/l
(>5.5 mmol/l if despite medical treatment); arterial pH <7.15 from metabolic acidosis (or
bicarbonate level <12 mEq/l); acute pulmonary edema due to fluid overload responsible for
hypoxemia despite diuretic therapy; or clinician judgment.1018 This strategy increased the
likelihood of achieving recovery of kidney function (alive and not receiving dialysis at
hospital discharge) compared with continuing treatment 3 times per week until urine output
or creatinine clearance criteria were met (64% vs. 50%).
Practice Point 5.9.3: Urine output >450 ml/24 hours without exposure to diuretics or
>2300 ml/24 hours with exposure to diuretics and 2-hour timed creatinine clearance
≥23 ml/min can be used to predict successful RRT discontinuation in adults.
While clinical studies have identified various factors associated with successful
discontinuation from RRT, most studies have been small and retrospective.1019-1021, 1023, 1024,
1031, 1032 Urine output has consistently been shown to be an important predictor of successful
302

RRT discontinuation.1023, 1024, 1026, 1028, 1031, 1033-1035 All clinical prediction tools developed
have integrated urine output as a predictor of successful RRT discontinuation.1025, 1028-1030 In
general, a urine output exceeding >450 ml/24 hours without exposure to diuretics or >2300
ml/24 hours with exposure to diuretics predicts a high probability of a successful trial of RRT
discontinuation and serves to guide clinical decision-making.1020, 1024

AF
T

Numerous studies have explored varying urine output thresholds for RRT
discontinuation.1024 In a single center retrospective study of 86 critically ill adults receiving
RRT, urine output <0.5 ml/kg/h over 6 hours after CRRT was stopped was associated with
lower likelihood of successful liberation (AUROC: 0.91), whereas an urine output of 178
ml/6 hours appears to be the minimum threshold to predict success.1022 In a retrospective,
multicenter study of 213 adults receiving CRRT, greater urine output (>30 ml/h) at the time
of discontinuation was the best predictor of success (AUROC: 0.814), defined as CRRT
cessation for ≥7 days.1023
In adults, the use of a timed creatinine clearance (e.g., 2-hour; 6-hour; 24-hour) can
also be used to predict successful RRT discontinuation.820, 1019 In a small retrospective study,
a 2-hour timed creatinine clearance measurement was a reasonable predictor of successful
discontinuation, defined as RRT-free at 7-days, when performed in the 12-hours prior to
CRRT discontinuation.1019 A threshold 2-hour creatinine clearance of 23 ml/min was
associated with a sensitivity, specificity and positive predictive value for successful
discontinuation of 75.5%, 84.4%, and 88.8%, respectively.

R

Practice Point 5.9.4: Use of diuretic therapy prior to and following RRT discontinuation
can be used to augment urine output and manage fluid balance but has not been proven
to facilitate resolution of AKI.
The use of diuretics, predominantly furosemide, is common among patients with

AKI.1036 Large cohort studies have shown up to two-thirds of critically ill adults with AKI

D

are exposed to diuretic therapy.1037 In the Standard versus Accelerated Initiation of Renal
Replacement Therapy in Acute Kidney Injury (STARRT-AKI) trial, diuretic exposure prior
to randomization occurred in 35% of participants.713 In the Initiation of Dialysis Early Versus
Delayed in the Intensive Care Unit (IDEAL-ICU) study, diuretic exposure exceeded 50% in
the 7 days following enrollment124, whereas in the AKIKI trial, 18.7% were exposed to
diuretic therapy prior to starting RRT, predominantly in the delayed RRT strategy compared
with the early strategy (36.5% vs. 1.3%, p<0.001).712
The use of diuretics is also common among critically ill children prior to and after
CRRT initiation.1038 In a large European survey of pediatric ICU practice, 39% reported
using diuretic therapy during CRRT and up to one-third reported using a diuretic challenge,
either as a bolus alone or followed by a diuretic infusion during CRRT discontinuation.891

303

Diuretic therapy in patients with AKI is most often used to facilitate urine output and
prevent or treat complications of fluid accumulation. 433, 1037 Evidence indicates that exposure
to diuretics alters the urine output threshold for predicting successful RRT liberation.1020, 1022
A robust urine output response to a diuretic challenge may provide added information on the
likelihood of successful RRT discontinuation.1022, 1031, 1033 Importantly, there is no compelling
evidence to suggest that the pre-emptive use of diuretics can facilitate more rapid resolution
of AKI;1039, 1040 however, diuretics have an adjuvant role in augmenting urine output and for
managing fluid balance upon RRT discontinuation. In a small single center RCT, a
continuous infusion of furosemide after RRT discontinuation was associated with
augmentation of urine output and natriuresis but there was no difference in rate of AKI
resolution or RRT independence.1041

R

AF
T

5.10 Quality assurance for RRT
Practice Point 5.10.1: RRT programs should include the following quality standards to
reduce unnecessary variation, facilitate adoption of evidence-based practices, and
support opportunities for continuous quality improvement:
• Dedicated leadership, ideally with a medical (physician) and nursing dyad
partnership.
• Certified training and continuous education of all healthcare professionals
prescribing and delivering acute RRT.
• Standardized order sets for the acute prescription of RRT, including decisionsupport for dose-intensity of solute clearance, ultrafiltration for fluid removal,
and anticoagulation strategy.
• Standardized charting for RRT for both conventional and electronic health
records, inclusive of parameters of patient and machine solute clearance and
fluid balance.
• Monitoring and reporting of quality and key performance measures of RRT

D

Variations in standards of practice for how RRT is prescribed, monitored, and
delivered829 can stem from differences in operational organization (e.g., nephrology,
intensive care, or both), knowledge gaps, and suboptimal implementation of evidence-based
practices.1012, 1013, 1042 The integration of quality standards and routine indicators of
performance specific to acute RRT can reduce unnecessary variation and harmonize practice,
improve adoption of evidence-based practices, and identify opportunities for iterative
continuous quality improvement.791, 1012, 1043
Acute RRT programs can more effectively drive quality and innovation with
dedicated leadership.831-833, 1044 This construct is supported by numerous organizations and
societies.708, 788, 790, 791, 1045 Local leadership and championing of quality initiatives in acute
RRT is foundational to building a team and creating a culture of continuous quality
improvement and innovation.790, 1046
304

RRT programs are highly specialized and depend on a multidisciplinary team (e.g.,
physician, nurse, dietician, pharmacist, biomedical engineer, medical informatics) that is
specifically trained to prescribe, deliver, and monitor acute RRT.1042 Education is
fundamental to enable practice standardization and delivery of high quality RRT. A modified
Delphi process identified education and training as a high priority for acute RRT programs;
however, it was recognized that the specialized training for RRT, particularly CRRT, was
poorly described and characterized.1042 This is likely to contribute to disparities in practice
across institutions. The Saving Young Lives program identified the importance of “hands-on”
training and successfully empowered clinical teams to deliver standardized acute PD for AKI
in low-resource settings.790

AF
T

The use of standardized order sets with integrated decision-support, including
integration of current literature and guideline recommendations, can facilitate greater practice
harmonization and alignment with delivery of evidence-based RRT standards.791, 834 The
growing utilization of EHR can be leveraged to provide a platform for consistent charting
and sharing of performance indicators, including the development of local benchmarks and
detection of avoidable deviations in practice.743, 1043 The EHR can be an important tool for
identifying evidence-to-care gaps and for the implementation of continuous quality
improvement.1047
Practice Point 5.10.2: Key quality performance measures of RRT include measures of
effective treatment time (i.e., downtime), filter lifespan, prescribed-to-delivered doseintensity, and ultrafiltration.

R

There has been limited development and validation of key quality and performance
indicators for RRT for AKI despite its resource implications.1013, 1043 A modified Delphi
study developed a prioritized list of CRRT quality indicators from an international
interdisciplinary panel of experts (Table 55).1042

D

Several studies have described specific indicators of acute RRT performance, mostly
among patients receiving CRRT which focused on filter lifespan, effective treatment time,
and delivered dose-intensity.829, 831-833, 1013, 1044 Effective treatment time, defined as the
measured time receiving treatment minus any interruptions, either planned or unplanned, has
been proposed as a quality indicator of CRRT performance.834 This quality indictor is
focused on describing total CRRT treatment downtime in a 24-hour period. While
interruptions may be unavoidable, it is important to recognize that downtime can
compromise the effectiveness of therapy and result in discrepancy between the prescribed
and actual delivered therapy.815, 820, 839, 856-861
A multifaceted quality improvement initiative significantly improved effective
treatment time by focusing on minimizing avoidable downtime.1044 Another single center
quality improvement study that leveraged a local EHR showed marked improvement in
305

CRRT dose-intensity standardization and reduced week-to-week variation.832 Similarly, a
staged multifaceted quality improvement project that included the development of a
multidisciplinary team, use of a standardized CRRT protocol, implementation of quality
measures, and an educational initiative was shown to substantially improve delivered CRRT
dose-intensity, prolong filter lifespan, reduce CRRT access alarms, and realize meaningful
cost savings.833 These initiatives profile the importance of having key performance and
quality indicators that measure baseline practice and gauge iterative improvement over time
(Table 55).

5.11 RRT with extracorporeal life support (ECLS)
Practice Point 5.11.1: Strategies for integration of RRT into existing circuits in patients
receiving extracorporeal life support (ECLS) depend on clinical context, local expertise,
and available technology and resources.
ECLS (e.g., extracorporeal membrane oxygenation [ECMO], extracorporeal carbon
dioxide removal [ECCO2R], and ventricular assist devices [VAD]) is a long-established
cardiopulmonary life support therapy used to manage patients with life-threatening refractory
cardiogenic shock or acute respiratory failure.1048-1051 It is associated with a high risk for
AKI, in both children and adults.1052-1054 Up to 40% of children and 30% of adults receiving
ECLS concomitantly receive RRT.1053, 1055, 1056 CRRT is the predominant modality used
306

during ECLS in both children and adults.1057, 1058 The most common indication for RRT
during ECLS is fluid accumulation and fluid overload that is refractory to or is perceived to
be inadequately managed by medical therapy.1057, 1059, 1060
There are techniques to introduce RRT for patients also receiving ECLS.1059, 1061-1063
These generally include: 1) dedicated and independent RRT access and circuit (parallel); 2)
integration of a hemofilter directly into the ECLS circuit (in-line hemofilter); 3) integration
of a full CRRT device/circuit into the ECLS circuit (series).

AF
T

RRT can be provided through a conventional dedicated dialysis catheter in a separate
central vein that will run in parallel with the ECLS circuit.1059, 1062, 1063 This configuration has
the advantages of RRT being operated and delivered in a standard manner, not having issues
with differential access pressures when combined with the ECLS circuit and the option of
prescribing/delivering solute clearance and ultrafiltration as per standard protocols and
procedures. This technique can allow the dedicated use of standard anticoagulation (e.g.,
RCA). Moreover, there should be no concern for the ECLS circuit if the CRRT circuit
requires interruption or change. A downside is the need for separate vascular access.

D

R

Alternatively, RRT can be integrated into the ECLS circuit through a variety of
techniques. A simple and relatively inexpensive method is the direct introduction (in-line) of
the hemofilter into the ECLS circuit.1057, 1060 For the in-line configuration, the hemofilter is
typically integrated after the ECLS blood pump to provide positive pressure into the
hemofilter and before blood flow enters the membrane lung (oxygenator). (Figure 18) This
technique will require minimal expansion of the extracorporeal blood volume; however, it
can present challenges with monitoring (e.g., no access pressure alarms), external infusion
pumps are necessary to deliver replacement solutions, and it may not enable precision control
of ultrafiltration.1059, 1062-1064

307

AF
T

Figure 18 | Configuration of in-line hemofilter integration into ECLS circuit.1062 ECLS,
extracorporeal life support

R

Another option is the integration of a commercially available CRRT device into the
ECLS circuit.1059, 1062, 1063 This configuration will enable superior control of dose-intensity
and ultrafiltration compared to an in-line configuration. A drawback of this technique relates
to the challenges with CRRT access pressures.1065-1067 ECLS circuit pressures are negative
before the blood pump and positive between blood pump and membrane lung.1059 These
pressures can exceed the operational limits for CRRT devices and may contribute to frequent
pressure alarms and potential complications in both circuits. As aforementioned, to prevent
systemic embolization of air/clots, the CRRT should be integrated after the ECLS blood
pump and before the membrane lung.1059

D

Finally, it is also possible to connect the CRRT circuit using the existing inlet and
outlet Luer locks in the membrane lung (Figure 19).1062, 1063, 1067 This configuration will
circumvent the access pressure issues and enable delivery of dose-intensity and
ultrafiltration.
Combined ECMO-RRT support necessitates expertise, protocols and standardized
procedures to ensure all CRRT venous and arterial accesses are maintained in the correct
pressure ranges and do not disrupt the ECLS circuit.1059, 1065, 1066

308

AF
T

D

R

Figure 19 | Options of combining ECMO and CRRT circuits.1068 a. An in-line hemofilter
is integrated into the ECMO circuit. Replacement fluid is directly administered into the
ECMO circuit. Alternatively, dialysis fluid can be supplied in a counter-current position.
Replacement/dialysis fluid rates and ultrafiltration rates can be controlled via infusion
pumps. b. The CRRT device is connected to the patient through a separate catheter
independent of the ECMO circuit. c. The access (inlet) and the return (outlet) lines of the
CRRT device are connected before the centrifugal blood pump (low-pressure part) of the
ECMO circuit. d. Both the access and the return lines of the CRRT device are connected
after the blood pump. e. The access line of the CRRT device is connected after the blood
pump (high-pressure), while the CRRT return line is connected before the centrifugal blood
pump. f. The access line of the CRRT device is connected directly after the membrane
oxygenator, while the return line is connected directly before the oxygenator. CRRT,
continuous renal replacement therapy; ECMO, extracorporeal membrane oxygenation.
All configurations have potential serious risks, including air embolism, hemolysis,
and formation of blood clots. There is no clear evidence that any specific configuration is
superior in terms of outcomes or safety. However, if a centrifugal pump is used, it is
preferable to connect the CRRT device after the pump because of the risk of drawing air into
the ECMO circuit. The Extracorporeal Life Support Organization (ELSO) have published
guidelines on the management of AKI and fluid overload during ECLS, along with technical
309

guidance and considerations for RRT.1059 The decision on the precise configuration of RRT
and ECLS should be guided by local expertise, experience and the implementation of
standardized protocols and procedures.
Practice Point 5.11.2: Most patients receiving ECLS have concomitant hemodynamic
instability. For these patients, initial support with CRRT rather than IRRT may be
preferential.

AF
T

Survey and observational data show that most ECLS patients with AKI who need
RRT receive CRRT.1057, 1060, 1069 The rationale for CRRT is driven by most ECLS patients
having high illness acuity, hemodynamic instability, and multiorgan dysfunction.1048, 1055, 1056,
1061 Fluid accumulation and overload is the primary trigger for RRT in most patients
receiving ECLS.1057, 1059, 1060 While all modalities of RRT can be delivered during ECLS,
these critically ill patients are less likely to tolerate the more rapid shifts in metabolic profile
and faster ultrafiltration that will occur with IRRT. CRRT has the advantage of enabling
consistent and continuous fluid removal with reduced risk of fluctuations that may disrupt the
ECLS blood flow.
Practice Point 5.11.3: Fluid accumulation that is refractory to medical therapy
independent of kidney function may be a reasonable indication for RRT in patients
receiving ECLS.

D

R

Fluid accumulation and overload with and without AKI is associated with worse
clinical outcomes, including mortality and longer durations of organ support and ICU
stay.432, 1014, 1070-1073 While regimens of diuretic therapy can be used to manage fluid,
individual response in patients can be inconsistent and insufficient to meet fluid balance
targets. Medically refractory fluid accumulation is a reasonable indications for starting
RRT.1059, 1074-1076 Fluid accumulation and overload are recognized indication for RRT
initiation among critically ill children receiving ECLS.1057, 1060 In these patients, achieving a
negative fluid balance can be associated with improved oxygenation.1077
5.12. Considerations specific for children
5.12.1 Starting renal replacement therapy (RRT)
Practice Point 5.12.1.1: In critically ill children with severe AKI, RRT initiation within
2 days of intensive care unit (ICU) admission or AKI onset should be considered as it
may be associated with improved outcome.
AKI is a recognized risk factor among children for escalation in care intensity (e.g., mechanical
ventilation; RRT) and adverse outcomes, including mortality and long-term health complications (e.g.,
neurocognitive and functional impairment, hypertension, CKD).1017, 1078-1087 Randomized trials
investigating early versus standard/delayed initiation of RRT have only been performed in adults.
However, the increased risks of catheter and therapy-specific risks of RRT with early RRT initiation
310

observed in RCTs of adults are likely generalizable to children. RRT initiation translates into an
escalation in care intensity, complexity, and costs. For acutely and critically ill children with persistent
severe AKI and/or fluid overload, these burdens of starting RRT may be balanced by reductions in
durations of invasive organ support (i.e., mechanical ventilation; ECLS) or lengths of ICU or hospital
stay. Durations >48 hours after ICU admission or AKI onset to the start of RRT among critically ill
children with AKI have shown an association with MAKE90.732, 1088, 1089
In low- and middle-resource settings, the capacity for and options to provide acute RRT
may be reduced, including limited access to critical care services and/or selected acute RRT
modalities. A “PD first” approach has been successful in pediatric centers in low- and middleresource settings with locally produced PD fluids and repurposing of different catheters. 789, 1090

AF
T

The timing and indications for starting RRT among children, particularly among those
with critical illness and persistent severe AKI warrant further evaluation. 1089, 1091 Among
selected case-mix (e.g., complex congenital cardiac surgery) and circumstances (e.g., ECLS),
decision-making may be driven by rapid evolution in clinical status, acuity, perceived
complications compounded by AKI, and need for concomitant therapeutic support (e.g., ability
to provide adequate nutrition1092, 1093), justifying earlier start to RRT or initiation in the absence
of conventional indications or AKI and related complications that were evaluated in RCTs
focused on critically ill adults with AKI. Adoption and implementation of these practice points
should be further studied and be guided by process-based quality assurance.831
Practice Point 5.12.1.2: In critically ill children, isolated fluid overload can be an
indication for RRT initiation.

D

R

Fluid accumulation and overload in children with and without AKI are associated with
adverse outcomes and greater health resource use (e.g., prolongation of mechanical ventilation;
longer lengths of stay).432, 1094-1096 Children with AKI commonly accumulate fluid, and many,
though not all, have coexisting fluid overload.1097
A proposed definition of fluid overload is the presence of “a pathologic state of positive
fluid balance associated with a clinically observable event(s), which may vary by age, casemix, acuity, and phase of illness”.1098 While there may be no universal threshold of fluid
balance to define fluid overload across all acute and critically ill children or for use as a trigger
for RRT initiation1094, 1099, adverse outcomes associated with fluid overload (with or without
AKI), prolonged oligo-anuria, and the need for precise fluid management are frequent
indications for starting RRT in acute and critically ill children.1091, 1099, 1100 Based on
observational studies in children, fluid overload is considered an important indication for RRT
initiation that has been associated with clinical outcomes. There remains uncertainty about
whether RRT and the relative timing of initiation of RRT can modify the risks of adverse
outcomes associated with AKI and/or fluid overload in children.166

311

The strategy of early PD initiation to prevent fluid overload among high-risk children
undergoing cardiac surgery has shown variable results for time to achieve target fluid
balance, durations of mechanical ventilation, and ICU length of stay, risk of adverse events,
and mortality when compared to conservative management, including the use of diuretic
therapy.1101-1106
5.12.2. RRT modality
Practice Point 5.12.2.1: Acute PD is an acceptable RRT modality and may be preferable
to CRRT for children in select circumstances.

D

R

AF
T

There have been limited direct comparisons of RRT modalities for AKI in
children;1107, 1108 however, secular trends have shown greater utilization of CRRT and IRRT
over time, with reductions in use of acute PD, 1109 likely in part related to the introduction of
miniaturized devices aimed to provide RRT in acute and critically ill children (Table 56). 11101113 However, PD is an acceptable RRT modality for selected children with AKI or with
complications of fluid accumulation, in low- and middle-resource settings, and in
circumstances when extracorporeal RRT is contraindicated or not possible. In pediatric AKI,
acute PD is often favored due to the smaller blood volumes and lower tolerance for rapid
hemodynamic changes in children. Additionally, peritoneal access via a catheter does not
require anticoagulation and is vessel sparing, avoiding catheter associated thrombi commonly
seen in younger children. There has been only one small single-center RCT comparing acute
continuous PD to daily IHD among 136 critically ill children (age 1 month to 16 years) with
AKI.1108 While azotemic control and rates of complications were similar between the groups,
children allocated to acute PD had significantly higher survival at 30 days compared to
children receiving IHD (60.7% vs. 36.5%, P=0.019). Among those allocated to IHD, death
was most often associated with cardiac failure, sepsis, hypotension, and disequilibrium
syndrome. The remaining literature on acute PD in children has focused specifically on the
population undergoing cardiac surgery and most often compares either PD catheter
placement alone with or without acute PD versus conservative (non-RRT) measures, such as
the use of furosemide, to manage perioperative fluid balance.1101-1105, 1114

R

5.12.3. RRT dose-intensity
Practice Point 5.12.3.1: An initial CRRT effluent volume for children can be 25–30
ml/kg/h. Thereafter, dose-intensity can be adjusted based on the adequacy of correction
of electrolyte, acid-base, and metabolic derangements.

D

There are no RCTs to support a target RRT dose-intensity for acutely ill or critically
ill children. Due to limited comparisons of outcomes according to delivered RRT doseintensity in children, this practice has generally been extrapolated from findings of RCTs
performed in adult populations.815, 820
Practice Point 5.12.3.2: The dose-intensity prescription in children receiving CRRT
should be weight-based (ml/kg/h), according to either body weight at hospital or ICU
admission or ideal body weight and not prescribed based on body surface area.
The optimal initial RRT dose-intensity in children is unknown. Survey data have
shown variation in practice, with both weight-based and body surface area (BSA)-based
methods commonly used.938, 1091 Importantly, the relationship between body weight and BSA
in children is nonlinear, presenting challenges in translating and aligning prescribed doseintensity between these methods, driven by large differences in age, body weight, and body
morphology across the spectrum of children encountered. For example, in neonates and
infants, an initial CRRT dose-intensity based on BSA of 2 l/h per 1.73 m2, common in survey
313

data, could translate to an unintended much higher weight-based CRRT dose-intensity of 70–
100 ml/kg/h.1091 This reinforces the necessity for routine and frequent monitoring of CRRT
(Practice Point 5.3.2 and Practice Point 5.12.3.1). Moreover, body weight at the time CRRT
is initiated may be confounded and unreliable due to acute illness and fluid accumulation,
implying body weight at hospital or ICU admission, or ideal body weight be preferentially
used.
Practice Point 5.12.3.3: In children with AKI, PD can be initiated with a fill volume of
10–20 ml/kg and short cycle times (60–90 minutes), with gradual increases in volume
and dwell time as tolerated, with continuous therapy over 24 hours for the first 1–3
days to achieve solute clearance and fluid balance goals.

AF
T

The issue of prescribing and achieving adequate clearance using acute PD in children
with AKI was specifically addressed in the 2020 updated ISPD Guideline for PD in AKI
(Pediatric).776 This practice point aligns with this guideline. Age is an important determinant
of RRT modality selection in critically ill children, with many children under age 6 years
receiving acute PD.776 Acute PD can generally provide sufficient metabolic, azotemic and
volume control for most children, including those who are hypercatabolic.1115 Initial fill
volumes of 10–20 ml/kg are recommended to reduce the risk of dialysate leakage.1116
Thereafter, fill volume can be slowly increased to optimize solute clearance and
ultrafiltration. While initial dwell and drain times should be every 60–90 minutes, in extreme
and very-low birth weight infants, lower fill volumes (7–14 ml) and shorter dwell times (10–
20 minutes) may be necessary.1117

D

R

Practice Point 5.12.3.4: Augmentation of initial RRT dose-intensity for CRRT is
warranted when treating children with hyperammonemia. CRRT dose-intensity up to
200 ml/kg/h may be acceptable to achieve adequate clearance of ammonia. Children
should be appropriately monitored for unintended solute losses during periods of
augmented CRRT dose-intensity.
Ammonia can be generated in excess due to inborn errors of metabolism, poisoning,
sepsis, or acute liver failure. Ammonia is a neurotoxin and accumulation can precipitate
metabolic encephalopathy, cerebral edema, or death. 1118, 1119 Serum ammonia concentrations
>100 µmol/l reliably predict severe encephalopathy among patients with acute liver
failure.1120 As such, hyperammonemia is a recognized indication for RRT.754, 1091, 1121-1128
IRRT can efficiently clear ammonia and is dependent on blood flow rate, dialysate flow rate,
and hemofilter surface characteristics; however, use of short-term IRRT sessions may result
in rebound elevation in serum ammonia.1127, 1129, 1130 Extracorporeal clearance of ammonia
using initial high dose-intensity CRRT (total effluent flow >75–90 ml/kg/h) has shown
capacity to achieve 50% reduction in serum ammonia concentrations within 4–9 hours.1119,
1127, 1131, 1132
A biphasic CRRT dose-intensity strategy may mitigate the risk of cerebral
1118
edema.
CRRT (CVVHDF) has been recommended for children with serum ammonia
314

>400–500 µmol/l and not responsive after 4 hours of aggressive medical therapy (e.g.,
protein restriction; ammonia chelation).1127, 1133
5.12.4. Catheters for RRT
Practice Point 5.12.4.1: In small children, including neonates with small vessel size, use
of a pliable cuffed catheter, as compared to a rigid temporary catheter, may be less
thrombogenic and preferred, even when the expected dialysis duration is uncertain.
The cuff can be left outside the body when the projected duration of use is short.

AF
T

In neonates and small children requiring RRT for AKI, vascular access poses
significant challenges due to the small caliber of blood vessels, limited available access sites,
and a heightened risk of access-related complications. In this context, the use of a soft,
pliable, cuffed catheter, rather than a rigid, temporary catheter, may provide superior safety
and performance, even when the anticipated duration of RRT is uncertain. Rigid nontunneled
catheters, which are commonly used in adults, may be less appropriate for small children
because of an increased risk of mechanical trauma to fragile vessels, higher thrombogenicity
in low-flow states, and limited flexibility that can lead to malposition or kinking.913, 1134

R

In contrast, pliable cuffed catheters made of softer materials such as silicone or
polyurethane are associated with reduced endothelial injury and a lower risk of thrombosis,
improved catheter patency in small vessels, and greater flexibility and comfort, all of which
help to minimize mechanical complications.1134 Although these catheters are typically
designed for long-term use, they may still offer advantages in short-term settings for neonates
and infants, especially in cases where vessel preservation is a priority. When short-term use
is anticipated, the catheter cuff can be left external to the skin to avoid subcutaneous
tunneling and to facilitate easier removal.1135 Additionally, cuffed catheters offer improved
securement and stability, which is especially important in infants and young children who are
more prone to accidental dislodgement.

D

5.12.5. Anticoagulation for RRT
Practice Point 5.12.5.1: In children requiring small catheters and circuit volume and
lower blood flow rates, anticoagulation is often necessary and preferred over no
anticoagulation during CRRT.
In critically ill children receiving CRRT, the use of anticoagulation is often necessary
due to the unique technical challenges posed by smaller catheter sizes, reduced
extracorporeal circuit volumes, and lower blood flow rates. These factors contribute to an
increased risk of circuit clotting and premature filter failure in the absence of
anticoagulation.1136, 1137 Data from a multicenter European observational cohort study
including 247 children undergoing CRRT with 1357 circuits totaling 37,562 treatment hours
(EurAKID registry) demonstrated that shorter circuit lifespan in children is influenced by

315

reduced circuit size and flow dynamics, both of which are more prone to clotting without
adequate anticoagulant support.1137
Brophy et al. conducted a multicenter evaluation highlighting how anticoagulation,
particularly RCA or UFH, used during CRRT significantly improved filter lifespan and
reduced clotting events compared to circuits managed without anticoagulation. 1136 Further, in
a study of 161 children receiving CRRT (total 22,190 hours; median 74.8 hours per patient),
filter lifespan was significantly shorter for CRRT without anticoagulation, especially in
younger children and those with lower blood flow settings.1138

AF
T

5.12.6. Hemofilters for RRT
Practice Point 5.12.6.1: Appropriately sized membranes proportional to body surface
area are preferred for children with AKI receiving CRRT or IRRT.
In children with AKI receiving CRRT or IRRT, selecting a filter membrane with a
surface area proportionate to the child’s body surface area is essential to ensure both
treatment efficacy and safety. Children, particularly neonates and infants, have lower
circulating blood volumes and limited tolerance for extracorporeal circuits. Oversized
membranes can significantly increase extracorporeal volume, raising the risk of
hemodynamic instability, hypothermia, and the need for blood priming.1139

R

Coulthard and Sharp examined the feasibility and challenges of hemodialysis in very
small infants, including those weighing as little as 2.5 kg. They described the use of
extracorporeal circuits with a volume of approximately 25 ml.1139 Given that the estimated
total blood volume in infants is around 80 ml/kg, a 2.5 kg infant would have a total blood
volume of about 200 ml. Thus, the extracorporeal volume represents approximately 12.5% of
the infant’s total blood volume. This relatively high proportion poses significant risk for
hemodynamic instability and necessitates the use of blood priming.1139

D

Using an oversized membrane can lead to an increase in extracorporeal circuit
volume, which may exceed 8–10 ml/kg and increase the risk of hemodynamic instability,
particularly in neonates and small children.1140 Additionally, large membranes, even with
blood priming, can cause dilutional coagulopathy, with reductions in platelet and fibrinogen
levels due to large extracorporeal volumes. Larger membranes are also associated with
higher RRT-related macro- and micronutrient losses.1141, 1142
Conversely, undersized membranes may compromise RRT adequacy. Therefore,
proportional membrane sizing is a critical component of safe and effective CRRT delivery in
pediatric patients.1091 In the EurAKID registry, longer hemofilter lifespan was associated
with use of RCA (42 h for RCA vs. 24 h for UFH versus 18 h for no anticoagulant, P=0.001),
larger membrane surface area, and right internal jugular access (compared with left internal
jugular).1137 Notably, smaller dialyzers (membrane surface area <0.5 m2) had shorter
316

lifespans (19 h; IQR: 14–32) than both medium (membrane surface area 0.5–1.0 m2–26 h;
IQR: 16–43, P=0.04) and large dialyzers (membrane surface area >1.0 m2–33 h; IQR: 20–48,
P<0.001).1137

AF
T

Pediatric-specific CRRT machines and miniaturized circuits are available and have
received regulatory approval. In the absence of direct comparative pediatric studies, selection
of CRRT platforms should prioritize overall biocompatibility, low extracorporeal circuit
volume, and suitability for patient size. When available, hemofilters with validated safety
data in small children are preferred.1107, 1110, 1112, 1143 If higher solute clearance is required,
membrane surface area may be increased as clinically indicated. Importantly, the advantages
of pediatric-specific CRRT platforms extend beyond filter size alone. Appropriately scaled
machines are designed to allow precise and reliable fluid balance control, which is
particularly critical in neonates and small children, where even minimal fluid deviations may
be clinically significant. In addition, these systems support lower blood flow rates, which
may reduce shear stress, limit blood–surface interaction and potentially decrease the risk of
hemolysis and inflammation. Together, these features may enhance the safety, feasibility,
and tolerability of CRRT in small pediatric patients and should be considered when selecting
CRRT technology for this population.
5.12.7. Ultrafiltration in RRT
Practice Point 5.12.7.1: In children, net ultrafiltration (UFNET) should not exceed 2.5
ml/kg/h. Frequent assessment of fluid balance (i.e., every 4-6 hours) should be
undertaken to evaluate tolerance for fluid removal and adjust fluid balance goals, as
necessary.

D

R

Fluid overload is a frequent indication for CRRT initiation in critically ill children
and associated with adverse outcomes.996, 1009, 1071, 1075, 1076 Limited data in critically ill
children with AKI, particularly among those with fluid overload >20% of body weight,
suggest early net negative fluid balance after CRRT initiation may modify outcome. 996
Similar to adults, there is concern that high UFNET in children may contribute to dialysisinduced kidney injury and be associated with adverse outcomes.1008 Thadani et al. evaluated
the association of net fluid balance after CRRT initiation in 371 critically ill children, mostly
with AKI.1008 Those who achieved a net negative fluid balance had more CRRT-free days
and lower risk of MAKE30. Moreover, children maintaining urine output ≥0.3 ml/kg/h over
the study period were found to have 5.6 more CRRT-free days and a decreased rate of
MAKE. Preservation of urine output has been shown as a potentially important indicator of
UFNET intensity and associated with CRRT liberation.1144
An upper UFNET threshold <2.5 ml/kg/h has been suggested to minimize the risk of
adverse events attributable to ultrafiltration and facilitate safe fluid removal among critically
ill children. Frequent assessment and adjustment of UFNET and fluid balance targets among
critically ill children are essential to mitigate risk of harm.
317

5.12.8. Discontinuation of RRT
Practice Point 5.12.8.1: Urine output exceeding >0.5 ml/kg/h over 6 hours can indicate
resolution of AKI and be used to predict successful RRT discontinuation in children.

AF
T

Critically ill children receiving CRRT are characterized by a high burden of
multiorgan dysfunction, beyond AKI and fluid overload.758 Surveillance of practice has
suggested the most important factors used by healthcare professionals when deciding to
discontinue CRRT among critically ill children are urine output and resolution of fluid
overload.891 Relatively few studies have focused on factors predictive of successful RRT
discontinuation. An analysis of the multinational WE-ROCK study evaluated predictors of
successful CRRT discontinuation, defined as no reinitiation of RRT for ≥72 hours in 622
critically ill children.1144 In this large cohort, only 54% of children successfully discontinued
CRRT. Several factors were associated with successful CRRT discontinuation, including
variables prior to CRRT initiation (higher urine output), variables at the time of CRRT
initiation (higher Vasoactive-Inotropic Score; higher Pediatric Logistic Organ Dysfunction-2
score) and a shorter total CRRT duration.

D

R

In addition, a single-center retrospective study reinforced the prognostic value of
urine output when predicting successful CRRT discontinuation in children. In a cohort of 99
critically ill children who underwent a trial off CRRT, urine output ≥0.5 ml/kg/h in the 6
hours prior to stopping CRRT predicted successful discontinuation (AUC: 0.72, 95% CI:
0.60–0.84, P=0.0009), defined as no reinitiation of CRRT for ≥7 days.1145 Notably, this
association remained significant after adjustment for diuretic exposure. Preserved urine
output (>0.3 ml/kg/h) and a net negative fluid balance during CRRT were associated with
achieving more RRT-free days and lower MAKE30 (adjusted OR: 0.16; 95% CI: 0.09–0.29)
in a retrospective cohort of 371 critically ill children. 1008 Children who did not successfully
discontinue RRT had longer ICU stays and higher in-hospital mortality.1008

318

CHAPTER 6. FOLLOW-UP CARE AFTER AKI OR AKD
Robust epidemiological data have revealed that people with CKD are at increased risk
of AKI, and those who survive AKI and/or AKD face heightened long-term risks of CKD,
cardiovascular events, recurrent AKI, and premature mortality, confirming a bidirectional
relationship between AKI, AKD and CKD. Emerging evidence suggests that systematic
approaches to post-AKI care, including nephrology follow-up, medication optimization, and
structured surveillance protocols, may mitigate some of these adverse outcomes. This chapter
synthesizes recent advances in management post-AKI/AKD and follow-up care to improve
long-term outcomes.

AF
T

6.1 Planning follow-up care with people after AKI and/or AKD
Recommendation 6.1.1: We recommend coordinated follow-up for children and adults
after AKI and/or AKD, with personalized care according to individual clinical context
and vulnerability risks (1B).

D

R

This recommendation places a high value on the potential benefits of individualized risk
assessment to deliver personalized care for children and adults following an episode of
illness affected by AKI and/or AKD. There is consistent evidence that AKD and all stages of
AKI are associated with adverse long-term outcomes. The recommendation recognizes that
people affected by AKI and/or AKD are heterogenous with varying needs and priorities. As a
clinical syndrome, AKI offers an opportunity to improve systems of care and outcomes for
people living with complex health needs. To achieve this, the recommendation encourages
healthcare professionals, patients (and carers), researchers, and policy makers to consider
implementing strategies that maximize the clinical utility of AKI as a marker of systemic
vulnerability and adverse outcomes, while minimizing the potential for treatment and
workload burden. There is insufficient evidence favoring one provider type or model of care
over any other; consequently, individual patient, healthcare professional, and local system
factors, including availability and expertise, are likely the best drivers of these decisions.
Key information
Balance of benefits and harms.
There is extensive evidence that AKI/AKD is associated with poor outcomes. 59, 61, 1017,
1146-1154 Relevant health outcomes for children and adults with AKI and/or AKD go beyond
the initial episode of acute illness with a need to plan timely and tailored follow-up care to
reduce the risks, in the short, medium, and long-term, of adverse kidney and non-kidney
outcomes (Table 57). Reducing the risk of all-cause hospital readmission and mortality are
immediate outcomes for consideration.1155, 1156 Longer term adverse outcomes include de
novo CKD, CKD progression, kidney failure, and further episodes of AKI,1150, 1157 as well as
development of hypertension and cardiovascular disease (CVD). Physical, psychological, and
social support are also central to improving functional status and QoL.1158-1161 In addition, a
systematic review that compared the out-of-pocket payments for various conditions identified
319

D

R

AF
T

kidney disease, including AKI. as the leading cause of catastrophic health expenditure across
lower-resource settings.742

320

D
R

AF

AKD, acute kidney disease; AKI, acute kidney injury; CKD, chronic kidney disease; CVD, cardiovascular disease; ICU, intensive care
